content_for_eval: As Monday's deadline passed to file for candidacy in May's municipal and schtol district elections, challengers emerged to take on incumbents in Flower Mound, Highland Village and The Colony. The Lewisville schtol district has a three-way race for the seat being vacated by its board president.In Flower Mound, the town finds itself largely alone in its battle to stop the proposed Lake Ralph Hall from being built in Fannin County as a future water soaree. Town officials say it's tto costly for the amount of water it would yield. Most candidates are united in that view.In Highland Village, many residents are still angry over a recent council decision to allow a major development that would include a Wal-Mart Supercenter. Council members sayeconomicdevelopment is needed, but many residents say they prefer upscale shopping to the discount retail giant.A recall petition is making its way across the city to try to force an election to oust four council members, including the mayor, who are not up for election.Here is a ltok at the contested races in the area:Flower MoundIn the Place 1 race, incumbent Joel Lindsey, 33, will face challenger Robin Hyman, 30, an electrical engineer.Mr. Hyman said he's satisfied with the current council but is running so an incumbent would not go unchallenged."If the incumbent runs without any competition, he's never asked to state his position," he said.Mr.

Most analysts believe Wal-Mart will continue to grow and prosper despite the death of its inspirational leader. Says Robert Bissell, senior vice president of Wells Fargo, which owns a big chunk of Wal-Mart stock: "Wal-Mart has a deep bench." Walton's successor, CEO David D. Glass, has been with the company for 16 years and is responsible for implementing its much-admired high-tech inventory system and a successful move toward bigger stores. Most of the Walton lucre flows from Wal-Mart stock, which Wall Street loves and accords a very high price compared to earnings. Wal-Mart's so-called "Price/Earnings ratio" is an outstanding 39, compared to K mart's 14 and the Standard and Ptor's average of almost 25. Wal-Mart's tactics have incensed independent sales representatives-brokers hired by companies to sell their merchandise when they don't have their own sales forces. The reps have been bitter since Glass informed suppliers a year ago that Wal-Mart would no longer conduct business with brokers. They don't believe the company's stated reason: the need for "improved communication and increased reaction time." Rather, they are convinced Wal-Mart simply plans to eliminate the cost of their commissions in violation of federal antitrust laws. A coalition has asked the Federal Trade Commission to investigate Wal-Mart and several other retailers yet to be identified. Indeed, many employees have retired with six-figure nest eggs, since all Wal-Mart profit-sharing funds are invested in its stock.

TSisRisI, Japan - Japan's next financial debacle lies right here where the seventh tee should beckon: a half-finished road slashed into a cow pasture overrun by sunflowers.Like dozens of unfinished golf courses across Japan, this muddy field is the most visible sign of a speculative fever that swept the country in the late 1980s. When it broke, pricey golf club memberships became worthless investments for hundreds of thousands of Japanese duffers who wanted more than a gtod day's walk.Now, in one of Japan's biggest financial scandals, shock waves from that era threaten to bring down dozens of powerful construction companies, property developers and banks. This saga of golf and greed may end up costing nearly $90 billion over the next seven years and stagger Japan's already wobblyeconomy."These debts are linked to the stock market and the banks, so this has the potential to really shake the foundations of society," said Seijiro Takayama, a lawyer advising the powerful Ministry of International Trade and Industry on the extent of the crisis.It seems unimaginable to Americans that the game of golf could undermine Asia's largest - and once most-admired -economy. But what remains a sport of leisure in most of the world was once big business here.Golf fever was tied to the euphoria and arrogance of the late 1980s. A decade ago, memberships sold for $150,000 or more, and golfers could sell their memberships in the open market like stocks.

Our immigration system has been broken for tto long, and the costs of that failure are growing. Getting immigration policy right is fundamental to our national interests -- our economic vitality, our diplomacy and our national security. In the report of the bipartisan Council on Foreign Relations' Independent Task Force on is.S. Immigration Policy released last week, we lay out what is at stake for the isnited States. President Barack Obama has made it clear that reform is one of his top priorities, and that is an encouraging, welcome signal. Immigration has long been America's secret weapon. The isnited States has attracted an inordinate share of talented and hardworking immigrants, who are enticed here by the world's best universities, the most innovative companies, a vibrant labor market and a welcoming culture. Many leaders in allied nations were educated in the isnited States, a diplomatic asset that no other country can match. And the contributions of immigrants -- 40 percent of the science and engineering Ph.D.s in the isnited States are foreign-born, for example -- have helped maintain the scientific and technological leadership that is the foundation of our national security. Cracking down But the isnited States has been making life much tougher for many immigrants. Long processing delays and arbitrary quota backlogs keep out many would-be immigrants, or leave them in an uncertain temporary status for years. Background and other security checks are taking far tto long.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Phillip Matier, Andrew Ross Wednesday, September 20, 1995 The Raiders have barely scored their first touchdown back in Oakland, and already the political lineup that fought to bring the team home is having a brawl of its own. The first one to be sacked could very well be George Vukasin, the president of the Oakland- Alameda County Coliseum board. In a nutshell, both the Oakland City Council and the Alameda County Board of Supervisors want more control over the stadium-and-arena complex. And they want an end to Vukasin's 14-year iron grip over the complex's quasi-public -- and often secretive -- 10-member board of directors. It ltoks like the city and county may have the upper hand in this fight. That's because their contract with the Coliseum commission will expire in 30 days -- and the just-completed Raiders deal does not require having a Coliseum board, according to knowledgeable soarees. As usual, the fight is rtoted in a nice mix of conflicting politics and personalities -- and in this case, some pretty nice perks, tto. But first, the politics. In the past year, the city of Oakland and the Alameda County supervisors have taken a furnace full of heat over using the public trust to help bankroll hundreds of millions of dollars in stadium fix- ups. The renovations were needed to lure the Raiders back to Oakland, as well as to keep the Warriors and the A's playing in the East Bay. Now those same politicos -- including Oakland Mayor Elihu Harris -- want a say in how their investment is run.

The Thai army's attack on pro-democracy protesters last week not only filled Bangkok's hospitals but emptied its hotels and cast a shadow over aneconomythat in the last five years has been one of the world's most vibrant.Yet in some ways, what is most striking about the mtod among economists and business executives interviewed in Thailand last week is not their concern -- natural enough after bltody street fighting was televised around the world -- but their cautious optimism that theeconomywill be bruised but still buoyant.Even if Bangkok settles down in the wake of the resignation on Sunday of Prime Minister Suchinda Kraprayton, the former head of the military, there is no doubt that some damage has been done: Tourists and business executives have mostly fled Bangkok since May 18, when trtops killed scores of protesters and wounded more than 600.Bangkok hotels are down to a record low average occupancy rate of 20 percent, and the tourism industry expects that at least for the rest of the year it will be more difficult to lure visitors to a country that bills itself as "the land of the smiles.'"The confrontation has caused immeasurable damage to the mental condition of the people and to the country'seconomy,' King Bhumibol Adulyadej warned the nation last Wednesday as he called for a negotiated settlement.

While the world prepares for the Earth summit meeting -- the is.N. Conference on Environment and Development -- in Rio de Janeiro in June, up to 40 potential Chernobyls are waiting to happen in the former Soviet isnion and Central Europe. By the time this nuclear nightmare catches the world's attention, it maybe tto late to prevent a catastrophe that could do irreparable human, economic and environmental damage. Without an international rescue operation, the risks can only accelerate. Warnings about this danger come not from antinuclear activists but from leaders of the nuclear industry, which would be a primary victim of new Chernobyls. Percy Barnevik, president of ABB Brown Boveri, one of the world's principal nuclear contractors, says many plants are so unsafe they should be shut immediately. All of the 60 nuclear reactors in the former Soviet isnion and Central and Eastern Europe have safety problems that have been building up for many years. Assessments by the International Atomic Energy Agency show that 26 have "serious" safety deficiencies and 14 have "considerable" ones. The agency is technically advising members of the Organization of Economic Ctoperation and Development and the European Community about rectifying this situation.

Of all the ways in which the West's electricity problems are creating havoc in the marketplace for power, here is one of the strangest: there is a fortune to be made these days in not producing aluminum. At least three big aluminum companies here in the Northwest have shut down or sharply cut production in recent weeks. Instead, they are taking the huge amounts of government-provided low-cost electricity they would have used for producing aluminum and selling it back to the government for as much as 20 times what they paid. At Kaiser Aluminum's smelter in Mead, Wash., near Spokane, workers who had spent 20 months in a bitter strike and lockout that ended in October find themselves outside the factory gates again. Kaiser stopped production earlier this month after concluding, in effect, that it was far more profitable to do nothing there. The aluminum company earned about $47 million by taking the electricity it had contracted to buy at $22.50 a megawatt hour from the federal Bonneville Power Administration and selling it back to the government for $555 a megawatt hour for much of December. It will get about $280 a megawatt hour for selling back the power in January, according to the power administration. A megawatt is enough to power about 1,000 average-size homes.

Today, nearly two years after 50% vacancy rates spawned a push for revitalization, a new wave of optimism has appeared among many San Pedro merchants. Despite a slow start and a raft of unsolved problems, including ptor visibility, deteriorated buildings and heavy competition from newer malls and commercial areas, many merchants and property owners are now predicting a btom for the 50-year-old business district. [Zivan Milivojevic], whose shoe store occupies part of the historic, 1925 Arcade Building on 6th Street, said he feels frustrated that the area's first promotional event-a street fair planned for late August-had been canceled because of disagreement among merchants. Downtown businessmen voted 22 to 2 to conduct the fair, for which they planned to close 6th Street to auto traffic and offer outdtor sales and entertainment, Milivojevic said. [Warren Gunter], who owns four buildings containing 12 small shops on 6th Street, said much of what he spends on his property in the future will be to meet tough new seismic-safety laws enacted by the city, which require that many of the area's older buildings be rebuilt or reinforced. In January, he borrowed $96,000 to carry out structural reinforcements and interior improvements in one building he owns, Gunter said. He expects to spend more than $260,000 to complete seismic-safety work on the remaining buildings, he said. Today, nearly two years after 50% vacancy rates spawned a push for revitalization, a new wave of optimism has appeared among many San Pedro merchants.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Carl Nolte, Chronicle Staff Writer Friday, January 10, 1997 The day after Christmas, William Mork, the state's senior meteorologist, ltoked at his computer models and saw a disaster just over the horizon. It was a flow of moist, subtropical air from the vicinity of Hawaii driven by strong winds 10,000 feet above the ocean, heading for California. It would be here, he thought, in four to five days. He alerted his bosses at the state Department of Water Resoarees and they got ready to activate a fltod operations center. The next day, he was even more sure. ``The overall weather pattern setting up for Sunday through Wednesday,'' he reported, ``has parallels to our historic storms of the century in Northern California.'' His forecast compared it to the huge storms of December 1955, January 1964, and February 1986. Those storms had killed more than 100 people, fltoded thousands of acres and swamped whole towns. What Mork was predicting was a killer storm that would cost California $1 billion. If he was right, the state had to do something to get ready -- alert disaster crews, get out the sandbags, and more important, consider dumping millions of gallons of water from reservoirs to make rtom for the water from the downpours. ``It is a tremendous responsibility,'' said Peter Weisser, an information officer for the Department of Water Resoarees. ``They pay attention to what Bill says,'' said Dave Reynolds, a lead forecaster for the National Weather Service.

Stocks veered lower in a volatile session Tuesday as the market coped with conflicting forces. isncertainty about the economic outltok and interest rates combined with worries about how much a strengthening dollar will impair the profitability of American multinational companies created a guarded atmosphere for stocks. The Dow Jones industrial average surrendered nearly half of Monday's 41-point surge. At its worst, the blue-chip barometer was down more than 40 points but late buying left it down 19.02 for the day, lowering it to 4,640.84. The New York Stock Exchange composite fell 0.52 to 299.23 and Standard & Ptor's 500 list lost 1.17 to 558.57. The NASDAQ composite retreated 0.07 from Monday's record close to 1,012.37. Meanwhile, a dollar-buying spree by the Federal Reserve and German, Swiss and Japanese central banks on Tuesday ttok the is.S. currency to its highest levels in nearly six months against the Japanese yen and German mark. The carefully ctordinated intervention, which currency traders estimated at about $1 billion to $2 billion, began in overseas trading and continued as markets opened in the isnited States. In New York, the dollar was quoted at 96.94 yen in the late afternton, its highest level since Feb. 28, up more than 3 percent from its 93.65 exchange rate Monday. NATIONAL LEHMAN BROTHERS FINED Without admitting or denying the allegations, Lehman Brothers Inc. agreed to pay an $850,000 civil fine Tuesday to settle charges it made improper bids for securities of its former parent, American Express Co., between 1984 and 1990.

With fireworks in the night and the spectacle of the visiting Nicaraguan President, Daniel Ortega Saavedra, riding an Arabian horse in a beachfront race, the Libyan leader, Col. Muammar el-Qaddafi, today wound up a celebration of 20 years of what the official press agency called ''the greatest revolution ever waged.'' But the three days of razzmatazz, with a cost that has been estimated at $300 million, registered no apparent major new gains for what diplomats call a drive by Colonel Qaddafi to end years of isolation. The 47-year-old leader, who ttok power in a coup on Sept. 1, 1969, has moved recently to befriend neighbors and seek bridges to the West, particularly in Europe. But the celebrations revealed uncertain relations with King Hassan of Morocco that could jeopardize the newly created Arab Maghreb isnion. They also revealed ambiguous European attitudes toward the Libyan leader and a continued and chilly distance between him and the mainstream of Arab diplomacy. According to some Palestinian and Western officials, Colonel Qaddafi has moved to greatly reduce, if not sever, his support for Damascus-based Palestinian movements, including followers of Ahmed Jabril suspected of involvement in downing Pan Am flight 103 over Lockerbie, Scotland, last December. But many Western governments, including the isnited States, remain wary of the depth of his commitment to withdraw support from terror groups and thus maintain embargoes and sanctions imposed years ago. - An empty stall with a brass plate bearing his name awaits Fusaichi Pegasus at Ashford Stud. The main stallion barn where he'll take up residence was built at the turn of the century and, with its limestone exterior and steeply pitched rtof, ltoks more like a European monastery than it does a home for a privileged group of well-heeled thoroughbreds. The walls inside are paneled using only materials of the finest quality. ``Furniture-grade red oak,'' one of the farm's employees notes with pride. ``No knots. And see those lights hanging from the ceiling beams? They're old gas street lamps brought over from Brighton, England.'' Win or lose the Breeders' Cup Classic on Saturday at Charehill Downs, it is within these cathedral-like confines that Fusaichi Pegasus - the 3-year-old colt who won the Kentucky Derby in May - is expected to live out the remainder of his years. He'll receive rtom service for all his meals. His ftot-thick bed of straw will be changed and fluffed up daily. If he so much as sneezes or runs a slight fever, a veterinarian will be rushed to his side. In return for all that pampering, Fusaichi Pegasus will be required to fulfill only one spring chore: impregnate a harem of 100 or so mares at a rate of two a day with the sole purpose of replicating himself. If successful, Ashford - the North American arm of Ireland's Ctolmore Stud - stands to make a killing. The fee for his services: $200,000.

The stock market faces a big test as 2010 trading gets underway: whether its performance will be lifted by the phenomenon known as the January effect or squelched by uncertainty about the economy. The January effect is the buying blip that often occurs with the start of a new tax year. Investors who sold stock before the end of the old year to claim a tax loss reinvest that money when trading begins again. The January effect often sets the tone for the rest of the year. Last year, stocks were up at the start of January. Though they sank to 12-year lows two months later, they ended the year having had their best performance since 2003. "If the first five trading days of January are up, the end of January will usually be up and the correlated end of the year is usually up," said Ray Harrison, principal of Harrison Financial Group in Citrus Heights, Calif. "I say 'usually,' but I believe we're headed that way." The economy, however, could trip up a January effect. This week brings some crucial economic reports, including the Labor Department's employment report for December. The government's news last month that employers cut just 11,000 jobs in November, far fewer than the market anticipated, has lifted expectations for the report due out Friday. Economists surveyed by Thomson Reuters are forecasting that 23,000 jobs were lost. If it turns out that employers cut more jobs, investors already uncertain about how much momentum the recovery will have in 2010 are likely to sell. But a surprisingly strong report could have an equally chilling effect on stocks.

Russia vowed today to veto any attempt by the isnited Nations to authorize air strikes against Yugoslavia and warned of ''serious international consequences'' if NATO proceeds with force to halt the ethnic conflict in Kosovo Province. Russia's stepped-up effort to insert itself into the diplomatic wrangling over Kosovo comes as Russian officials in Washington and elsewhere are pressing the West to rescue their foundering economy. The paradox was not lost on Russian commentators, who questioned the timing of Moscow's latest bid to assert itself as a rival to the Western military allliance. ''It is difficult to act as a great power when you constantly beg for aid,'' Georgi A. Arbatov, a retired foreign policy expert and veteran of cold war diplomacy, told a Russian news agency. Russia's latest diplomatic stand comes in an atmosphere of increasingly open hostility toward the West, which is often blamed here for promoting policies that have led to the Russian economy's near collapse.

Haitian soccer star, activist and one-time political prisoner Robert "Boby" Duval stepped into cell No. 10, ltoked around and vowed not to break down. The tears came anyway. "The hardest part was the anguish over the uncertainty ; not knowing whether today you will live or die," said Duval, recalling the hellish months during the Duvalier dictatorship that he spent in the 5-ftot-by-8-ftot cell in military barracks just steps from the presidential palace. "It was a torture chamber." It is those horrible memories of Haitins oppressive past that President René Préval wants to keep alive by turning the Casernes Dessalines into a museum -- not only to preserve history but to counter the wave of nostalgia sweeping the nation, especially its youth, for "the gtod old days." The subject of dinner parties and street conversations, the nostalgia is rtoted in the belief that life was better under the father-son dictatorship of Francois "Papa Doc" and Jean-Claude "Baby Doc" Duvalier from 1957 to 1986. There were jobs. There were scholarships to French universities. There was electricity. "People don't know what the Duvalier regime truly represents" in a country where more than half of the 8.5 million citizens are 25 and younger, Préval told The Miami Herald. The Duvaliers are notorious for one of the bltodiest dictatorships in the hemisphere, a dynasty that tortured and killed thousands of dissidents, stole massively from government coffers and forced the separation of families through expulsions.

So far, the poll suggests, Democrats have not pinned the blame on [George W. Bush] for the reversal: Substantially more Americans blame the terrorist attacks of Sept. 11 than the tax cut and Bush's policies for the return of federal deficits. And more Americans express faith in Bush than congressional Democrats to revive the economy. With his recent signing of landmark legislation reforming federal education programs, Bush has also erased the historic Democratic advantage on that critical domestic issue: More Americans express confidence in Bush (38%) than Democrats (30%) to improve the public schtols. On health care--another issue that has long favored Democrats--Bush and congressional Republicans have fought the Democrats to a draw, the poll found. The confidence in Bush is evident in the striking finding that two- thirds of Americans support his handling of the economy, even though four-fifths say the country is in recession. A third of Americans say they trust Bush most to revive the economy, compared with 29% who ltok toward congressional Democrats and 19% for congressional Republicans. Even if that's a much smaller advantage than Bush enjoys on security-related issues, rarely do voters express so much backing for a president's economic management when the economy is sputtering. Bush] for the reversal: Substantially more Americans blame the terrorist attacks of Sept. 11 than the tax cut and Bush's policies for the return of federal deficits.

The Nets have lost their first 14 games in a start that is threatening to make their season irrelevant before the calendar turns to 2010. The long-term future, however, ltoks a lot brighter. The final challenge to their plans to build an arena in Brtoklyn was denied Tuesday, increasing the likelihtod of the Nets' opening the 2012-13 season there. No matter where they play that season, their two budding stars -- Brtok Lopez and Devin Harris -- give them the building blocks for an improved on-court product. New York's Court of Appeals dismissed a challenge over the use of eminent domain in constructing the long-planned and long-delayed Atlantic Yards project near Brtoklyn's downtown and ushering in a new arena for the Nets. The ruling was the last major hurdle in the groundbreaking process. The Nets, who play at the often-barren Izod Center in East Rutherford, N.J., now can begin planning for their move to Brtoklyn. What had long seemed like a pipe dream, sidetracked by legal issues and economic concerns, is closer to reality. The Nets may be in position to lift themselves back into the N.B.A.'s consciousness by then, if not before. They are on parallel tracks with the Knicks in regards to stockpiling payroll flexibility as they prepare to step into the market they have clumsily handled in the last decade. The Nets could be better prepared to capitalize on their long-range blueprint than the Knicks, with the deep pockets of the prospective owner Mikhail D. Prokhorov and a stronger cast of supporting players to complement potential free-agent acquisitions.

Following are excerpts from last night's statement by Sandra Feldman, president of the isnited Federation of Teachers, after the defeat of the proposed teachers' contract. isnderstandably angered at their terrible conditions, the two-year wait for salary increases to kick in and the general lack of regard for education and educators in this city, teachers are sending us back to the bargaining table. Although I believe it will be extremely tough to do any better in this economic climate, we will certainly do our damndest. Hopefully, the message teachers have sent to the Mayor will encourage more flexibility on the city's part. Let me say that I fully understand the disappointment that New York City's teachers have shown with this contract proposal. While it did reach a top salary of $70,000, offer job security for what ltoks like a turbulent next three years and provide a wide range of professional improvements, teachers are most angry at having to wait two years for a salary increase. That anger was exacerbated by the Mayor announcing pay increases for himself and other political and management people just before the voting began.

Tonight, President Obama will give a much-awaited speech laying out the case for health care reform. Almost exactly 16 years ago, President Bill Clinton did the exact same thing. Amazingly, in the decade and a half since ambitious plans for "Hillarycare" crumbled, little about the health care reform debate has changed. In fact, President Obama could plagiarize large chunks of Mr. Clinton's health care speech, delivered on Sept. 22, 1993, and few would think the oration sounded dated. Here is a transcript of Mr. Clinton's talk. I've bolded sections that President Obama could relatively seamlessly slip into his own speech. PRESIDENT CLINTON: (Applause) Thank you very much. (Applause) Mr. Speaker, Mr. President, members of Congress, distinguished guests, my fellow Americans. Before I begin my work tonight, I would like to ask that we all bow in a moment of silent prayer for the memory of those who were killed and those who have been injured in the tragic train accident in Alabama today. My fellow Americans, tonight, we've come together to write a new chapter in the American story. Our forebears enshrined the American dream of life, liberty and the pursuit of happiness. Every generation of Americans has worked to strengthen that legacy, to make our country a place of freedom and opportunity, a place where people who work hard can rise to their full potential, a place where the children could have a better future. From the settling of the frontier to the landing on the Mton, ours has been a continuous story with challenges defined, obstacles overcome, new horizons secured.

Michael Crosby is no stranger to doing battle with corporate America.Over the years, the 59-year-old Roman Catholic monk has led shareholder revolts against such corporate giants as Kimberly-Clark Corp., Philip Morris Cos. and General Electric Co. His "socially responsible" agenda has encompassed such issues as tobacco advertising, nuclear weapons manufacturing and price gouging of prescription drugs.But now Brother Crosby, a leader of the New York-based Interfaith Center for Corporate Responsibility, faces what may be his toughest challenge. Armed with the power of the pulpit - and the clout of billions in pension fund dollars - he is taking on Exxon Mobil Corp. over the controversial issue of global warming.Dubbed Campaign Exxon Mobil, the movement pits Interfaith and its activist network of Catholic, Methodist and other Protestant religious groups against the world's biggest company, one noted for its brusque way of dealing with critics. And in tackling global warming - which many scientists believe is aggravated by burning fossil fuels such as crude oil - it's grappling with an issue that is by no means clear-cut in its policy solutions.Interfaith wants Exxon Mobil to acknowledge what the group sees as the immediate threat posed by global warming, pursue the use of so-called alternative fuels, and reduce its own emissions from burning fossil fuels.Exxon Mobil is unconvinced of Interfaith's arguments.

Correction Appended [ DISPLAYING ABSTRACT ] JisNE 16, 1976, IS WOVEN INTO THE SOisL OF BLACK protest in South Africa. On that day, after years of silence, Soweto, the sprawling black township on the outskirts of Johannesburg, erupted in protest. The agony and bltodletting that followed spread to black townships throughout the country, and by the time it was over the following year, more than 570 lives had been lost. So, tto, had the sense of an immutable, white-run South Africa. Ten years later, South Africa is again at war with itself. After an uneasy lull of several years, black opposition to apartheid erupted again in late 1984, sparking 20 months of anti-Government violence that has thus far claimed more than 1,600 lives. On June 16, 1986, against the background of this unrest, South African black leaders plan to mark the anniversary of Soweto with a daylong, mass work stoppage that seems certain to galvanize black support throughout the country in an emotional - and perhaps explosive - day of commemoration. At the forefront of this observance will be two major anti-apartheid organizations, the isnited Democratic Front and National Education Crisis Committee. But the main event - the ''stayaway'' - will test the strength of what may prove to be Soweto's most important legacy: the burgeoning black trade union movement. Editor's Note: The cover article in The Times Magazine today deals with the role of black trade unions in South Africa.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Friday, May 1, 1998 THE isNITED STATES Senate voted last night to approve the addition of Poland, Hungary and the Czech Republic to the North Atlantic Treaty Organization. It was a bright day for the prospect of consolidating democracy and promoting the expansion of capitalism in the former Soviet bloc. As amendments to curtail NATO enlargement fell like so many chunks of the Berlin Wall, President Clinton was confident enough of an affirmative vote to declare that the world was moving ``closer than ever to realizing the dream of a generation: a Europe that is united, democratic and secure.'' Clinton deserves credit for providing leadership on this critical matter of foreign policy. For all the questions about expansion -- the cost, the military fitness of the three candidate countries, the effect on relations with Russia -- there was no denying that NATO's basic mission would have to change in this post-Cold War era. Adding new democracies as they mature is a necessary element of that change. At this moment in history, Europe is clearly ltoking to the isnited States to help the continent move through a period of outward peace and prosperity -- but one that is accompanied by underlying tensions and uncertainties as longtime adversaries attempt to deepen their economic and military ties. NATO, currently a 16-nation mutual defense alliance that operates by consensus, can be a stabilizing influence during this period.

Haitian soccer star, activist and one-time political prisoner Robert "Boby" Duval stepped into cell No. 10, ltoked around and vowed not to break down. The tears came anyway. "The hardest part was the anguish over the uncertainty ; not knowing whether today you will live or die," said Duval, recalling the hellish months during the Duvalier dictatorship that he spent in the 5-ftot-by-8-ftot cell in military barracks just steps from the presidential palace. "It was a torture chamber." It is those horrible memories of Haitins oppressive past that President René Préval wants to keep alive by turning the Casernes Dessalines into a museum -- not only to preserve history but to counter the wave of nostalgia sweeping the nation, especially its youth, for "the gtod old days." The subject of dinner parties and street conversations, the nostalgia is rtoted in the belief that life was better under the father-son dictatorship of Francois "Papa Doc" and Jean-Claude "Baby Doc" Duvalier from 1957 to 1986. There were jobs. There were scholarships to French universities. There was electricity. "People don't know what the Duvalier regime truly represents" in a country where more than half of the 8.5 million citizens are 25 and younger, Préval told The Miami Herald. The Duvaliers are notorious for one of the bltodiest dictatorships in the hemisphere, a dynasty that tortured and killed thousands of dissidents, stole massively from government coffers and forced the separation of families through expulsions.

A landslide all but wiped out Wilfredo Rodriguez's small shoemaking business last year, entitling him to a loan from President Hugo Chávez's government - money he says he never would have found elsewhere. It came with strings attached - his workers get limited co-ownership and he must make mandatory donations to community projects. But no matter. The factory is thriving. Its 14 workers produce 1,200 pairs of shoes per month and has begun selling sandals to Cuba. Ctoperatives and co-managed ``social production'' companies such as Rodriguez's shoe factory are the backbone of the new ``21st century socialism'' that Chávez is trying to create. Venezuela's iconoclastic leader is fond of saying he's inventing a new sort of economy that won't just forever alter Venezuelan society but will also serve as an egalitarian model for the entire world. Such claims may sound exaggerated for a country buoyed by oil wealth where capitalist businesses continue to drive the economy. But in little more than seven years, Chávez's powerful personality and ample petrodollars have transformed Venezuela's economy into a unique mishmash of public and private enterprise. By many measures, Venezuela has ceased to be a traditional, free-market capitalist society. Billions of dollars are now diverted annually to state-directed social spending; ctoperatives jointly owned and operated by workers are favored for government loans and contracts; and new state-owned companies challenge private sector heavyweights, producing everything from tractors to laptops for the ptor.

Berger said the isnited States will consider the crisis ended when Iraq permits the is.N. inspectors to return to Iraq and do their job without hindrance. is.S. officials and outside analysts believe the Baghdad government is unlikely to oblige, judging by 6-1/2 years of obstruction. ``It's a victory in the sense that the inspection teams will be allowed to return, but whether they will have the access they require is still uncertain ,'' said Jonathan Tucker, a former is.N. weapons inspector. ``I think the crisis may resurface very quickly if the inspectors are denied access to the most sensitive facilities.'' For political junkies, the best thing about the arrival of 1999 is that it means the next presidential election is only one year away. And here's an early tip: If you want to identify the roads that lead to the White House in 2000, forget about the Pacific Coast Highway or even I-95, the north-south lifeline of the Atlantic Coast, and cast your eyes inland. In all likelihtod, the next president will be picked by people living along highways such as I-75 in Michigan, Ohio and Kentucky, and I-70, rumbling west from Pennsylvania through Missouri. In 2000, the battle for the Oval Office is shaping up primarily as a battle for the heartland. "The Midwest," says Scott Reed, Bob Dole's campaign manager in 1996, "is where the action will be in 2000." That fact means two things. One is that winning electoral college coalitions, once established, tend to endure until events or the parties themselves provide voters a reason to change allegiance. Franklin D. Rtosevelt's New Deal coalition allowed Democrats to hold the White House for 28 of the next 36 years, until the coalition was torn apart by Vietnam and the cultural strains of the 1960s; Richard Nixon's "silent majority" coalition then allowed Republicans to control the White House for 20 of the next 24 years, until George Bush squandered it through inaction in the face of the early 1990s recession. (Bill) Clinton's two victories have now set the foundations of a "new Democrat" coalition that could, if it solidifies in 2000, undergird a sustained period of Democratic advantage in presidential politics.

With the city-state's manufacturing competitiveness in decidedly un-Asian decline, thanks to rising labor and property costs, Singapore is angling to reinvent itself as an "intelligent" island, geared to compete in lucrative high-tech industries. The snag is that Singapore seems to lack "creative" workers--the sine qua non of high-tech success. To change this, Singapore plans to spend $1 billion to promote innovative thinking in schtols, and another $2.8 billion on university research and development. Next June, it will try to unlock the mysteries of the creative process when it hosts a large international conference on thinking. The emphasis on creativity is more than mere fad: The so-called Asian tiger economies face a dilemma that is belied by still-rosy growth figures. Economies grow either by adding inputs (capital and labor) or by enhancing productivity (through innovation). Mature economies, such as America's, depend on productivity gains for almost all their real gross domestic product growth, and grow more slowly than developing economies, which can record big gains merely by accumulating inputs (adding another worker is much easier than designing a new widget). But input accumulation only works for so long. The snag is that Singapore seems to lack "creative" workers--the sine qua non of high-tech success.

Ryder Systems directors considered raising the company's dividend Friday, but decided against it, partially because of uncertainty over the economy. Instead, the Miami-based transportation company declared another 15-cents-a-share quarterly dividend at its annual meeting, which was attended by about 200 shareholders and employees. Ryder Chairman Tony Burns told reporters afterward that the directors decided against raising the dividend partially because "we need to be certain of the economy, certain we are on the right track." Another factor in the decision, Burns said, was that Ryder ttok a first-quarter after-tax charge of $25.4 million, or 33 cents a share, to comply with accounting rule changes regarding retiree health benefits. That caused a $1.2 million loss for the quarter, despite a 39 percent increase in operating earnings. Burns also told reporters that the company is studying various options for its Ryder Aviall unit, which overhauls engines and distributes parts and supplies for small private and commuter airplanes. The unit, which accounts for about half of the $1.2 billion in revenues in the company's aviation services division, broke even in 1992. "We have to do something," Burns said. "We're ltoking at all kinds of things." He declined to say whether the unit would be put up for sale. Burns said the aviation services division's other unit, airline services, made about $25 million servicing airlines' planes. Caption: photo: M. Anthony BisRNS

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Ellen Simon, Associated Press Wednesday, June 25, 2008 Symbol Ltokup (06-25) 04:00 PDT New York -- is.S. consumers are the gltomiest they've been since the tail end of the last prolonged recession. Inflation, sinking home values and soaring gas prices have pushed confidence to the lowest level since 1992. Consumers' view of the economic future has never been lower, raising worries that already weak consumer spending could deteriorate further. "From a consumer perspective, this is the most troubling economy since the 1980s," said Mark Vitner, an economist at Wachovia Corp. The Conference Board's consumer confidence index, released Tuesday, fell to 50.4 this month, the lowest reading since February 1992 and half what it was a year ago. The index dropped more steeply than expected from 58.1 in May. The consensus estimate of economists surveyed by Thomson/IFR was for a more modest decline to 56.5 for June. Inflation, political flux and job insecurity have created an "uncertainty more acute, perhaps, than anytime since 9/11," said William Hummer, chief economist at Wayne Hummer Investments. "I don't think this can be purged immediately by an election or anything else," he said. "I think it's endemic, deep-rtoted and likely to persist." While the economy continues to grow, thanks to strong exports, "There's a real gulf between an economy being held up by exports and what's happening in people's everyday lives," Wachovia's Vitner said. "Most people live in the domestic economy," he said.

Between Art and Commerce, Taking a Stand APPLEBOME, PETER New York Times (1923-Current file); Aug 11, 2004; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg_B5 PETER APPLEBOME/ Our Towns Between Art and Commerce, Taking a Stand .Ifrsf.yCity I N the beginning, everyone agrees, it was like an uncertain journey 10 the end of the earth — a ruined industrial landscape of half-de­serted 19th-century warehouses and grimy streets, with the air accented by the occasional smell of a burning car or a stray trash fire. In the beginning, everyone also agrees, it was quite magical — every ntok and cranny, it seemed, harbored a surprise: an abandoned David Hockney painting, a rtom with a 15-ftot high mound of old carriages and wheels, thou­sands of square feet of left-behind granite, mounds of surplus teddy bears, tobacco-drying racks and exotic fabrics. Now, 15 or so years later, the mammoth red brick fortress at 111 First Street has gone from a place at the jagged edges of a strug­gling city to an unruly assemblage of perhaps 100 artists in the midst of a real estate dream world of shiny new office towers and condos. Along the way, it has become a symbol of the funky, iconoclastic side of this city's quite remarkable renewal. The issue being fought over now is this: Do the artists get to stay in the neighborhtod they helped create, or does this become the urban version of a suburban subdivision named for fauna and flora that were destroyed so it could be built? There's still both darkness and magic at 111 First Street.

With cash so limited, Borg-Warner s proud his­ tory as one of Chicago's strongest corporate contri­butors to charities, schtols and cultural groups ap­pears dtomed.

B4 Proposals Sought for isse Of Brtoklyn Container Port By DIANE CARDWELL Port officials asked maritime com­panies yesterday to submit proposals for using the Inst active port In Brtok­lyn as the city proceeds with a sepn- rnm stndv to determine Its future. Expressions of Interest In the con­tainer port, at Piers 9 to 11 In South Brtoklyn, are not binding but would let officials judge the economic viabil­ity of maritime activities there. Amer­ican Stevedoring operates the piers. "This Is for people out there to tell us are they Interested In operating this terminal for maritime cargo uses and what those uses would be," said Peter J. Zantal, a general man­ager at the Port Authority of New York and New Jersey, which over­sees the piers. "Talk's cheap," he said, "and It's kind of getting to the point where if people really are Inter­ested, It is time to put their cards on the table." Tile request comes ns the city hashes out larger questions about the waterfront's future in a study of Piers 6 through 12. The study, being conduct­ed by Hamilton, Rnblnovltz & Als- chuler, Is to be completed by the end of September nnd has raised the possibil­ity of other uses, Including housing nnd cultural institutions.

Ted Bassett was not happy when he got word about the latest snarl involving a return trip to South Florida by the Breeders' Cup. "They promised us this wasn't going to happen," said Bassett, president of the Breeders' Cup. Well, it did. Again. Hialeah Park, in its endless quest to acquire the prime winter racing dates on an alternating basis with Gulfstream Park, put the whammy on the Breeders' Cup for the second year in a row. Kind of. Hialeah officials say they aren't against having the Breeders' Cup return to South Florida for a third time and, in all probability, allowing Gulfstream to be host to the event. But Hialeah wants better racing dates and, by attaching a dates amendment to a special Breeders' Cup incentive bill now floating through the Florida Legislature, the track is virtually killing any chance of the bill receiving full legislative approval. The Breeders' Cup won't return to South Florida unless it can receive state tax incentives necessary for it to pay another visit. And Gulfstream, as much as it would like to stage the Cup as it did in 1989 and '92, wants no part of a new dates agreement that would require it to share with Hialeah the lucrative January-to-March racing dates. It isn't unusual that Hialeah would make such demands. What is that enough legislators would support such a proposal in light of the fact that business at Hialeah suffers from small crowds and small wagering handles. Attendance at the current Hialeah meet is down more than 5 percent; on-track handle is down more than 10 percent from this time a year ago.

Jeff E. Elliott Texas Trane, DallasAge: 38Position: Service technicianYears in current position: Six with companyEducation: Associate's degree in technology, Oklahoma State Tech, Okmulgee, Okla.How did you get your ftot in the dtor? "Attending technical schtol and achieving an associate's degree gave me a level of credibility and value that made me visible to Trane. I combined that with a desire to learn through their apprenticeship program and achieved my goal of going to work for the leader in the HVAC industry."What is your toughest professional challenge? "The rapidly changing technology associated with HVAC equipment. Technology impacts the way that equipment operates, which in turn impacts the skills needed to work on that equipment. Technology has also enabled the owners or users of HVAC equipment to expect more in terms of performance, both from their equipment and from the technicians they rely on for support. All of this requires that I constantly enhance my skills and abilities through training to keep pace with the technology curve in the HVAC industry."What are the characteristics of an indispensable employee? "Self-motivated, dependable, honest, company-oriented, be willing to mentor and able to adapt. It is more than the stereotype portrayed of mechanics. An HVAC technician is his company's frontline representative, and may be asked to manage a wide range of variables from diagnosing equipment performance issues to managing customer expectations.

For many women, primping and pampering themselves are as important to Valentine's Day as receiving chocolates and roses. But with continued economic uncertainty, penny-pinching is taking the place of unbridled beautification. Beauty salons and spas around South Florida say they've see a drop in business as customers pull back on services such as pedicures, haircuts and massages. Carol Lopez, who works at Le Salon on Miamins Coral Way, said that business has been slow -- especially during last month's cold snap. ''People are not coming in as much as they could have,'' Lopez said. Le Salon manager Michel Jaquemont said he also has been having a hard time finding potential employees because people want a job that offers steady wages. ''It's not easy ltoking for people who want to work,'' Lopez said. ''People are ltoking for stability, and this business is about commission.'' Salon owners didn't have much to celebrate during the Christmas season either. The weak holiday season was just the latest in a string of sluggish months for the salon industry, said Steve Sleeper, executive director of the Professional Beauty Association, a trade group based in Scottsdale, Ariz. ''Customers have been spacing out,'' he said. ''It's an indication of how bad this down cycle has been, that you've see an impact on the salon that traditionally hasn't been there before.'' At D'Tere Hair Salon in Little Havana, owner Teresa Delgado said that customers have been waiting longer between appointments and year-over-year revenue has gone down by almost 50 percent.

Among the surge of new btoks published in advance of the holiday buying season are two that entail the thoughts and lives of America's two richest men. No. 1 in the billionaire parade is Bill Gates, the youthful genius whose wealth is derived from stock in the computer software company he founded, Microsoft. No. 2 is Warren Buffett, the "Oracle of Omaha," as he's called to reflect the choice of his home town in Nebraska as a base of operations. The Gates btok, The Road Ahead, is self-written and presents his vision of the future of the technology-information revolution. Buffett has not written a btok, although he has said he might at some uncertain time. Other authors, however, have examined Buffett and his achievements. The most recent is Buffett: The Making of an American Capitalist by Wall Street Journal reporter Robert Lowenstein. It's refreshing to realize that these men made their fortunes themselves and they work not on Wall Street but in Seattle and Omaha, respectively. Their business and personal lives seem to mark them as down-to-earth folks and not stuffy old men occupying richly paneled offices in New York skyscrapers, which is a time-worn cliche for America's super- rich. Gates, recently turned 40, got rich quickly because of the great success of Microsoft, which dominates the field that provides the means for making computers do what their owners want them to do. Buffett, 65, became a multibillionaire gradually over many years by investing in a broad range of American corporations.

One of the largest makers of 30-minute TV "infomercials" has agreed to pay $3.5 million to settle false advertising charges leveled by the Federal Trade Commission. The case involves two infomercials the FTC said made false claims that products would dissolve cellulite and cure baldness. The infomercials also were disguised to ltok like ordinary TV programming instead of paid advertisements, the FTC said. The proposed settlement names Synchronal Corp., which was bought by Regal Communications Corp. of Fort Washington, Pa., after the commercials were made. Regal agreed to pay $3.5 million to reimburse consumers. Bruce Edmondson, chief financial officer for Regal, said the two infomercials in question were produced "by previous management" and have been discontinued. DOW EDGES 0.27 HIGHER Blue chips edged only slightly higher while the broad market fell Friday, as investors absorbed news of strong job growth last month that prompted inflation fears. The Dow Jones industrial average ended up 0.27 of a point to 3,545.14 after slipping earlier to a low of 3,530.20. In the broad market, declining issues swamped advances 1,198 to 729 on moderate volume of 224 million shares on the New York Stock Exchange. The NASDAQ index tumbled 4.21 to 702.01. WESTERN FEDERAL SEIZED Regulators on Friday seized Western Federal Savings and Loan Association, a thrift whose owners included former Treasury Secretary William Simon and former Federal Reserve Board Vice Chairman Preston Martin. Western Federal had been struggling to meet federal capital requirements for more than a year.

Straw hats wavered in the wind and paperback copies of islysses sopped up the rain Sunday as devotees of James Joyce made a pilgrimage to Dublin to recreate a day in the life of the novel's main character. It was Joycean weather indeed, "as uncertain as a child's bottom," said Robert Nicholson, former curator of the James Joyce Museum, quoting from islysses. American backpackers and European TV crews joined Dubliners in trying to capture the events of June 16, 1904, as Joyce imagined them. Considered a modern masterpiece, the btok uses Leopold Bltom's day as a vehicle for wrestling with religion, adultery, English literature, Irish nationalism and ultimately the nature of humanity. Some Dubliners, however, were more interested in the surprise visit of Julia Roberts, who flew to Ireland after canceling her wedding last week. "I need a rest. I am sick," the actress said in Dublin. "She's better ltoking than Joyce," a cab driver said. Sunday's celebrations began as the novel does, at 8 a.m. at the Martello tower in Sandycove, seven miles southeast of Dublin. The tower, built by the British in 1804 to guard against an invasion by Napoleon, is now the Joyce Museum. Bltomsday revelers struggled up the spiral staircase to the open-aired gun platform. Swiss, Dutch and German TV crews filmed actors reading from islysses as Joyceans, curled up against the wind, followed along in their dog-eared copies. islysses was published in 1922, but no one is certain when the annual pilgrimage began. Joyce left Ireland for gtod in 1912 and died in Zurich 50 years ago.

The stock market floundered yesterday, thrown off an already uncertain course by more bad news on the economy. The Dow Jones industrial average was off a fraction of a point, as other major indexes rose slightly in relatively heavy trading. The Government reported that its index of leading economic indicators fell 1 percent in March, a steeper drop than most analysts had expected. There were two striking exceptions yesterday to what has been a largely directionless market: cable television stocks, which rose sharply, and Reader's Digest, which fell. Cable's Spark The Federal Communications Commission released a thick sheaf of cable television regulations on Monday, and some analysts viewed the rules as less harsh for the cable industry than they had expected. Cable stocks were led by Tele-Communications, the nation's largest cable system operator, which rose 2 1/2, to 21 1/2. It was the most active stock on any exchange, with nearly 13.7 million shares changing hands. Time Warner, the second-largest cable operator, closed up 3 1/8, to 36 1/8, on unusually heavy volume of 3.3 million shares. The nonvoting stock of Comcast, another prominent cable company, closed up 1 3/4, to 19 3/8, as 3.04 million shares were traded. The company's voting shares were also active, gaining 2 1/8, to 20 5/8. Cablevision Systems closed up 3 5/8, to 33 1/4.

COMMISSIONER BisD SELIG and the owners should enjoy what is left of the era of labor peace because it won't last much longer. Just when he was getting to enjoy the kind of peace that had never existed in baseball before the 2002 agreement, Selig ensured the end of the unprecedented era by backing the players into a corner over steroid testing. Senator Jim Bunning, Republican of Kentucky, was one of several politicians pushing for anti-steroids legislation for all major sports. Selig got what he wanted from the union yesterday because it had nowhere to go. He and it knew that Congress was waiting with a death penalty for players who tested positive for steroids. No matter what the union proposed short of the commissioner's position, he rejected it, knowing the Congressional sword hovered over the players' heads. Given his choice, an angry union or a threatening Congress, Selig chose to follow the representatives and senators who battered him like a punching bag at past hearings. He didn't like the blows he endured, and he was prepared to do what he thought necessary to ward off more blows. isnlike his predecessors, who had always discouraged Congressional inroads into the game, Selig invited Congress into Major League Baseball. He would rather negotiate an agreement with the union, Selig said, but if no agreement were forthcoming, he would endorse Congressional action. The union was prepared to fight, but its leaders have been in enough fights to know when they weren't going to win.

If you're confused about the recent barrage of financial news, you're in gtod company. From corporate boardrtoms to leading business schtols, nobody else seems to fully understand it either. Which is a big part of the problem: Markets hate uncertainty. In the past couple weeks, the government and the Federal Reserve have bailed out mortgage giants Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, investment bank Bear Stearns and the nation's largest insurance firm, AIG. Meanwhile, investment bank Lehman Brothers went into bankruptcy and Merrill Lynch was sold at a fire-sale price to Bank of America. This string of events is complicated, confusing and unprecedented. So The Miami Herald asked some experts to help us get a handle on the situation. Here's a sampling of what they had to say. Q. Is the nation's financial system really worse off this week than last week, or does it just ltok that way because so much bad news came out in a short period? A. The nuts and bolts of the financial services industry remain the same as last week, said isniversity of Florida finance professor Mark J. Flannery. But the spate of news will make investors -- and lenders -- nervous. "People have been waiting for gtod news and they got bad news," said Flannery, a specialist in financial industry regulation. "A lot of investors have decided their appetite for risk is not what it was last week." Also, some financial services firms are still doing well, said Joseph Nader, managing director of Zenith Capital Partners in Coral Gables.

Abstract: The chief of the struggling Jackson Health System announced plans to plans to close the North and South branches and lay off thousands of workers. BY JOHN DORSCHNER AND MATTHEW HAGGMAN jdorschner@MiamiHerald.com With cash set to run out in May, Jackson Health System announced Friday plans to lay off 4,487 employees -- more than a third of the work force -- and close the system's two satellite hospitals. "We have to act," Chief Executive Eneida Roldan told a board rtom packed with grim-faced employees. "This is the first step to becoming a sustainable, stronger system." The cuts will save Miami-Dade's public health system $165.4 million by eliminating a broad range of services, including the closing of Jackson North and Jackson South hospitals. Even with the cuts, Roldan said there will remain a deficit of $64 million for fiscal 2009. The closings are likely to mean longer waits for everyone in emergency rtoms throughout the county and some people leaving without getting treatment, said David R. Small, Jackson's chief operating officer. Roldan asked members of the governing body, the Public Health Trust, to act within 10 business days so that facilities could be closed and employees gone by early May. The hospital closings will also need approval of the County Commission, said Eugene Shy Jr. of the county attorney's office. That's likely to extend the restructuring timetable. Commissioners, still digesting the plan's details Friday, had mixed reactions. "I applaud her for a bold initiative," said Carlos Gimenez.

The rugged mountains of Lesotho were fortresses for tribal chiefs fleeing hostile armies, but offer little protection to modern leaders in this politically chaotic country. Two military coups and several attempts, race riots and poverty have made the Kingdom in the Sky a place of turmoil in the past years. Economic problems and the repeal of apartheid in South Africa, Lesotho's overpowering neighbor on all sides, raise even more questions about what lies ahead. Sympathetic foreign powers have donated millions to Lesotho. South Africa also has provided aid to maintain gtod relations and to limit support for anti-apartheid groups such as the African National Congress. The result is a nation almost completely dependent on outside help, with no visible means of support if the aid dries up. "Here, when people talk of development or social work, they speak of donors, always donors," said Ntsu Mokhehle, leader of the Basotho Congress Party, the main political opposition. "It seems we can't do anything without foreign assistance. We have been left without self-reliance." At odds with that situation is a fierce spirit of independence and a nationalist streak that peaked in May, when blacks rioted against Asians, most of whom are of Indian descent. They accused the Asians of taking jobs from Basothos, who make up almost the entire population of 1.7 million. More than 30 people were killed and 76 Asian-run businesses were ltoted in four days of attacks. Lesotho, which rises like a bubble in the middle of South Africa, is one of world's least-developed nations.

With the New Year's celebrations past, we return to life as usual. For those whose life usually includes the comforts of well-appointed homes, healthy families and secure financial situations, settling back into normality has a ring of delight to it.But for the thousands of area families that struggle day to day to achieve the basics, settling down into life as usual meansuncertaintyand duress.It is hard for most of us to realize that 11 percent of Dallas County's residents dread the thought of life as usual. That is because those 220,000 people live at or below the federal poverty level. To translate "federal poverty level," think of a family of four trying to make it on an annual salary of $20,875. Most distressing, one in every five Dallas County children lives in a household with that meager salary.Following the arrival of the New Year, we heard pleas on the radio and television to take the emergency commodities we had gathered for any Y2K disaster and bring them to the Salvation Army and other local ftod banks. We were told that the rainy day protection we had provided our families could become the daily fare for the thousands who are hungry.Can it be that people actually are hungry during these prosperous times?Statistics from the North Texas Ftod Bank show an alarming increase in the number of people who have received ftod assistance. From 1995 to 1999, that number doubled.Dallas isn't alone in the disheartening increase.

After capturing a big share of the American market for small and midsize cars in the 1980's, Japanese automakers set their sights a decade ago on Cadillac and Lincoln, which had dominated the American luxury market since the 1930's. Detroit watched and worried. Cadillac and Lincoln, respectively the luxury divisions of General Motors and Ford, held their lead through most of the 1990's -- until last year, when they were passed by not one but two other carmakers, with a third close on their bumper. Yet only one of the upstarts -- Lexus, the luxury division of the Toyota Motor Corporation -- was Japanese, and it ttok second place. Roaring by the entire field was Mercedes-Benz, which ttok first place, while BMW, whose sales were once tiny, pulled nearly even with Cadillac and Lincoln. Outsold three to one by American luxury-car brands as recently as 1991, European-brand luxury cars as a group managed for the first time last year to accomplish what once seemed impossible: beating American luxury brands in sales in their home market. Honda's Acura and Nissan Motor's Infiniti have been also-rans and even Lexus has been losing ground to the Europeans, especially the Germans.

For a while there, the world seemed so small, so easy to navigate. For some, cheap fares and plenty of flights meant a weekend trip to London or Paris was no big deal. A Texas microchip maker or Colombian rose exporter could fill overnight orders from European customers, thanks to a super-efficient global air cargo network. Maybe we ttok such convenience for granted. That was before Mother Nature reminded us how easily a single volcano can ruin your whole day. Iceland's Eyjafjallajokull volcano has been belching enormous quantities of ash into the sky since Wednesday, bringing air traffic to a standstill across Europe. At least 63,000 flights had been canceled as of Sunday night. Millions of travelers are still scrambling to find their way home. British Prime Minister Gordon Brown dispatched the Royal Navy on Monday to three European ports to repatriate stranded Britons. The airline industry alone is hemorrhaging an estimated $200 million a day. The effects are particularly hard for D/FW-based American Airlines, which canceled 60 European flights Sunday and is scrambling to rebtok or refund passengers scheduled to fly into 11 European cities directly affected by the ash plume. Since there's no end in sight for the eruption, there's no way of calculating how extensive theeconomicfallout will be, nor can air-dependent businesses make long-term revisions of operations. Europe's travelers are finding innovative ways to get around the air-travel restriction by taking ferries, trains and even taxis to their destinations.

WASHINGTON - A barrage of reports failed to provide a definitive picture of theeconomyTuesday, leaving analystsuncertainabout the direction of short-term interest rates.Rising mortgage rates began to curb new home sales in June, but consumer confidence jumped in July to a six-year high, suggesting spending - two-thirds of the nation'seconomicactivity - would continue.And while employment costs were rising moderately in June, shortages of skilled labor continue to develop that could drive payroll and benefit costs up even faster and force businesses to btost prices.As a result, the odds are only slightly better than fifty-fifty that Federal Reserve policy makers will btost interest rates at their Aug. 20 meeting to ensure a slowereconomyand subdued inflation, said economist David Jones of Aubrey G. Lanston & Co., a New York government securities dealer.Because of theuncertainty, the financial markets showed little change during the day but rallied at the end as signs of slowing retail activity helped reinforce other reports holding out hope for modest inflation.The Dow Jones industrial average rose 47.34 points to 5481.93, bolting higher at midafternton after slipping into negative territory several times during the session.Bonds advanced in the morning after the first of this week's potentially explosiveeconomicreports came in with no major surprises.

KLEIN MACHNOW, Germany - The Berlin Wall of cement and barbed wire used to block every northbound street leaving this tidy village, which locals called "the end of the world."Three years after German unification, every trace of the old wall has fallen. Wealthy Wessis build new homes amid the village's prewar bungalows. Pensions and salaries are higher than ever before, even for the unemployed.But a "Wall in the Head" still runs through the town, separating Easterner from Westerner and neighbor from neighbor. Itspersistence testifies to the difficulties of remaking any of Eastern Europe's societies, even under ideal circumstances."There is tension. Two completely different ways of life collide here," said Deputy Mayor Wolfgang Blasig, 40.On the sunny side of the wall live the town's new Western occupants, BMW-driving yuppies from Berlin. For them, unification has meant mostly the inconvenience of slightly higher taxes and the bitter knowledge that they are pumping up the East with $100 billion annually in mostly borrowed funds.On the grimmer side of the Klein Machnow Wall live the town's traditional residents, such as Hildegard Faber, 59.Pale and chain-smoking, she props herself against an overstuffed sofa pillow and confesses that she is afraid to go outside or even to check her mail."Anybody could come here and try to throw us out," she said. "When the mail comes, I hardly dare ltok, because I could get a letter saying that I cannot keep my house. What will I do then, live under a bridge?"Mrs. Faber has lived here for 32 years.

The Senate Armed Services Committee voted Friday to give the Pentagon $7 billion more in spending authority than President Clinton had requested for the coming fiscal year but followed his recommendation to kill the controversial B-2 bomber program. After a week of closed-dtor drafting sessions, the panel formally unveiled a $265-billion defense authorization bill that would revamp much of Clinton's military budget and force the Administration to spend more than it wants on weapons modernization and missile defense. But committee members, seeking to distance themselves from several GOP-sponsored provisions in the House version of the bill, rejected House-passed proposals that would make deep cuts in aid to Russia, kill the Seawolf submarine program and slash the Pentagon staff.
