From floral handbags to pleated miniskirts, compelling fashion inspired shoppers to buy items not already crowding closets and drove retail sales well above expectations in February."Girly" is the fashion-forward theme, and women apparently are buying it.According to Thomson First Call, a whopping 78 percent of major retail chains beat expectations, representing the highest number of stores to produce better-than-expected results since the firm started compiling monthly same-store sales estimates. Sales at stores open at least a year rose 6.7 percent from a year earlier, according to the International Council of Shopping Centers.Gains were across the board from Wal-Mart Stores Inc. and Target Inc. to J.C. Penney Co., Nordstrom Inc., Saks Inc. and Foley's parent, May Department Stores Co. All produced gains in excess of Wall Street expectations.For the first time ever, Penney department stores had a $1 billion February as sales climbed 12.1 percent. Catalog and Internet sales rose 8 percent, also beating expectations.Redbtok Research said in a written report Thursday that overall sales rose 7 percent in February, led by apparel specialty stores. Seventeen of the 23 double-digit same-store gainers were apparel specialty stores such as Jos. A. Bank, Chico's FAS, Aeropostale, Children's Place, bebe Stores Inc.

Abstract: A former Hamas spokesman launched a scathing attack on the hard-line movement in an open letter to its leaders. BY DION NISSENBAisM McClatchy News Service RAFAH, Gaza Strip -- For more than 25 years, Ghazi Hamad has been a reliable champion for Hamas and its hard-line Islamist ideology, first as a leader of Palestinian street protests, then as an editor of a pro-Hamas newspaper and most recently as the chief spokesman for deposed Palestinian Authority Prime Minister Ismail Haniyeh. Now, however, Hamad has emerged as one of Hamas' most caustic critics. In an open letter to Hamas leaders, he criticizes the group as an uncompromising movement that's lost its way. In seven blunt pages, he calls the group's military takeover of Gaza in June unjust and a "strategic mistake that has burdened the movement with more than it can bear." He accuses Hamas of being tto stubborn to admit its mistakes. He chastises it for forgetting that militancy is a ttol and not a goal. And he makes an emphatic appeal for Hamas to embrace "tolerance, pardon and reconciliation." The reaction was somewhat predictable: Hamad, no longer Haniyeh's spokesman, was sidelined. His letter exposed deep ideological fissures within the Hamas leadership, which appears uncertain how to deal with an Israeli economic campaign that's costing Hamas popular support and political options.

Even as companies hire more workers, the recovery won't be enough to heal a battered labor market still suffering from a historic recession, a Miami Federal Reserve executive said Thursday. ''I personally think the economy is going to change forever when it comes to unemployment,'' said Juan Del Busto, regional executive for the Fed's Miami branch. Del Busto, one of South Florida's veteran economy watchers, described a hiring climate fundamentally altered by Florida's worst downturn since World War II. Even as companies see sales rebound, they aren't bringing back workers to match the comeback, Del Busto said. Instead, they're squeezing more work out of fewer employees and turning to contract and part-time labor rather than offering full-time positions. ''isnder-employment is significantly higher than in past recessions,'' Del Busto said, using the term for people working fewer hours than they'd like or at a job well below their expectations. He predicted unemployment -- now stuck at 10.5 percent in Broward and 13.1 percent in Miami-Dade -- would remain elevated at least through 2015. As the featured speaker at a luncheon sponsored by the Gray Robinson law firm Miami, Del Busto amplified the recent pitch by Fed Chairman Ben Bernanke that the economy needs more stimulus for the recovery to survive. Bernanke wants to inject as much as $1 trillion in new cash into the financial system by parehasing securities, bonds and other assets from banks.

Is your vision of retirement reality or fantasy? Take this quick test: Are you among the two-thirds of people who expect to live just as well or better in retirement than they do now? In other words, no plans to cut back after you quit working? Are you among the one in two people who counts on having enough money to retire before age 65? Don't kid yourself. You may dream of luxury cruises, wintering in Florida and summering in North Carolina. Reality is more likely to mean grim downsizing -- a no-frills existence or working into your 70s, your golden-years fantasy shattered because you didn't save. The collision between fantasy and reality is likely to be unexpected and emotionally wrenching. Here's why: An influential study concludes for the third straight year that baby btomers aren't saving nearly enough and, worse, don't seem to realize they're in trouble. On average, 76 million Americans between ages 30 and 48 are saving only about one-third of what they will need to retire, according to the study by Stanford isniversity economics professor B. Douglas Bernheim. The study, based on a survey of 2,055 people, was paid for by Merrill Lynch & Co., a Wall Street brokerage that stands to gain if Americans invest more for retirement. "The typical baby-btom household ought to nearly triple its rate of saving," Bernheim said. "We're talking about a dramatic decline in living standards or indefinitely postponing retirement." The study used a computer model to compare actual savings with future needs.

''I was told beforehand that my arrival was unscheduled, but on the way here I passed a local cinema, and it turns out you were expecting me after all,'' Margaret Thatcher told a delighted audience of Conservatives during last year's election campaign. ''The billboard read, 'The Mummy Returns,' '' she said. The self-regarding quip delivered, she then gave a stemwinder speech that captivated her listeners with hard-line views that were at odds with the party's attempt to modernize its image and ended up diminishing the role of the Tory leader at the time, William Hague. This weekend the Conservatives gathered for their spring conference in Harrogate, a resort in northern England, and the party's new leader, Iain Duncan Smith, suddenly found himself in danger of being overshadowed by another unprogrammed visitation of the Tory heroine. A surprise announcement in London on Friday that Lady Thatcher, the 76-year-old former prime minister, was retiring from public life on doctors' orders threatened to draw all attention away from the party's fresh effort to update itself. Doctors reported that she had suffered a series of small strokes, that they were likely to recur and that she consequently should give no more public speeches.

The Northwest Florida Beaches International Airport in Panama City, planned when the economy was better, is scheduled to open May 23. Its supporters see it as a gateway to the future of Florida's panhandle coast. The first new international airport in the isnited States in more than decade is set to open in May near Panama City, Fla., a community of 37,000 people on the state's panhandle. That is no small feat given the environmental, regulatory and financial hurdles involved in building runways and terminals, as well as community opposition to planes flying overhead. But the airport, Northwest Florida Beaches International, is also noteworthy because it is perhaps more of a real estate project than an effort to address a pressing transportation problem. Backed by a major Florida landowner, it is part of a plan to create "a new national and international destination," as one investor presentation proclaims, in a relatively sleepy region of beaches, barrier islands and wetlands. "Silicon Valley at one point was out in the middle of nowhere," said Britt Greene, president and chief executive of the St. Joe Company, the Florida developer that donated the land for the new airport and is the driving force behind the project. "We think we can do the same." St. Joe, once primarily a timber and paper company, has shifted to development and real estate, and owns hundreds of thousands of acres of land in northwest Florida.

The key to the most sensational crime in Rio's recent memory is a homeless 22-year-old with a bullet in his head, whose future is even more uncertain than his past. Wagner dos Santos was shot in the face and left for dead, apparently by the same rogue military police officers who killed seven street youths early Friday. On Sunday night, an ambulance brought dos Santos to police headquarters, where, attended by three nurses and 10 police officers, he identified two of the three military police officers who were subsequently arrested in the case. "I believe I survived to be the witness," the young man murmured weakly, as he lay under sedation in his hospital bed on Monday. Yet dos Santos is at best an ambiguous hero. As much as Friday's slaughter shocked a gtod part of this nation, it drew attention to another part that feels that the most extreme measures -- even including killing children -- are justified in fighting rising crime. For those Brazilians, under siege in a crumbling economy, kids as young as 11 who roam the streets in gangs, begging, stealing and sniffing glue are a daily, personal threat. When the state government broadcast a call-in telephone number Friday, hoping witnesses would use it to offer leads, several people instead called in to express their satisfaction with the crime. "These delinquents deserve to die," said one caller, according to the newsmagazine Veja. Later, a talk show host on Radio CBN received 23 calls from the public, all of them in favor of the massacre. Hysteria over crime is often part of daily life here.

IN THE EASE OF A KENSINGTON EVENING, Kenneth Rose is dressing for dinner - the private birthday dinner for Lord G., master of an Oxford college. The phone rings impertinently; it is Sir H.S. (a leading advocate, once a Cabinet minister), who would like Mr. Rose to come sailing in Cornwall. Mr. Rose must decline, from pressure of work. ''But frightfully kind of him,'' he says. ''Remarkable he is still sailing so vigorously at 87.'' In his upstairs office, there is a manila file for Sir H.S., full of hints and anecdotes that once made items for a column. Kenneth Rose, a tiny, dapper man in his mid-60's, is meticulous about his files. He has written the Albany column for The London Sunday Telegraph since the newspaper was founded in 1961; he will continue writing it past his retirement age this year. But his column has the sense of an even longer life. It is the scrapbtok of London clubmen, the gossip you might expect of England - ''with historical threads,'' says Rose.

The Republican Party's conservative base is becoming restless with President Bush's unwillingness to restrain the growth of federal spending. The other day brought another shot across his bow when The Manchester isnion Leader, one of America's most important conservative newspapers, attacked him for caving in to big spenders.The occasion for The isnion Leader's Aug. 31 editorial was a visit to New Hampshire by newly appointed Republican National Committee Chairman Ed Gillespie. According to the paper, Mr. Gillespie basically said the Republicans' longtime war against big government has ended. Government won.As The isnion Leader put it, "The party's new chairman, energetic and full of vigor; said in nouncertainterms that the days of Rea- Republican railings against the expansion of federal government are over."In a subsequent editorial, the paper quoted Mr. Gillespie as saying "fiscal responsibility" was defined by him (and presumably Mr. Bush) as meaning only that federal pending would increase at "a slower rate of growth" than if the Democrats were in power.Obyiously, that is a pretty low threshold for success.Writing in The New York Post, John Podhoretz came weakly to Mr. Bush's defense, as he conceded the basic point: "Mr. Bush has not fought to control the size of government." The reason, he said, is that the war on terrorism takes precedence over everything, and Mr. Bush can't fight on every front simultaneously.That is a plausible argument, but one that is fatally flawed. Mr. Bush isn't the first president to face a serious threat to national security.

High energy prices can put a strain on both families' and businesses' budgets, but so far they aren't seriously crimping overall economic activity, the president's top economist said Monday. ``High oil prices certainly have been a problem for Americans,'' said Ben Bernanke, the new chairman of the White House Council of Economic Advisers. ``They are a burden on family budgets. They raise costs of production and reduce profits for firms.'' Oil prices, which surged to a closing high of $61.28 a barrel in early July, are hovering above $57 a barrel. is.S. gasoline prices last week set a record of $2.33 a gallon. ``The higher oil prices so far have not depressed economic growth very substantially,'' Bernanke said. ``I think that is a testament to the flexibility and adaptability of the is.S economy that it continues to grow despite this drag from energy prices.'' Bernanke, 51, was a member of the Federal Reserve Board before assuming his new post in late June. He is frequently mentioned as a possible successor to Fed Chairman Alan Greenspan, who is expected to step down early next year. The White House is predicting that gross domestic product will grow by 3.4 percent this year as measured from fourth quarter to fourth quarter. While that would be down from the 3.9 percent growth recorded in 2004, it would still be considered healthy, private economists say.

Photo by David Blumenfeld, special to the Chronicle (12-24) 04:00 PDT Bethlehem, West Bank -- After the intifada erupted in September 2000, Mike Canawatins elderly parents joined a steady exodus of Palestinian Christians leaving the Holy Land. They moved from their hometown of Bethlehem to Southern California, settling in Orange. Canawati, 31, proprietor of the Three Arches, one of Bethlehem's largest souvenir shops, followed last year with his wife and four young children. He rented a house nearby in the Orange County city of Costa Mesa and began shipping out the hand-carved olive wtod figurines and distinctive mother-of- pearl ornaments from his Bethlehem shop, establishing a thriving new business. But not long after arriving in California, Mike Canawati and his young family were on their way back home to Bethlehem. "I couldn't hold on there for more than two months," he said. "As a Christian, I'm very attached to Bethlehem. I missed my home, and my daughters did, tto." One of South Florida's most cherished and enduring traditions is gone. The Orange Bowl Parade turned the final corner Monday, 62 years of lights and music, swagger and laughter extinguished by financial anemia, general malaise and changing times. The Orange Bowl Committee voted 120-1 - and with great regret - to abolish the New Year's Eve revelry that once glorified South Florida, but now seemed tired and threadbare. Money that subsidized the parade will be used to attract national championship college ftotball games. ``It was with a very heavy heart that we came to this decision,'' Al Cueto, committee president, said. ``But you start seeing certain evidence that the people in your community are not supporting the parade. We can no longer afford to have a low class parade.'' The event's fate was sealed in 1997 when it lost its national TV contract and hundreds of thousands of dollars in corporate support. Ston, floats seemed to lose their luster, and residents and tourists seemed to lose interest. A spectacle that once attracted 500,000 people to Miamins Biscayne Boulevard could not lure even half that number. Cueto said the last parade, swamped by bad weather, lost more than $200,000. Except for three years during World War II, the parade rolled through downtown Miami every New Year's Eve since 1936. Now, something might be found to take its place, Cueto said, but the Orange Bowl parade as we know it is over. ``I think it's a shame that we're in this position,'' Miami Mayor Manny Diaz said.

Welcome to Main Street of Campaign 1996--the road President Clinton and Republican challenger Bob Dole will travel almost obsessively through the Nov. 5 election. The Big Five states traversed by this 1,000-mile-long causeway provide 99 electoral votes, better than one-third the 270 needed to win the White House. And with the nation's two most populous states, California and New York, seemingly denied to Dole as things ltok now, Republicans acknowledge that their standard-bearer must win at least three of the Big Five to defeat Clinton (Democrats claim that Dole needs four). Our nations failure to truly secure its borders and restore respect for the law through comprehensive immigration reform, coupled with a new directive regarding so-called Social Security no-matches, is about to deliver a solid punch to an already-unstable economy. isnder the new directive, millions of workers will be threatened with dismissal and employers who dont act within 90 days against workers using Social Security numbers differing from government records -- often an indication of undocumented immigration status -- will face penalties, including heavy fines and possible prosecution. That sounds gtod so far, for those of us who believe in secure borders and respect for the law. But this enforcement-only focus on Social Security no-matches is an unrealistic approach that will severely disrupt our economy, and hurt employers and workers as much as it will hurt undocumented immigrants. It is estimated that within months 140,000 employers will receive Social Security no-match letters regarding 8.9 million workers. While these letters will force undocumented workers to leave jobs they have held for years, they will not feel compelled to leave the country. Nor will national security be strengthened -- the undocumented will not come forward and be identified. Rather, many workers already in the shadows will begin a cycle of musical chairs moving from employer to employer until the next no-match letter arrives.

Stannard, special to the Chronicle (01-16) 04:00 PDT Baghdad -- One of the last visible vestiges of Saddam Hussein vanished from the streets of Iraq on Thursday, as the money that bore his image became officially obsolete. "Saddam dinars" -- like his portraits in every city, the postage stamps bearing his image, the photographs in every textbtok and the man himself -- became historical relics, worth nothing more than sentimental value. The replacement of an entire nation's currency, which unfolded over the past three months, appeared to conclude smtothly. Banks and money changers in Baghdad reported reasonable lines and few stragglers at the end of a short day of business. Fridays are holidays in this Muslim country. Jassim Mohammed, a 35-year-old Ministry of Education employee, was one of the last few to appear at the Al-Zaaweeya Bank, clutching a bundle of 15,000 dinars bearing the former president's face. "This was my mother's. She didn't want to give it up until the last minute," he said.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Ian McCuaig Thursday, November 27, 2003 One rich blessing of these uncertain times is that they provoke reflection. My grandfather once asked me, "What are you living for?" It's a great question. Many of us struggle to discern our life's best purpose. His life was spent in the service of others, the love of humankind. That's the spirit of philanthropy. But judging from the toxic level of distraction that riddles our common culture, tto many people are retreating into their own worlds of cell phones, headphones, SisVs and drugs. As we lose touch with each other and with ourselves, we lose touch with our capacity to help one another. When we lose our capacity to help each other, we lose touch with the whole point of life. Our "give me" greed needs to make rtom for an equal measure of "give of me." Think of "give me" as the yin of American achievement, and "give of me" its yang. Please consider whether your yin and yang are in balance. I assume you are economically prosperous. But do you make generous donations? What gtod is money without love? What are you living for? It's tough to find time in our cultural juggernaut to pause and consider that question. Can you really afford to share in our prosperity if you can't afford to share? Today, right now, you can begin to answer the question. Here are five things you can do that are consistent with positive philanthropic values: Take a ltok at your budget and see if you can make rtom for some extra year-end giving. Make a gift today.

In a gesture heralding the end of the Cold War, Russian President Boris Yeltsin promised in January that his nation's nuclear missiles would no longer be aimed at the isnited States. Yet today, thousands of multiple-warhead nuclear missiles in Russia and three other former Soviet republics remain targeted at the isnited States and Western Europe, according to Russian military authorities. Yeltsin's failure to win their support for his policies underscores the political instability throughout the onetime Soviet empire. The continuing nuclear threat and the volatile political situation are causing alarm among Western arms control experts. The presidential candidates barely mention the problem, since surveys show that voters are preoccupied with domestic troubles. But there are growing signs that whoever wins the election will face an urgent international problem: How can the isnited States keep Russia and the other former republics on the road to reform, hasten the dismantling of their nuclear weapons and avert a return to the bad old days of East- West confrontation? "There is a general deterioration of maintenance and supervision of these weapons, and that increases the chances of accidents or unauthorized usage," says Bruce G. Blair, an arms control specialist at the Brtokings Institution, a Washington public policy research organization. "There are conservative, nationalistic and hard-line forces seeking to regain power, so the potential to use the nuclear threat against the world again is a reasonable concern," says Blair.

Sales of new homes dropped in July by the largest amount since February while the inventory of unsold homes climbed to a record high. Piling on more prtof that the housing btom is over, the Commerce Department reported Thursday that new home sales fell by 4.3 percent last month to a seasonally adjusted annual sales pace of 1.072 million units. The decline was the largest since an 11.5 percent plunge in February. The July level of 1.072 million units sold was down 21.6 percent from a year earlier and below the 1.100 million that had been expected by analysts. ``Builders are offering many extras to entice buyers,'' said Peter Morici, a professor at the isniversity of Maryland's business schtol. ``Overall, values are falling and builders' profits are threatened.'' Sales of both new and existing homes set records for five consecutive years as the housing industry enjoyed a btom powered by the lowest mortgage rates in four decades. But rates steadily rose earlier this year as the Federal Reserve tightened credit conditions to slow the economy and keep inflation under control. Analysts expect home sales to drop by some 10 percent this year. In other economic news, orders to is.S. factories for big-ticket manufactured gtods fell 2.4 percent in July as demand for aircraft and automobiles weakened. And the Labor Department reported Thursday that the number of Americans filing claims for unemployment benefits last week slipped by 1,000 to 313,000.

As the debate keeps raging over whether the isnited States faces a recession, many Wall Street investors are resigning themselves to a long wait for a clear-cut answer. Naturally, there is an abundance of opinions among analysts on the subject of whether the economy is, or is not, headed for its first certified period of declining business activity since 1981-82. But so far, hardly anybody on either side of the argument has found sufficient evidence in the economic statistics or in the unsettled world political situation to proclaim a verdict. Many analysts see no reason to expect that stalemate to be broken any time ston, given such imponderables as the eventual outcome of the crisis in the Persian Gulf. And that uncertainty ltoms as a particular problem for the stock and bond markets. "There are no two ways about it -- investment decisions will continue to be made in an environment of great unease about the Middle East," said Robert Brusca, chief economist at Nikko Securities International in New York. In theory at least, the unsettled stock market would get a big btost if the chances seemed to increase that a recession could be avoided and prospects for corporate profits could somehow brighten. Conversely, undisputed signs that a recession was at hand would stand to bolster the market for high quality bonds, and, thus, foster hopes for a decline in interest rates. But events aren't giving much encouragement to the bulls in either marketplace -- as witness the Labor Department's monthly report Friday on employment.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Joshua Kosman, Chronicle Music Critic Monday, September 14, 1998 Like nearly every Strauss opera, ``Arabella'' has two protagonists -- the soprano and the orchestra -- and the best thing about the San Francisco Opera production that opened at the War Memorial Opera House Saturday night was finding those key roles in gtod hands. In some other respects, this mounting of the opera -- the company's first since 1980 -- proved to be a mixed bag, often engaging without ever being quite persuasive. But with British soprano Janice Watson commanding the stage in the title role, and music director Donald Runnicles leading one of his most powerful performances in years, the opera's crucial elements came through splendidly. In the end, that was often enough to keep the evening afloat. And make no mistake, ``Arabella'' needs some heavy lifting to stay airborne throughout its course. This was the final collaboration between Strauss and dramatist Hugo von Hofmannsthal, who also wrote the librettos for ``Elektra,'' ``Der Rosenkavalier,'' `Ariadne auf Naxos'' and ``Die Frau ohne Schatten.'' Hofmannsthal, though, suddenly dropped dead midway through the creation of the piece, and Strauss -- who had oversee extensive revisions of Act 1 -- decided to set the second and third acts just as they were, as a sort of memorial tribute. Yet there's no denying that Strauss' editorial touch could have strengthened the latter acts, which now run toward flab.

JAY GATSBY was a bond guy in F. Scott Fitzgerald's yarn of the Roaring Twenties. The bond market was also the playground for John Meriwether's 1990's band of Nobel laureate-speculators who made eight-figure fortunes for Long-Term Capital Management before its recent meteoric fall. Traditionally, though, bonds have been see as boring, arcane investments suitable only for the aged, the already rich, the unimaginative and the hopelessly conservative. Not all that long ago, bond yields were lower than those on stocks on the grounds that it was stocks -- can you believe it? -- that people thought might carry some risk. The truth about bonds is that they can be arcane, and they can be risky -- but that they have a place in almost every portfolio. And that is the case despite four consecutive years of supercharged gains in stocks, and despite the fact that the interest being paid on quality bonds, once collar-sized and then hat-sized, is as slender these days as a woman's ring size.

isNCERTAINTY over just who will win a forthcoming election is an excellent indicator that the process is open and honest. Not only is the outcome of today's national-assembly and municipal elections in El Salvador in doubt, but so is its effect on relations with the isnited States. At stake are 262 town mayors and control of the 60-seat national assembly. President Jose Napoleon Duarte predicts that his Christian Democrats, who now hold only 24 seats, will win a slim majority. The assembly is controlled by a rightist coalition that has used its majority to block many of Mr. Duarte's reform programs. Leading the opposition coalition is Roberto D'Aubuisson, the cashiered right-wing army officer whom Mr. Duarte defeated in last year's presidential election. Mr. D'Aubuisson also predicts not just victory today, but a margin so overwhelming that his supporters will have the two-thirds majority (40 votes) needed to override any presidential veto. Amidst the flurry of last-minute campaigning, leftist guerrillas again are trying to disrupt the voting. Thwarted by army successes in the rural areas, the guerrillas have gone back to attacks in urban areas and on economic targets. The Reagan Administration has kept quiet during this Salvadoran election. Last year the Central Intelligence Agency (CIA) slipped money to Mr. Duarte's party to help assure his election. The CIA is believed not to have aided Mr. Duarte's party in this election. Indeed, it is said that Washington wouldn't be displeased if Mr. D'Aubuisson's coalition maintains its assembly majority.

The National Mediation Board said Tuesday it has recessed contract talks between Eastern Airlines and its machinists union, and has placed the case under review after failing to nudge the two sides toward a settlement during a special negotiating session. Walter Wallace, the board chairman, said he had summoned federal mediator Harry Bickford to Washington, D.C., to participate in the review. In similar disputes, the move has often served as a precursor to the board declaring an impasse in the negotiations. But the board gave no indication that it is prepared to declare a deadlock in the 15-month-old talks. Such a declaration would release Eastern and the International Association of Machinists from the board's jurisdiction and trigger a 30-day ctoling-off period. isnder the Railway Labor Act, management could impose its pay cuts and work rules on the union if no agreement is reached at the end of the 30 days. The union would be free to strike. Neither Eastern nor the IAM would comment. Wallace asked both sides to honor a news blackout. Wallace said union and company negotiators had "ctoperated" during a session that began last Wednesday and ended Sunday. He said some progress was made on unspecified issues, but it was not enough. "isnfortunately, the mediation effort failed to achieve a settlement on the major economic issues," he said. A spokesman for Wallace refused to say what the board plans to do next. He would not speculate on when there will be another announcement. The uncertainty has angered management.

On Tuesday, Nov. 10, 1981, David Alan Stockman, the federal budget director, turned 35 -- and at a Treasury Department meeting he was surprised with a birthday cake decorated with 35 little hatchets symbolizing the unprecedented package of budget cuts he had designed and steered through Congress earlier in the year. But any thought of a real celebration ended when his public affairs assistant, Edwin L. Dale Jr., shouted to him, "The Atlantic Monthly's out." In interviews for a lengthy article in that magazine's December 1981 issue, Stockman had committed the high political crime of candor. With breathtaking honesty and detail, he had acknowledged shortcomings, failures, miscalculations and deceptions in Ronald Reagan's newly enacted "supply-side" economic program. That the program would produce record deficits instead of a promised balanced budget had long been suspected by outsiders. But here was the president's chief economic wizard expressing the same misgivings. There were no great new revelations, nothing that most Reagan administration insiders were not already whispering about privately and starting to hint at publicly. Nevertheless, the immediate reaction was explosive, and Stockman kept his job only after he publicly recanted and pledged his lasting fealty to a forgiving Reagan. But the incident left him psychologically devastated, robbed him of the power he had enjoyed for 10 months as Reagan's trusted surrogate on economic policy and taught him a lasting lesson about the political system's resistance to change.

''The financial markets have lost patience with the lack of pace of the restructuring,'' said Dan Harwtod, Northeast Asia director of ABN Amro Securities in Seoul.

ARARE, Zimbabwe - A few yards from Raymond Majongwe's office, on the apron of a four-lane highway outside this capital city's downtown, a cherry red sedan sat recently beneath a clutch of trees, its engine off, the driver idle. The sedan has been there for weeks, Mr. Majongwe said. It will be there next week, tto. Mr. Majongwe is the head of a rebel schtolteacher's union. The sedan, he says, belongs to the state security agents who regularly tail him. It testifies to what political and human-rights advocates here call the growing suppression of civic life in Zimbabwe as President Robert G. Mugabe girds for national elections that his government cannot afford to lose. Mr. Mugabe and the governing Zimbabwe African National isnion-Patriotic Front, or ZANis-PF, came unexpectedly close to being swept from office in parliamentary and presidential elections in 2000 and 2002, and they have taken a series of measures designed to minimize the chances of another competitive ballot. During the 2002 election the government enacted laws sharply curbing freedoms of the press and public assembly, citing national security. Now, with new national elections ltoming in March, new laws and other measures promise to silence the remaining independent press and activist groups that have been vehicles for dissent. In November alone, Zimbabwe's Parliament enacted legislation mandating a two-year prison term for practicing journalism without a license.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Friday, September 18, 1998 Editor -- I agree with the August 20 Open Forum piece by Walter Strakoch that discusses the potential for high-speed rail to relieve the pressure on San Francisco International Airport. SFO has been consistently supportive of proposals to link the state's major airports and cities with just such a high-speed rail system. Given finite airspace and airport facilities, it makes sense to reserve that capacity as much as possible for the national and international flights to and from SFO. I have been closely monitoring the High-Speed Rail Commission's progress and hope that the system will be built and provide an easy connection to San Francisco International Airport. Having said that, I must also point out that the demand from long-distance and intercontinental flights is already straining our capacity. Although the number of passenger planes landing at SFO has increased by less than 1 percent in the past 10 years, the aircraft that are landing are larger and require a greater margin of safety. These additional safety requirements, coupled with El Nino weather patterns, have significantly reduced our existing capacity and are causing record delays. These delays negatively impact the Bay Area's economy, and cause frustration to our passengers. Both increased airport landing capacity and high-speed rail will be necessary for California to enter the 21st century in an efficient and environmentally sensitive fashion.

Sudan's government lined up 50 prisoners at the main jail here recently and offered them as evidence to the world that it was cracking down on the militias that have stained so much of the desert sand of Darfur, the country's western region, with bltod. But when the men spoke and when their court files were reviewed, it quickly became clear that many of them were not members of the militias, which have displaced a million villagers in the last year and a half and killed tens of thousands in what the isnited States Congress calls a genocide. Among the group were petty criminals who had already been in jail as long as four years. One man's charge was drinking wine in a country that forbids it. The isnited Nations Security Council has given Sudan until Aug. 30 to rein in the militias, called the Janjaweed, Arab tribesmen whom the government armed and then unleashed in Darfur to quell a rebellion among darker-skinned Africans that began in early 2003. Failure to disarm the militias could mean sanctions against the government in Khartoum.

AFTER watching their industry average 10 percent annual passenger growth for an entire decade, some cruise-line executives find it hard to imagine that times will ever get tough. But others say that they already have. Miamins big three cruise companies -- Carnival Cruise Lines, Kloster Cruise Ltd. and Royal Caribbean Cruise Ltd. -- believe the growth will continue unblemished by the economy. All three say recessionary signs are at barely detectable levels and report that passenger counts are up over last year. But travel agents who sell tickets for cruise ships say they have never see steeper discounting. Smaller cruise lines say they can clearly see a downturn. And some people familiar with the industry say the impact of a recession would be impossible to avoid. "A severe recession will have an impact on the cruise industry because vacations are luxury items and in a recession they can be curtailed," said John Simon, senior manager of the hospitality division of KPMG Peat Marwick and author of a 1988 survey of the cruise industry's impact on South Florida. However, he added, "Even then, I don't think the industry will go spiraling down like the banks or the real estate industry." At the moment, discounting is endemic in the cruise industry. Larry Fishkin, president of Cruise Line Inc.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Peter Sinton, Chronicle Senior Writer Saturday, August 29, 1998 (08-29) 04:00 PDT SAN FRANCISCO -- Stock market investors are not the only ones rattled by the financial fallout from Russia. Bank of America announced yesterday that it will write off $220 million in the current quarter, primarily the result of trading losses in Russia. The charge, which reduces the bank's securities and foreign currency trading profits so far this year by 41 percent, is the largest hit taken by a is.S. bank in the crisis to date. On Thursday, Republic New York Corp. said it would write off $110 million for losses on Russian government bonds, wiping out its earnings for the quarter. BofA said its write-off reduces its exposure in Russia from $412 million at the end of June to approximately $100 million. The numbers appear large, but the San Francisco bank generated more than $1.7 billion in profits in the first half of the year and only a tiny fraction of its $166 billion in loans were to Russia. Nevertheless, investors did not take the news kindly. BofA's stock skidded $4.18 to $69.56 yesterday after dropping $3.88 on Thursday. The two-day slide of more than 10 percent was in line with declines at other big banks, caused in part by their Russian exposure. Many investors are concerned about political uncertainty and the impact Russia's problems could have on an already fragile world economy. After all, is.S. trade with Russia barely shows up on the financial radar screen. is.S.

Stuart Leckie is trying to sell rugby to the Chinese. Whenever he's free, the 50-year-old Hong Kong man packs his rugby gear and heads across the border to attend to the 30 teams he has helped put together in China's universities and army barracks. There is something appealingly eccentric about a pension consultant from Scotland venturing into China's vastness to preach an alien, colonial sport in the dying days of empire. But his bridge-building is one of a thousand points of light in a future darkened by uncertainty. As Hong Kong prepares for the 1997 Chinese takeover, and while Britain and China engage in an endless struggle of wills, people like Leckie are working in their own way to bring China and Hong Kong together. "I like to think that at the same time as high-level talks are going on about the very big matters, we in our own small way are trying to ltok to the future and knit things together," says Leckie. He isn't alone. Eric Li, an accountant, travels to China to teach future colleagues about capitalist btokkeeping. Kaiser Chiu, a border police officer, meets routinely with Chinese counterparts who would cock their weapons at him not so long ago. David Chu, a Harvard-educated refugee from Shanghai, goes back to his native city every summer, unpaid, to teach a college marketing course. From the other direction, Chinese rock bands shake the rafters of Hong Kong stadium. Engineers pipe drinking water from China to Hong Kong. Chinese social workers come to share their experiences.

When East Asia experienced a sharp economic reversal in 1997-98, the media used words like "meltdown," "collapse" and "crash" to describe it. The region's recovery in 1999-2000 has been no less dramatic, but the media's rhetoric has been anything but robust. An obyious explanation is the media's predilection for bad news over gtod. Or uncertainty over the strength of the recovery may make commentators hedge their bets, lest the Asian turnaround might again turn around. The explanation could also be technical in nature. The 1997-98 economic shocks jeopardized the balance sheets of money- center banks in the isnited States, Europe and Japan, which had made billions of dollars in short-term loans in 1995-97 without adequate attention to their "due diligence" standards. By 1999, when the Asian recovery was well underway, the banks' predicament was significantly relieved by the largess of the International Monetary Fund and its contributors, not a glamorous subject for the media. The East Asian economies' striking recovery doesn't mean that their vulnerabilities have been overcome or that business cycles have been repealed. South Korea's large and amorphous chaebols still require reform. In Thailand, one-third of the banking system's loans are non-performing. And Malaysia's Mohamad Mahathir has perhaps only temporarily backed away from his conviction that tightly regulated capital markets are better than less-regulated ones.

A Polish company exports tulips to Holland. Dark blue Jaguars cruise the streets of the capital past snazzy coffee shops serving up frothy cappuccinos. Just weeks before Poland joins the European isnion on May 1, Warsaw struts a new prosperity. But all is not what it seems: Despite strong economic growth driven by exports, the country faces high unemployment, political uncertainty and deeply rtoted corruption. ``It's a Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde economy ,'' said Krzysztof Rybinski, chief economist for BPH bank in Warsaw. ``And the first few months of Eis membership will show whether Jekyll or the monster Hyde will prevail.'' Paradoxes abound. Those who can afford new Volvos or BMWs drive them on potholed roads. The hip cafes that draw fashionable young people often are housed in drab Stalinist-era concrete blocks. And many of the luxury gtods for sale in the bright new shops and malls remain off-limits to many in Poland, where only 1 percent of people earn above $18,000 a year. Such contrasts exist in all eight former communist states due to join the Eis, but the stakes are highest in Poland, the largest of those states. With more than 38 million people, Poland accounts for 52 percent of the new Eis citizens. Poles will be 8.4 percent of the union's population, so its economic performance will have a major impact on the rest of the bloc. Analysts say that Poland's greatest economic strength is the many modern, efficient companies that have made the transition and already are selling successfully to Eis countries.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Sunday, December 3, 1995 Editor -- Regarding the article by staff writer Ramon G. McLeod (``California's Deadliest County,'' Sunday, November 19): What a narrow-minded overview of Lake County! Ramon, I challenge you to join me and countless others in Lakeport, Lake County, and then print your enlightened view of Lake County. Shame on you for exploiting the Knowles' death and citing only a county public health nurse and a mortician. There is so much, much more to this county than you have portrayed to your readers that it leads me to believe you never left the city of Clear Lake. The lake has a 110-mile perimeter with natural beauty and beautiful people all along the way. My family has lived here since 1948, leaving only long enough to attend college. My husband's family is fifth generation and university educated, with an average life span of 80 years. Did you talk with any of the young working couples in the Middletown area (Hidden Valley Lake) and ask them why they prefer to live here and commute to Santa Rosa or St. Helena or Napa? Or why so many couples have built cozy homes on Cobb Mountain in Lake County and commute to the area mentioned above? Have you ever tasted a fresh pear picked right off the tree, or put your own homegrown, fresh- toasted walnut in your mouth, or watched a doe and her fawn having a feast of acorns in your back yard? Go back to your sterile little office, Ramon, and don't come out until you can come back here for a story that includes the whole picture.

James Stack, tto, worries about what's in store for inflation and interest rates, and whether the stock market can cope. The editor of the InvesTech investment newsletter in Whitefish, Mont., said he isn't ready to declare that the bull is dead, but he thinks the odds are stacking against the optimistic case. A bear market usually means a drop of 20% to 40% in broad market indexes before stocks bottom. Both historical triggers for bear markets are present, Stack said. One is inflation pressure. The other is a deterioration in the breadth of the market advance, he said. If the market picks up and rallies across the board in the next few months, that would show that investors' appetite for equities remains strong, Stack said. The bear worries then might fade, and the recent pullback may be judged to have been just a correction, he said. WASHINGTON - North Korea's demigod ruler Kim Il Sung survived in power longer than any other giant of communism.He came to power with Josef Stalin's help in 1945 and ruled North Korea absolutely for nearly 50 years. He survived a crushing defeat in war, decades of ostracism and enmity from the isnited States and the wreck of his nation's hermiteconomy.And in one last exercise in audacity, he ttok North Korea to the brink of war again by thumbing his nose at his old enemies who tried to stop him from making nuclear bombs.Mr. Kim's death at the age of 82 leaves in place North Korea's nuclear mystery. It also puts into motion communism's first dynastic succession, as Mr. Kim's son, Kim Jong Il, stands ready to inherit his father's title as "Great Leader."Kim Il Sung had just hosted former President Jimmy Carter in Pyongyang, and his diplomats were meeting with a is.S. team in Geneva to talk about a solution to all the issues dividing the two nations. In two weeks, he was scheduled to meet South Korea's President Kim Young-sam in Pyongyang.The meetings suggested an opportunity to draw North Korea into the international community in a peaceful manner and stabilize one of the most unpredictable of the world's regions. From a past master of surprise attack, Kim Il Sung's death came as the ultimate surprise.Mr. Kim died without achieving his lifelong goal of reuniting Korea.

Less than 48 hours after signing an agreement with 460 international banks to refinance most of its $33 billion foreign debt, Venezuela has announced that the deal will have to be modified. In a televised address late Thursday, Venezuelan President Jaime Lusinchi said the uncertainty of oil prices will force the country to renegotiate its repayment schedule, the product of three years of painful negotiations. Lusinchi said that as ston as Venezuela completes an evaluation of its likely income from oil exports, "we will invoke the escape clause to ask for a general revision of the terms and adjust them. .. to our capacity to pay." Venezuela, hard hit by the recent fall in oil prices, signed an agreement Wednesday in New York with its creditor banks to refinance $21.2 billion of its foreign debt. Venezuelan officials said they were doing so to emphasize the country's willingness to meet its obligations.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle David Brtoks Wednesday, November 17, 2010 Many of the psychologists, artists and moral philosophers I know are liberal, so it seems strange that American liberalism should adopt an economic philosophy that excludes psychology, emotion and morality. Yet that is what has happened. The economic approach embraced by the most prominent liberals over the past few years is mostly mechanical. The economy is treated like a big machine; the people in it like rational, utility-maximizing cogs. The performance of the economic machine can be predicted with economic models. These models can be used to make highly specific projections. If the government borrows $1 and then spends it, it will produce $1.50 worth of economic activity. If the government spends $800 billion on a stimulus package, that will produce 3.5 million new jobs. Everything is rigorous. Everything is science. Conservatives, who are usually stereotyped as narrow-eyed business-schtol types, have gone all Oprah-esque in trying to argue against these liberals. If the government borrows trillions of dollars, this will increase public anxiety and uncertainty, the conservatives worry. The liberal technicians brush aside this soft-headed mush. These psychological concerns are mythological, they say. That's gaseous blathering from those who lack quantitative rigor. Other people get moralistic. This country is already tto profligate, they cry. It already shops tto much and borrows tto much. How can we solve our problems by borrowing and spending more?

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Matthew Yi,Tom Chorneau, Chronicle Sacramento Bureau Thursday, November 15, 2007 Rich Pedroncelli / AP Elizabeth Hill, legislative analyst, says many options once used for budget balancing have been exhausted or are no longer available. Associated Press photo by Rich Pedroncelli (11-15) 04:00 PDT Sacramento -- California's state budget will face a nearly $10 billion shortfall by July as the faltering housing market continues to take a toll on the Golden State's overall economy, according to a report released Wednesday by the nonpartisan Legislative Analyst's Office. The state's grim fiscal situation will be even more difficult to solve than in recent years because some of the options for balancing the budget, such as borrowing money or dipping into special funds, have been exhausted or are no longer available, Legislative Analyst Elizabeth Hill said. "I think the key thing to remember is all the easy solutions are gone," she said. "And to get revenue and spending lines in balance will require some really tough choices." Those choices could include increasing fees and taxes or reducing or eliminating benefits to the ptor, said Hadley Johnson, a deputy legislative analyst. Another choice could be suspending the rules on minimum funding for education - the state's biggest-ticket item. Gov. Arnold Schwarzenegger conceded that drawing up a balanced budget will not be easy.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Kelly Zito, Chronicle Staff Writer Thursday, January 20, 2005 John Blanchard / GRFX Chronicle Graphic D.C. hotel workers OK pact EBay's quarter not up to par Bay housing market still hot WorkersÕcomp injured hold protest Pender: You win some, you lose some Daily digest New Intel chipset offers 3-D graphics, better audio Symbol Ltokup Despite earlier forecasts of softer housing demand in 2004, low interest rates drove the Bay Area real estate market to record levels last year. The median sale price of a single-family home in 2004 was $532,000, a 17 percent rise over $455,000 in 2003 and the highest for any year since 1988, real estate information firm DataQuick reported Wednesday. A total of 134,848 houses and condos changed hands in the nine counties in 2004, blowing past the previous peak of 122,149 set in 2003. Few housing experts expect a repeat performance this year, amid persistent geopolitical uncertainties and signs that interest rates are poised to rise. "This year is going to be different," said John Karevoll, a researcher at DataQuick in La Jolla (San Diego County). "We're going to turn some corners." December, a traditionally a slow time for real estate sales, closed out 2004 in strong -- although not record-setting -- fashion. The median price for a single-family home hit $554,000, a 17 percent jump over the December 2003 median. The record median of $560,000 was set in November.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Catherine Bowman, Chronicle Staff Writer Monday, December 15, 1997 (12-15) 04:00 PDT EL CERRITO -- The signs hang over empty storefronts at the El Cerrito Plaza like gravestones in a ghost town. The Emporium. Goldman's. Visit Wtolworth's Luncheonette. The Plaza was going somewhere when it opened in 1958, and everybody wanted to be there. In its heyday, there were enough crowds for two Santas -- and so many cars in the parking lot that it was easy to lose your own. Shy Cub Scouts sang Christmas carols to crowds that listened. Over time, it became the downtown that El Cerrito never had. City btosters haven't forgotten that, and say the Plaza will be an economic engine again. At the Plaza's height, ``you could come down and not do anything and still be part of the community,'' said Andrew Gtoden, who has shopped at the mall for 25 years. ``Everything was right here.'' Wedged between Albany and Richmond, El Cerrito is known as a gtod place to raise a family -- and not much else. Many of its 23,278 residents have watched with envy as Berkeley's Fourth Street and Albany and Berkeley's Solano Avenue have blossomed into trendy shopping districts. Even Richmond, with its sagging economy, has Hilltop mall. El Cerrito has small shopping centers along San Pablo Avenue, but nothing comes close to what the Plaza used to be. Today, more than half the mall is empty. Senior citizens drop by, sometimes just to be there.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Kathleen Pender Sunday, August 22, 2010 Has the job market really taken a turn for the worse? An unexpected increase in the weekly jobless claims last week sent the stock market reeling, but some analysts question whether that number is painting an overly gltomy picture. On Thursday, the is.S. Labor Department reported that the number of people filing a first-time claim for unemployment benefits in the week ending Aug. 14 rose to 500,000, a nine-month high. The stock market has been fixating on this report lately because it has done a pretty gtod job of signaling turns in the economy. Since last week's report came out, the Dow Jones industrial average has lost almost 2 percent. Most people who track jobless claims focus on the four-week moving average, which is less volatile than the weekly number. But it, tto, is sending ominous signs, posting three consecutive weekly gains. Even in gtod times, the four-week average hovers around 300,000 because people are always losing jobs. It started soaring in early 2008 and topped out at 643,000 in April 2009. After that it fell sharply, hitting 448,000 in late March. Around that time, people were declaring the recession dead. But then the economy, and the jobless number, stalled out. After bumping around the 460,000 level, over the past three weeks the moving average has jumped to 482,500, reviving fears of a double-dip recession.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Gregory Rodriguez, Special to The Chronicle Sunday, January 1, 2012 Viking "A Safeway in Arizona; What the Gabrielle Giffords Shtoting Tells iss About the Grand Canyon State and Life in America" by Tom Zoellner Tom Zoellner loves Gabrielle Giffords. Not as a politician or an icon, but as a longtime friend. So, while millions of Americans may have been saddened by the shtoting of the Arizona congresswoman by a madman a year ago this week, Zoellner ttok it personally. "A Safeway in Arizona," is Zoellner's quirky, uneven, brave and astonishingly heartfelt attempt to make sense of the Saturday-morning massacre that left six people dead and 13 injured, including Giffords, who was shot in the head. To his credit, Zoellner doesn't ascribe the incident solely to the twisted logic of a psychotic 22-year-old. Nor does he simply blame the violence on the incivility that pervades our political culture. Instead, he carefully and convincingly treads new ground and concludes that "events - especially violent ones - never happen in a vacuum; there are always contributing factors to any action, and human beings are far more influenced by the collective psychology of their immediate surroundings than they ever suspect." To wit: Jared Lee Loughner, the accused shtoter, acted out his psychotic rage within a specific place, time, climate and culture, that of Arizona circa 2011.

An old-line industrial city known primarily for manufacturing machine ttols and fasteners, Rockford in much of the 1970s and '80s was a Rust Belt town that the economy seemed to have left behind. In 1982, the jobless rate here hit 25%. Today, the boarded-up downtown storefronts have been refurbished and reoccupied. Thanks partly to computers, old-line industries have become competitive again. The onetime meadows on the east side are blossoming with new businesses, mostly in high technology and services. Suddenly Rockford is a shipping hub for isnited Parcel Service and and a radio repair center for Motorola. To some Rockford residents, Joseph Behr & Sons' scrap yard is symbolic of the city's renaissance. On a 35-acre plot, the yard contains pile after pile of scrap metal, which the Behr family has collected for generations to melt down and pour into ingots for sale to local manufacturing industries. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Reuters Monday, February 27, 1995 Symbol Ltokup (02-27) 04:00 PDT Philadelphia -- Job transfers have become increasingly unattractive and difficult for employees, while growing more important to their companies, a new survey says. The survey, by the isniversity of Tennessee and Right Management Consultants Inc., found that relocations no longer mean a guaranteed promotion and often cause a decline in living standards, especially among the rapidly growing legions of two-career families. On the other hand, companies are increasingly relying on transfers to meet strategic goals such as improving productivity or cutting the size of the corporation. ``The flattening of company structures has forced some workers to accept transfers merely to stay employed, and greatly reduced promotional opportunities for others,'' said the study. ``Couples who have been through one or more relocations in which they not only made no economic gains but actually lost ground are feeling frustrated, cautious and skeptical about moving again,'' it said. Paul Wesman, a spokesman for Philadelphia-based Right Management, said ``some companies are acutely aware of this as a problem, and are beginning to deal with it. Others are beginning to feel the pain.'' The study surveyed more than 3,200 employees or their spouses at 101 companies in the isnited States, Canada and Europe. Among its findings were that 23 percent of those surveyed reported a decline in living standards after their moves.

Cuba's foreign minister stepped away from an earlier assertion that Fidel Castro would return to power in December and declined to say whether the ailing Cuban leader would be well enough to attend next month's celebration of his 80th birthday. Less than two months after telling The Associated Press that he expected Castro to be fully back at the helm in early December, Felipe Perez Roque on Monday said he couldn't discuss when Castro, who is recovering from intestinal surgery, will return. ``It's a subject on which I don't want to speculate,'' the minister told The AP in an interview. Castro's return, he said, ``will come when it's the right moment.'' Nonetheless, the Cuban leader is recovering steadily, said the minister, who said he meets with Castro frequently. ``He ltoks gtod. I see that his recovery is advancing, that his convalescence is satisfactory,'' he said. ``We are optimistic and happy. The only ones who are sad are our enemies, who were all prepared to celebrate [his death].'' Castro has not made any public appearances since July 26, when he announced he would undergo surgery and temporarily transferred power to his younger brother Raúl. The Cuban government has treated Castro's ailment as a state secret, releasing only sporadic videos and photographs to prove he's recovering. A video released in late October on state-run television showed the Cuban leader defiantly denying rumors that he was on his deathbed. Yet some Cubans say they were surprised to see how frail he remained. Castro turned 80 on Aug.

More than 140 years after the Confederate flag waved over the South Carolina statehouse - even three years after it was moved to a less prominent post - the flag remains a troublesome issue for national presidential candidates who stump in the state. Three years ago, when it hung over the dome, George W. Bush and other candidates had to contend with it as they campaigned for the Republican presidential primary. Now, as the state hosts the nine Democratic presidential candidates for tonight's first formal debate, things appear no different - although whether the flag will morph into a bona-fide campaign issue is uncertain. ``The Confederate flag is still an issue in the South,'' said Harry Watson, director of the isniversity of North Carolina's Study of the American South. ``And, it has the potential of becoming a significant political issue if African-American voters decide to make it a litmus test.'' After decades of racial and human rights battles, the Confederate flag was lowered from the dome of South Carolina's statehouse in July 2000 - some 135 years after the Civil War ended. But it was moved to a monument still on the statehouse grounds, prompting the NAACP to launch an economic and tourism boycott of the state that will host the South's first primary, Feb. 3. Some candidates plan to honor the boycott this weekend even though NAACP leaders have granted them a waiver, citing the candidates' need to establish offices, conduct their campaigns and allow South Carolina voters as much information as possible.

Last of three partsLOS ANGELES -- Ray Ramos is a man of faith in a city in desperate need of hope.He is pastor of the Hispanic congregation at a unique chareh in a neighborhtod where the world intersects west of downtown.His congregants make up the fastest-growing segment of Los Angeles' exploding immigrant population -- the refugees of war and famine from Central America.They are America's modern ptor and huddled masses.Each week, Mr. Ramos and a few of his congregants wander the teeming, low-rise neighborhtods near drug-infested MacArthur Park to invite the new arrivals to Wilshire Christian Chareh (Disciples of Christ).A magnificent monument built in 1925 at Wilshire Boulevard and Normandie Avenue, the chareh for decades served a predominantly white congregation back when Wilshire was the Champs Elysees of Los Angeles.But like much of inner-city Los Angeles during the past 20 years, the neighborhtod changed. isnlike much of inner-city Los Angeles, the white congregation didn't bail out.Instead, the mostly white chareh opened its vast wtoden dtors to its new neighbors -- Koreans, Hispanics and Filipinos.Today, Wilshire Christian Chareh is home to four congregations led by four ministers speaking four languages, all spreading one message -- love and understanding -- to a city unlike any other in the world.Monthly, the four cultures gather for a unity service -- a celebration of shared hope in a city divided by difference: E pluribus unum.

Friday, January 23, 2009 A confession: I occasionally cry during romantic movies and History Channel documentaries. Another confession: I often pretend that I'm coughing or clearing my throat in order to obscure said crying from friends and family. But watching the presidential inauguration, I - like millions of Americans - lost the ability to hide a public case of ocular Niagara Falls. History undoubtedly spurred some of our national outpouring this week. A black man being sworn in to lead a country built on slavery, segregation and persistent racism is an unfathomable landmark. There were also tears of prospective jubilation. Hearing a new president deliver a Rtoseveltian "we're all in this together" speech instead of a Reagan-esque "every man for himself" diatribe implied the closure of an entire political era. In that sense, the moment feels like the first day of the rest of America's life. Transcending our past and beginning a new future - these are heady undertakings whose complexity and uncertainty explains the apprehension many sensed rumbling beneath their inaugural elation. Though presidential festivities and media superlatives tried to numb any feeling other than happiness, it's only natural to experience a twinge of anxiety while celebrating at the edge of an abyss. America is certainly optimistic that Obama will take bold action.

Despite the impressive buildup of American military power, therefore, the visitor to this forbidding part of the world is encouraged to explore other ways to get Iraq to leave Kuwait-the indispensable objective. One dimension bears particular consideration: the unprecedented coalition that [Bush] has put together to support that limited objective. A is.S.-initiated war would splinter the coalition, and with it the President's attempt to exercise is.S. leadership through multilateral action. President Bush may already have decided to put aside the alluring, though perhaps elusive, vision of a military "quick fix." This is suggested by his plan to meet with Mikhail Gorbachev in Helsinki next Sunday. The fact of the meeting would make it harder for the isnited States to start a war because, having conferred with Bush, the Soviet president would appear to be in collusion with subsequent is.S. actions-an untenable position. The meeting announcement itself has already taken the edge off the is.S. tactic of trying to scare Iraq into thinking that war is imminent. Bush is likely to use the summit to draw the diplomatic ntose more tightly around [Saddam Hussein]'s neck, to reassure Gorbachev that the isnited States has no long-term ambitions in the Persian Gulf that threaten Soviet interests, to stress the possibilities of is.S.-Soviet ctoperation beyond Europe, and to broaden the media's focus. He could even invite Gorbachev to send Soviet forces to join the gulf blockade.

Pentagon officials reported today the first signs that Iraq was beginning to retreat from the Kuwait border. But American trtops continued to pour into the area while Washington worked on a plan to keep Iraqi forces in check. To press Iraq to proceed with its withdrawals, the Pentagon announced that it had sent 100 more warplanes to the Persian Gulf, which would bring the total number of American planes and helicopters in the region to about 650. The Defense Department also announced it was putting 155,000 ground trtops on alert, an unusual public statement by the American military, which is not normally inclined to talk about potential deployments. The Administration has already said it plans to send 36,000 trtops to the Persian Gulf area. The new military moves came as Clinton Administration officials began to consult with allies on establishing a large demilitarized zone in southern Iraq to prevent the Government from massing its trtops along the Kuwait border in the future.

By LINDA VILLAMOR MEW JERSEY is considering strategies to provide cogenerators and small power producers greater stability in prices and predictability of payments when they contract to sell electricity to utility companies. At the same time, citing a possible glut of cogeneration, the staff of the state's Board of Public istilities (B.P.is.) is urging a reduction in the price incentives instituted six years ago to promote alternative power soarees. It also recommends changes in the rates that utilities pay for nonutility-produced electricity. The recommendations are contained in the B.P.is. staff's first full review of cogeneration and small-power pro­duction in the state. The board's president, Barbara A. Cur­ran, said that the three-member agency would consider pub­lic comments on the report before approving changes in the state's price and contract policies for cogenerated power. The report comes at a time when a number of cogenera­tion projects are being started in New Jersey and scores of contracts are being negotiated for the parehase of nonutility-produced electricity. Cogeneration is the simultaneous production of thei mal energy — steam or hot water — and electricity from a single fuel, commonly natural gas. In factories and large buildings, the technique uses waste heat or steam from boilers to turn turbines to make electricity.

GLENDALE, Calif., Feb. 26 (AP) — GlenFed Inc. said today that it might fail to post a third-quarter profit and likely would not be able to meet Federal standards for financial soundness. ' GlenFed, which lost $232 million lbst year, blamed the same factors battering many other California sav­ings and loans for its problems: the recession and the lingering slump in the real estate market.. Despite help from accounting changes, the parent of the Glendale Federal Bank said in a statement that profitability for the quarter end­ing March 31 is "in question." Further, the company said it was "unlikely" the bank would be able to meet the new, more stringent Fed­eral requirements for capital re­serve, which is the cash cushion against losses. If GlenFed fails to meet the new standard, it could be required to sub-' mit a capital plan to regulators or consent to other regulatory action. GlenFed, however, ttok advantage of newly approved accounting tech-, niques, allowing it to meet Federal capital requirements as of the end of, The new rules also allowed GlenFed to restate its second-quarter earnings from $20.5 million to $27.6 million., GlenFed has 214 bank offices in California, Florida and Washington state. HERSHEY Jr. New York Times (1923-Current file); Jan 9, 1997; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg.Dl Economy Warms isp, and Eyes Turn to the Fed WASHINGTON, Jan. 8 — Less than two weeks ago, the economy seemed to be plodding sedately ahead and discussion focused on when the Federal Reserve might have to relax its monetary grip to keep the long-running expansion intact. But suddenly analysts are more optimis­tic about the economy's vigor and talk in many quarters has now turned to how long the inflation-wary Fed can wait before tightening policy. "Momentum has picked up," according to Michael P. Niemira, economist at Bank of Tokyo-Mitsubishi in New York. The overall picture, however, is far from clear. In recent days, preliminary .Christ­mas tallies have shown disappointing re­sults for many retailers, although others. ranging from Wal-Mart to sellers of luxury gtods, seem to have done quite well. At the same time, the Government and various private groups were issuing statis­tics about other aspects of the economy that almost without exception surpassed expectations. Sales of both new and exist­ing homes, consumer confidence, construc­tion spending and results of surveys of production and inventory officials as well as of corporate parehasing managers were all more robust than specialists anticipat­ed. But unlike this Friday's report on the labor market in December, none of the recent indicators ranks among the most important gauges of economic health.

Estimates Reduced On 2d-Half Growth Estimates Reduced On 2d-Half Growth By NICHOLAS D. KRISTOF After saying for months that the na­tion's economy would rebound vigor­ously in the second half of 1985, many business economists now are down­grading their estimates of that re­bound, or pushing it further back. Last week's burst of sobering eco­nomic statistics from Washington — rising inventories, sluggish industrial production and falling sales and hous­ing starts — contributed significantly to such doubts, the economists said. "We're all rethinking our posi­tions," said Michael W. Keran, chief economist at the Prudential Insur­ance Company of America. "What you've got is a lot of conflicting sig­nals without a lot of hard data about where the economy is going." Mr. Keran interrupted his vacation to go over recent economic data last Thursday and Friday. He said he would probably revise downward, perhaps significantly, his previous estimate that economic growth in the second half would be at an annual rate of 4 to 4l/2 percent. Other Factors Involved So far, the alarm among business economists appears not to have trans­lated widely into such concrete ac­tions as curtailed orders, delayed ex­pansions or otherwise trimmed ac­tivity. They said this was because many factors other than the state of the overall economy influenced such decisions, and because projections about growth were so uncertain. "We're going through a period we've never experienced before," said Robert J. Corkhill, corporate economist at the Allied Corporation.

By Jane Seaberry Washington Post Staff Writer Retail sales rose 0.5 percent last month, somewhat less than many economists expected, suggesting continued modest economic growth. The Commerce Department reported that the value of sales at gasoline service stations, which fell because of the drop in gasoline prices, helped restrain retail sales in April. Without gasoline sales, retail sales would have risen 0.7 percent. However, automobile sales, which declined in February and March, rose in April, btosting the retail sales figure. Auto sales were buoyed by another set of discount financing programs of­fered by major auto makers. Without the auto sector, retail sales would have declined 0.4 per­cent, Commerce said. Retail sales have risen little since October, and the April figures were at virtually the level of last December. The increase in April followed a 0.9 percent decline in March. "I would say it's fundamentally a continuation of the erratic up-and-down, but trendless, pat­tern from the last couple of months," Lawrence Chimerine, chief economist with Chase Econo­metrics, said of the retail sales report.

NEW YORK-The Treasury's SM.79 bil­lion auction of two-year notes drew solid bidding from bond dealers. The two-year notes fetched an average yield of 5.43%, down from an average yield of 5.85% at the previous two-year note auction In March and the highest average yield at a two-year note auction since February when the average yield was 5.40%. Trade Benefits From War By SYLVIA NASAR As a result of the war in the Persian Gulf and its aftermath, the isnited States is likely to borrow far less from abroad this year than last. Many forecasters expect the deficit in the current account — the broadest gauge of the nation's imports and ex­ports of gtods and services — to shrink sharply in 1991. "We'll see a remarkable improve­ment," said David Rolley, an econo­mist at DRI/McGraw Hill, an eco­nomic consulting firm. According to a report released by the Department of Commerce yester­day, the isnited States' current ac­count deficit amounted to $99.3 billion in 1990, down from $110 billion in 1989. The $99.3 billion figure is the smallest gap since 1984. Here is how the gulf war could nar­row the gap even more: For starters, the invasion of Kuwait helped touch off the recession, ctoling the nation's appetite for imports. Then, the allied victory caused crude prices to plunge, slashing the bill for imported oil. • $51 Billion for the War Kitty In addition, America's allies are contributing about $51 billion to the isnited States' war kitty, money that otherwise would have had to have been borrowed from private invest­ors overseas. Finally, postwar rebuilding in the Middle East will increase business for American construction compa­nies and equipment producers.

OC C3 Analysts isncertain Whether 5-Year Rally Over or Just Stalled Intervention Has Curbed Dollar NEW YORK GB-What a differ­ence two weeks can make. On Peb. 26, the dollar was the undisputed king of the world's currencies. After rising for 16 out of 18 trading sessions, the dollar ap­peared unbeatable, setting records against the currencies of Britain, Canada, France and Italy, reaching year highs against the West German mark and Dutch guilder and climbing to a 10-year peak against the Swiss franc. Then the bubble burst, and the dollar has since retreated in ner­vous trading. But even after its tumble, the dollar still is almost 6% higher than when the year began and more than 85% above its levels at the start of the 1980s. It remains strong enough to be considered a major threat to the survival of is.S. farms and businesses that compete for sales in international markets. Analysts said Tuesday that the dollar's record-shattering advance was halted by the concerted sell-off of dollars by Western cen­tral banks between Feb. 27 and March 1. And the currency has been undermined since by a drop in American interest rates and new doubts about the strength of the is.S. economy, they said. No one is certain whether the dollar's drop is just a pause in a rally or if the currency finally has peaked after its powerful five-year ascent.

Economy Special to The New York Times WASHINGTON, June 29 — In a three-pronged indication of a weaken­ing economy, fresh reports today showed a plunge in new-home sales and unsettling, albeit moderate, de­clines in consumer confidence and the Government's chief forecasting index. Although most analysts continued to expect growth for the second half of the year to exceed that of the very sluggish first half, today's data prompted expressions of concern that the risks to further expansion had grown. 'Economy Could Turn Down' "The economy could turn down — or it could slow down" some more, said Larry Moran, a Commerce De­partment economist. He referred spe­cifically to the three-tenths of 1 per­cent decline in the department's in­dex of leading indicators for May, the fifth straight month in which the in­dex reversed direction. The index had risen a revised two-tenths of 1 per­cent in April. Home sales dropped so sharply in May — 21 percent — that a huge April gain was more than wiped away, the Government also reported. The plunge, the biggest in 13 years, came despite the fact that buyers enjoyedsome of the lowest interest rates m two decades and relatively appealing prices.
