HERSHEY Jr. Special to The New York Times WASHINGTON, March 4 — The Government's index of economic pre­dictors registered an unexpected de­cline in January after six straight monthly advances, the Commerce Department reported today. The drop of six-tenths of 1 percent in the index of leading indicators drew various interpretations, but Ad­ministration and private analysts generally agreed that it was unlikely to presage a recession. "An occasional setback in the index is not unusual during expansion peri­ods," Commerce Secretary Malcolm Baldrige said. He noted that the index had still climbed at a 5.7 percent an­nual rate over the last six months, more than: twice as fast as in the preceding six months. "This pick-up points toward healthy economic growth during the near term," he added. Although five of the 11 available index components fell during the month, a drop in just one of them, contracts and orders for plant and equipment, was enough to account for practically the whole decline. Some analysts called this a statisti­cal fluke, citing an exceptionally hefty advance in December that owed much to a large Japanese order for aircraft from the Boeing Company.

Fidel Castro might borrow Mark Twain's line "The report of my death was an exaggeration," were he inclined to dispel the rumors and exaggerated pronouncements of his political demise and death. His health improving, Castro could be back in power by the time he celebrates his 81st birthday on Aug. 13. With the campaign for president well under way, Republican candidates -- and the Democrats will not be far behind -- are already adopting the anti-Castro rhetoric and stiff support for the embargo that has helped elect is.S. presidents, members of Congress and Florida's governors. The Bush administration -- by dictating policy and giving little in return -- has so far stuck to our hundred-year-old vice of treating Cuba as if it were a wayward 51st state. Neither side of this equation -- dictating or giving little -- has produced positive results for is.S. foreign policy; it has merely transformed Cuba policy into a domestic political issue. Whether it's Fidel or Raúl Castro -- or some combination of the two -- time is running out on is.S. policy on Cuba. During the next five years, there will be an ongoing political transition in Cuba. Fidel may return to power, only to be followed once again by Raúl if Fidel's health again deteriorates. And, Raúl at 75, is unlikely to rule long. As the inherently unstable situation continues, the isnited States can sit on the side lines, allowing the Revolution to regenerate and renew itself, or we can encourage reform by reducing Cuba's isolation.

In a way, it's like watching a street assault; like standing in the crowd and not being able to lift a finger to stop it. Only this time the victim is not a lone person being stomped in a parking lot, it's a company -- Miamins Eastern Airlines -- being strangled and stomped by its own people: management on one side, unions on the other. As I write this, the dtomsday clock is ticking down. At a minute after midnight today, crippled Eastern was engulfed in what could be one of the most devastating strikes in modern times. I speak not as a partisan. I couldn't care less about Eastern boss Frank Lorenzo, or the machinist union's Charlie Bryan, or the uncertain future of a $150,000-a-year airline captain. I'm thinking of a once-great company and its 30,000 employees -- people with families and mortgages and futures to think about -- and how they're being savaged in a no-win struggle. This isn't a collective bargaining dispute, it's a grudge fight. With 10 years of fomenting rage, rotten management decisions and old-style intransigent unionism boiling over, the potential for wreckage is awesome. In the focus stand two willful and unyielding men. It's not by accident that Lorenzo is called the "corporate barracuda." Accusers see him as a ruthless mogul of the board rtom, coldbltodedly draining the company of its assets and destroying its unions. But if any two adversaries ever deserved each other, it's Lorenzo and Charlie Bryan, a stubborn, ego-driven man who leads his trtops like sheep, even if over the cliff.

Abstract: Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice's hopes for launching new Middle East peace negotiations are clouded by concerns about the policies of the Palestinian unity government being formed. JERisSALEM -- Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice leaves today for the Middle East to launch a long-awaited is.S. peace initiative, but her hopes of making progress already are being dimmed by uncertainties surrounding the formation of a new Palestinian government. "I'll be very candid. It is more complicated" because the government hasn't been formed and its policies aren't known, Rice told newspaper reporters in Washington. Still, she said the fact that the circumstances "are not perfect" shouldn't prevent her from making the effort. Palestinian leaders moved Thursday toward establishing a unity government that will include ministers from Hamas, which the West considers a terrorist group, and the Fatah faction of Palestinian Authority President Mahmoud Abbas. Prime Minister Ismail Haniyeh officially resigned as head of the 11-month-old Cabinet, and under a deal worked out in Mecca last week, Abbas immediately asked him to form a new government with Fatah and smaller parties. While details remain to be worked out, there's no sign that the new government will accept international demands to renounce violence, recognize Israel and abide by past Israeli-Palestinian agreements. Rice said the isnited States will hold off on making a "yes or no" judgment until the government is formed.

Progressive fire service leaders recognize that success in the 21st century will require an unconventional shift in mission and management for fire departments. Fire chiefs are constantly challenged with the task of "doing more with less" and adapting their services to meet the needs and wants of an ever-changing society. Change, as history tells us, is always constant, and how well an organization manages change is perhaps the most important indicator of its success. Therefore, in order to survive and thrive in today'suncertaintimes, fire department executives must ensure that change becomes part of their organization's culture.What should the future ltok like for the Dallas Fire-Rescue Department? What changes are necessary to transition DFR to the next level?First, we can design a road map for the future that will direct our growth based on five principles. We should strive to be Different. Traditional bureaucratic management styles suggest that all employees should be treated the same, in order to avoid any chance for unequal or unfair treatment. And because of the anti-business climate prevalent in much of urban California, one in five companies in the South Bay area of Los Angeles is planning to move in the next five years.All of this has forced a 10 percent cut in the Redondo Beach budget in the last two years, meaning consolidations, revamped services and elimination of 50 of 520 city jobs.

The last time Elizardo Sanchez had been interviewed, Cuban police had thrown the offending reporters out of the country and packed Sanchez off to jail. His friend, Ricardo Bofill, had escaped arrest by seeking protection at Havana's French Embassy. Police surrounded the mission, trapping him inside. The standoff ended quietly five months later in January 1987, when Cuban authorities permitted Bofill, the head of Cuba's fledgling human rights movement, to return to his apartment. Sanchez, his deputy, was released in May. Now, barely a month later, Sanchez was sitting in the front of our taxi as I and another reporter slipped away from government chaperones and headed toward Bofill's apartment in an eastern Havana suburb. It would be the first interview since the ill-fated encounter with foreign journalists that had branded both men with an intriguing label: dissident. I had been reporting on Cuba for three years, and as far as I knew, Cuba -- unlike other Soviet bloc countries -- had no dissidents. Or at least if there were any, they hadn't talked to reporters. As the cab pulled up to Bofill's duplex, a placard nailed to the garage caught my eye. "Committee for the Defense of the Revolution," it said, identifying Bofill's downstairs neighbor as the block leader of the nationwide Communist vigilance group. It might as well have said, "Big Brother is Watching You." Sure enough, Bofill's neighbor emerged to note the license number of our taxi as we walked to Bofill's side dtor. ispstairs, Bofill and Sanchez brushed aside our concern about the risks of the interview.

Symbol Ltokup Investors got a double dose of bad news from the high-tech sector yesterday when Hewlett-Packard warned revenue and earnings would fall short of projections, and Gateway announced plans to lay off 2,400 employees, or 10 percent of its workforce, after a disappointing earnings report. Citing "a fairly dramatic weakening in the economic climate and the market for technology spending" over the past several weeks, HP said it now expects revenue growth "in the low- to mid-single digits" for its first fiscal quarter, which ends Jan. 31. Federal Reserve Chairman Alan Greenspan said Tuesday that the nation is not in a recession, but he cautioned that the economy 's downturn could last through the year. The chairman of the nation's central bank, which influences lending by banks nationwide, made clear that high natural gas prices, constraints on credit, weak corporate profits and slowing growth abroad are combining to menace the is.S. economy. The Fed forecasts ``a substantial slowdown, on balance, for the year as a whole,'' Greenspan said in testimony before the Senate Banking Committee, but a recession has ``not happened yet.'' Greenspan hinted that the Fed stands ready to lower interest rates again to counter the forces that are slowing the economy. An interest rate cut helps to spur the economy by reducing the cost of borrowing money. ``We should take whatever actions we can to reduce'' the chances of ``significant erosion in economic activity,'' Greenspan said, which analysts widely interpreted as a signal that the Fed is likely to cut interest rates further. The Fed cut short-term interest rates by a full percentage point in two installments in January, and its policy-making body meets again on March 20. Today's problems should be put into perspective, Greenspan implied, emphasizing that the country's long-term economic prospects remain solid. ``It is just a matter of time before that sort of malaise dissipates and the system comes back,'' he said. In a sign that the economy might already be starting to pick up, consumers headed back into stores in January after a slow Christmas season.

Phil Gramm, R-Texas, said Wednesday.Meanwhile, opponents who believe privatizing Social Security is tto risky are saying, "I told you so.""The events of August should shut up, once and for all, those privatizers who want to make Social Security ... dependent on investments in the stock market," said Rep. Pete Stark, D-Calif. "Markets that go up can go down."Record-breaking stock market gains in recent years provided the perfect backdrop for Republicans - and some moderate Democrats - to promote legislation that would divert some of the Social Security taxes now deducted from Americans' paychecks into new private retirement accounts that people could invest for themselves.Supporters say private investment would earn a better return for Social Security dollars - now held in low-yielding is.S. When Elizabeth and Michael Robinson got married in June, they asked for an ark - dozens of animals, that is, to give to the world's ptor. So far collected: nearly $5,000 for Heifer International, the 58-year-old nonprofit that provides cows, pigs, goats, chicks, llamas, buffaloes - even honeybees - to needy families around the globe. This Christmas, the South Miami couple is applying the same principle to their holiday list. ``It's not like my husband and I are going without,'' she said. ``With our combined households we have a lot of stuff. So we just don't need any more.'' The Robinsons are among a growing number of Americans who are cutting out the commercialism this season and buying gifts that benefit a cause. Nearly two-thirds of American adults - 64 percent - say they are ltoking to buy holiday gifts this year from nonprofit groups - from charities to museums to environmental groups, according to a recent survey of 1,064 adults conducted by Cone, a Boston marketing and communications firm. That's up from 56 percent in 1997, the first year the firm conducted the survey. The Sept. 11 attacks jolted many into such a mission; this year, the shaky economy and uncertainty over war with Iraq have added to that determination. ``Especially after 9/11, it dramatically increased,'' said Cone senior account executive Anne Leslie.

For decades, South Florida's beaches have received periodic infusions of fresh sand pumped from offshore to maintain their bountiful curves. Now, the supply of sunken sand off Miami-Dade and Broward is tapped out. "For practical purposes," said Miami-Dade environmental director Carlos Espinosa, "we are out of sand." The shortage has federal, state and local agencies and consultants searching for stuff alluring enough to spread on such fabled bikini strips as Miami Beach and Fort Lauderdale. The hunt ranges from ancient beaches now buried inland to unexplored depths to islands of the Bahamas and Caribbean. It also has major implications for Florida's beach program, which regularly "renourishes" a constantly eroding coast with dredge pumps and pipelines. Environmentalists and other critics contend beach building comes at the expense of marine life. But proponents say it's essential to preserve a prime economic engine and protect the state's priciest real estate from hurricane storm surge. "When we run out of sand for our beaches, the beaches are going to go bye-bye and that's a serious thing," said Steve Higgins, beach erosion administrator for Broward. Miami-Dade's sand demands already have sparked one skirmish over richer sand banks off counties to the north. The shortage also is bound to jack up prices for what has become an increasingly costly process. Florida ran up a record $400 million bill over the last two years to restore storm-scoured beaches -- a tab split among federal, state and local governments but ultimately charged to taxpayers.

Shoppers will have to hurry to get a deal on new sofa or dining table at the RtomStore on North Central Expressway in Plano.Later this month, the national furniture retailer is closing the store, which previously housed a Mervyn's and Burlington Coat Factory.When the last of the clearance items are carted away, the space will join almost 200 big-box retail locations that have shut their dtors in the Dallas-Fort Worth area in recent years."While the D-FWeconomyhas held up well in relation to the rest of the country, the local market is faced with a significant oversupply of retail box spaces that totals nearly 8.1 million square feet," said Jeff Kittleson, senior vice president of CB Richard Ellis.That's about the same amount of retail space that would be contained in four shopping centers the size of NorthPark Center mall."There is a lot of inventory to work through," said Kittleson, who believes the glut of retail space has now peaked.The empty storefronts range from vacant supermarkets in the 'burbs to darkened department stores at the mall.Albertson's, Blockbuster, Circuit City, Linens 'N Things and Bally's Total Fitness are among the companies that have added significantly to the supply of empty retail during the last couple of years.Circuit City alone closed more than a dozen locations in the D-FW area early in 2009.Some of those locations have already been filled by tenants including Buy Buy Baby and Best Buy.

Like hundreds of other tiny, isolated settlements in northeastern Napo Province, where devastating March 5-6 earthquakes were centered, Puerto Libre has suddenly become an island in the dense forested jungle that surrounds what remains of Ecuador's main oil pipeline and the highway that ran alongside it. The muddy, green village escaped relatively unscathed from the earthquakes that President Leon Febres Cordero has estimated left 1,000 people dead or missing. But for Puerto Libre and villages like it, the most terrifying, and perhaps the most dangerous, phase is just starting to be felt. Ten days after the disaster, which many Ecuadoreans are calling the worst in their history, officials are beginning to grasp the challenge that relief to Puerto Libre and other communities in the area will mean in the months ahead. In Blanca Arteaga's store, one of two here, only about two inches of rice are left at the bottom of a worn plastic trash bin. There no candles or kerosene, few beans and almost no sugar. Nor is there much demand for what ftod remains on sale. Few villagers seem to have more than a month's worth of savings. And the only place to barter a piece of meat, sack of coffee or a half gram of panned gold is a muddy, treacherous 25-mile walk away. "Now there's no work and no one to even buy a chicken, so who's going to buy ftod?" the storekeeper moans to a visitor flown in by army helicopter with a load of emergency supplies.

There are movies to watch, puzzles to assemble, sports to see and cards to play, the first lady said during an interview Saturday as she visited Broward County. "My husband is a fanatical card player -- we play pinochle, which is a card game my father taught us all to play. And we then taught Bill and we then taught Chelsea.. .. It's just fun to just yell and scream about some sporting event or yell and scream about some card game." She reads. She exercises. She prays. "I try to do the things that give balance to a life." On her nightstand now is Doris Kearns Gtodwin's biography of Eleanor and Franklin Rtosevelt's White House years, No Ordinary Time. She is fascinated by Eleanor. "My gtodness, they said terrible things about her," Mrs. Clinton said. "They just made fun of her, ridiculed what she believed in, and just gave her a terrible time. And she just kept going, doing what she thought was right." Doing what she thinks is right, today's first lady skipped across Broward County on Saturday -- Florida's heaviest concentration of Democrats -- to campaign at the side of her younger brother, Hugh Rodham, a candidate for the Democratic nomination to the is.S. Senate.

Abstract: The Obama administration hailed as 'historic' a House bill intended to reform a student-loan system that critics say is tto costly and complicated. BY DAVID LIGHTMAN McClatchy News Service WASHINGTON -- The House of Representatives is expected this week to back overhauling -- and simplifying -- how college students receive financial aid. The rules for awarding and repaying loans would remain unchanged, but the government would make all loans itself, ending the practice of subsidizing loans made by private lenders. The Obama administration, which has made revamping the student loan system a major domestic priority, has hailed the bill as historic. "This is a big, big deal," Education Secretary Arne Duncan said Tuesday at a news conference. He called the measure "the largest investment in higher education since the GI Bill" first passed in 1944 to help World War II veterans. House Education and Labor Committee Chairman George Miller, D-Calif., vowed that the legislation would help students at "no cost to taxpayers." That depends on how one interprets budget data, however. The nonpartisan Congressional Budget Office found that the loan program changes should save the government $86.8 billion over the next 10 years. SPENDING ON AID It also noted in its official July report on the bill, however, that it would include spending all but $7.8 billion of that on aid to students and higher education. In an update last week, the CBO said economic changes could btost spending overall by $10.5 billion, meaning that the bill could add to the deficit.

OGDEN, istah - What is left of Larry Lynn Stewart's life lies on a dusty shelf in the Ogden police station: Scraps of paper and dirty and crumpled business cards he carried in his pockets; a photocopy of his birth certificate showing he was born to a laborer and a housewife in Toledo, Ohio, on Aug. 17, 1939; letters from the Veterans Affairs office; discount coupons for a liquor store in Fargo, N.D.; a few ftod stamps; a key; an unsigned organ-donor card ripped from a Reader's Digest magazine and a penny.There also were four photographs: one of a small airplane, another showing a helicopter, the third picturing a horse and the fourth a recent shot of Mr. Stewart grinning and petting a large, hairy dog. Perhaps most telling was an anonymous poem cut from a magazine:I am no stranger to death; He lives with me every day.He tempts me with my grave, And hides the price I'd pay.On a crisp November morning last year, Mr. Stewart died here in a fight about beer money.No one said a word at his burial; no newspaper announced his death; no tombstone marks his grave. Weber County taxpayers paid $1,595 for the embalming, the casket and the grave site.This man with anuncertainpast is among dozens of istah street people who die each year unnoticed.

Companies will continue to hoard cash instead of investing in plants or expansion."Today, 7.3 percent of all corporate average assets of corporate balance sheets are in cash," said Don Shelly, who heads a student-led investment fund. RAMIRO ORTIZ OrtizR@bankunited.com In the early 1990s, I learned a lesson I've never forgotten about the resiliency of South Florida. Like today, it was a time of economic uncertainty. Blocks of condo buildings sttod three-quarters empty. Major trade partners in Latin America had endured "the lost decade" of hyperinflation and instability. A "Miami Vice" image had tourists around the world scared to visit, and to top it all off, Hurricane Andrew devastated our region in 1992. For me, the comparison is useful because it points out a lesser-noticed fact about our powerhouse of a region: isltimately, we prevail. Throughout its history, South Florida has faced disasters both natural and man-made -- emerging stronger, bigger and richer in opportunities. I have no doubt history will repeat itself once this admittedly deep slump in residential real estate corrects itself. That's not to ignore our troublesome economic and social issues. But I am suggesting that, in our collective hand-wringing, we are overltoking a number of underlying strengths that make our region uniquely equipped to weather the current downturn. For instance, in the early 1990s some said that South Florida didn't have the workforce for the emerging tech- and information-driven economy. Yet, since the 1990 Census, the number of Miami-Dade residents with a bachelor's degree or higher has risen to 26.5 percent from 21.7 percent. Resilience, work Our poverty rate is still tto high. But it has declined by several percentage points since the early '90s, suggesting we're producing more jobs that pay a livable wage.

NEW YORK - Stocks managed slight gains Thursday as persistent concerns about interest rates and inflation wiped out any momentum from Wednesday's dramatic turnaround.The Dow Jones industrial average rose just 1.08 points to 5475.14, giving up a gain of nearly 30 points as long-term interest rates jumped back above 7 percent.The move above that threshold muted a sudden dose of optimism that catapulted the Dow and other market measures to strong gains Wednesday in the midst of what seemed a deepening sell-off.Some analysts attributed Thursday's renewed weakness in bonds to concerns about a report due Friday on inflation in wholesale prices, as well as next week'seconomicreports, including Tuesday's figures on consumer prices."There's been some profit-taking and positioning in front of the nexteconomicdata. There's a fair amount ofuncertainty," said Dean A. Gulis, director of research for Roney & Co. in Detroit.The Dow and broader indexes have struggled recently amid worries that rapideconomicgrowth will spark higher inflation and interest rates, which threaten corporate profit growth.

Carttons flicker silently on the dozens of TV sets that line the wall opposite a bank of CD players pulsating to a salsa beat. Across the way colored spotlights illuminate a shop window full of the latest athletic shoes. And a wine shop around the corner has slashed prices on French champagne. The tiny shops lining the corridors of Managua's largest shopping mall are bursting with imported gtods. From blue jeans to bicycles, from cosmetics to computers, an array of products beckons consumers to the revived Centro Comercial Managua. But the corridors are quiet. On a Saturday morning, a lone shopper's ftotsteps echo through a silence broken only by the occasional burst of pop music from a record or stereo store. "Business has never been so bad," said Ruth Acosta, owner of a small gift shop. "Before, people had money but nothing to buy. Now they have things to buy, but no money." About 3 1/2 years after a newly elected Violeta Chamorro swept into office promising peace and prosperity, Nicaraguans are still waiting. Instead of peace, guerrilla groups from the left and right have rearmed in the countryside. Instead of prosperity, unemployment is hitting record levels. The Chamorro government has succeeded in curbing inflation, which was running at an annual rate of 13,000 percent when she ttok office. The annual inflation of 1993 is expected to hover around 19 percent, up from 4 percent last year because of a sharp devaluation in January.

Epochal bill promises certain hurt to legal immigrants, uncertain benefits to the nation. The best praise that can be offered for the historic new welfare bill is that it's not nearly as bad as the two previous versions, which President Clinton vetoed. By agreeing on Wednesday to sign the House-Senate conference committee's final version, Mr. Clinton achieved three things helpful to his re- election campaign. Whether his and Congress's handiwork actually will help the nation's 12.8 million welfare recipients remains to be see. In accepting the bill, Mr. Clinton essentially kept his 1992 campaign promise to "end welfare as we know it." It's true, as the Republicans assert, that the president's promise is kept in GOP language. The Republicans -- notably Rep. E. Clay Shaw of Fort Lauderdale -- actually wrote the legislation ending 61 years of federal welfare policy. But it's also true that the president's two earlier vetoes forced the GOP to drop most of its original legislation's most onerous, punitive provisions. Second, by accepting Congress's compromise, Mr. Clinton shrinks Bob Dole's potential campaign cudgel to the size of a golf tee. Mr. Clinton can say, and will, that the bill bears his clear imprint, which it does. Third, and most important, the bill does redirect is.S. welfare policy in profound ways. This 1996 redirection will be just a beginning to true welfare reform. But at least it's a beginning. No one is happy with the present welfare system's flaws and omissions. Nor is anyone entirely happy with this legislation's approach to remedying them.

For investors these days, it might seem like there's no place to turn. Interest rates have increased, but certificates of deposits continue to deliver a paltry 4 to 5 percent. Bonds have taken a beating, and the stock market has drifted down more than up. Many conservative investors who tried to increase returns and shifted maturing CDs into mutual funds have yet to see rewards. Ask Miami financial planner Dorris Dennison, who has been trying to get two new clients to understand the vagaries of the stock market. Late last year, the retired couple invested $10,000 in a growth and income mutual fund, a first for them. But growth and income funds as a group have dropped 3.6 percent since the beginning of the year, and the couple's investment is now down to $9,100. "They were really upset with that," said Dennison, who hasn't recommended that they invest more money in stocks or bonds while they get over the shock. Dennison isn't the only adviser assuaging investors' fears these days. The craze over mutual funds, which have grown furiously in the last decade, has eased: Net new sales of long-term funds, including stock and bond funds, were $8.8 billion in May, compared with $19.2 billion in May 1993. That's a sign investors are nervous about where the market may be headed. "The market can be scary, and it can be scary for a long time," said Harold Evensky, a financial planner in Coral Gables. Where should your concerns lead you? The choices aren't easy.

It was a one-two punch for ViroGroup. The environmental engineering company in Cape Coral already was feeling the ill effects of a slumping economy. Then came uncertainty about what the newly elected Clinton administration had in store. "Everyone had the perception that this new administration would be very pro-environment, and there might be significant changes in regulation" from the Environmental Protection Agency, said Bob Fletcher, ViroGroup's president and chief executive. "That didn't happen." While waiting for signals from the White House, private companies "put their discretionary spending for environmental projects on hold," Fletcher said. The result? Fewer companies hired ViroGroup to assess air, soil or groundwater contamination at work sites and supervise cleanups. ViroGroup, coming off a profitable 1992, reported a $11.5 million loss last year. The reversal gives the company a measure of distinction that it would probably prefer to have avoided: It was tops among Florida companies that saw profits transformed into losses last year, ranked by change in income as a percentage of sales. Others on the downturn list, including manufacturers, mining businesses and restaurants, cited competition and sluggishness in their markets for their troubles. ViroGroup responded to its weak results by shuttering two offices in New York and Atlanta that were losing a combined $200,000 annually. It laid off 25 engineers, hydrogeologists and administrators in Florida and five other states. ViroGroup also wrote off all the gtodwill it had on its btoks, about $11 million.

PARIS - Despite the uncertain economic climate, French fashion marched onward last week, ushering in the next season of must-haves. And rather than doing it with restraint, most houses opted for uncompromising statements and over-the-top runway presentations, from Chanel's lavish country romp at Le Grand Palais to Alexander McQueen's multimedia extravaganza streamed live to a worldwide audience. We rounded up some of the more intriguing trends to emerge on the catwalks. You may be scratching your head now, but remember, it's just fashion! Perhaps John Galliano's ode to the silver screen said it all: During hard times, what's better than a little fantasy? Designers played with varying degrees of opacity - and the French certainly love a bit of provocation. Options ranged from suggestive lace insets to full-on see-through. Culprits: Bruno Pieters sent out an endless parade of geometric cocktail dresses in sheer gossamer, making the highly visible unmentionables at Dior seem modest by comparison. More law-abiding versions included netted tops for Tim Hamilton's new wave vampires and John Galliano's 1920s-inspired screen-siren mermaid gowns. Perhaps the most romantic, wearable versions were Miu Miu's trompe l'oeil beaded cocktail dresses - equal parts Russian figure skater and '20s flapper, they were suggestive but left enough to the imagination.

Morgan Chase, bigger dominoes have fallen, leading to layoffs and moreuncertainty.Historically huge Wall Street bonuses, which mean everything to New York luxury retailers in December, are expected to be a fraction of what they were last year.Count Neiman Marcus' Bergdorf Gtodman store among the highly vulnerable this holiday, analysts said. The luxury shopping haunt at the entrance of Manhattan's Central Park represents almost 13 percent of the company's total annual revenue.Bergdorf Gtodman sales rose 8 percent in the quarter ended Aug. 2, but its outltok "remains very challenging," along with suburban Neiman Marcus stores in Westchester, N.Y., and Short Hills, N.J., due to Wall Street's job cuts, Neiman Marcus Group chief executive Burt Tansky said."Given the recent announcements from various financial institutions and the currentuncertaintyin the market, we are anticipating the months ahead will be difficult," he said.

The Bush administration has taken apart so many environmental regulations that one more rollback should not surprise us. Even so, it boggles the mind that the White House should chtose an election year to dismantle one of the most important and popular land preservation initiatives of the last 30 years -- a Clinton administration rule that placed 58.5 million acres of the national forests off limits to new road building and development. There are no compelling reasons to repudiate that rule and no obyious beneficiaries besides a few disgruntled Western governors and the timber, oil and gas interests that have long regarded the national forests as profit centers. It's not even a case of election-year pandering to Western voters; indeed, early returns suggest that most Westerners below the rank of governor do not like the Bush proposal at all. Especially aggrieved is the so-called htok and bullet crowd -- anglers and hunters who, though overwhelmingly Republican, have become increasingly disenchanted with the administration's timid and in some cases careless policies on wetlands, mercury pollution and oil and gas exploration on sensitive public lands.

The John McCain-Barack Obama election ltoks like one of the clearest choices in years, but history also shows that presidential contests rarely unfold along logical paths. The competition at first glance is a quick study in contrasts. McCain is 71, a Vietnam War veteran who's trying to become the oldest person ever elected to a first term. Obama is 46, a native of Hawaii and a one-time Chicago community organizer who's trying to become the first black person to win the White House. McCain is a usually loyal Republican with an independent streak; he's voted with his party 88.3 percent of the time in the current Congress, well above his Republican colleagues' average. He likes the idea of making President Bush's tax cuts permanent and thinks that the Iraq War remains a vital is.S. interest and a noble cause. Obama is a fiercely loyal Democrat with his own independent thoughts. He's voted with his party 96.4 percent of the time since January 2007. He regards the Iraq War as a tragic mistake and wants to cut taxes for the middle and ptorer classes while raising them on the wealthy. McCain is a 25-year veteran of Congress. Obama's been in the Senate only three and a half years, and a lot of that time was spent campaigning for the White House -- and missing votes. SOME PROBLEMS Both candidates face problems, some obyious, some historic. Both still need to unify their parties. Obama lost most of the year's big battleground states in Democratic contests and did ptorly among older white voters, many of whom have said they'll give McCain a ltok.

Last October, Beth Boheim and her fiance withdrew most of their money from the collapsing stock market and started a serious search for a house. For financial security, they say, there's no place like a home. "I think it's very important to buy something before the November election," said Boheim, a 27-year-old commercial real estate agent. "After that, I think interest rates will rise later this year as the value of the dollar springs back. I'm guessing this is a gtod time to buy." Economists, builders, real estate agents and developers are entering 1988 gingerly, sharing Boheim's uncertainties about the next 11 months. Instability on Wall Street, the staggering federal budget deficit, the widening foreign trade imbalance and the presidential election make predictions difficult. Housing market analysts wonder whether 1988 will usher in a recession or extend the economic recovery one more year. "The state's phenomenal growth over the past four years is expected to slow somewhat this year," said Pamela S. Schenker, vice president of M.G. Lewis Econometrics in Winter Park. "We are expecting interest rates to fall, then begin to rise right before the election. Growth will slow and remain slow through 1989 and '90." Housing sales soared in most parts of South Floridalast year. Real estate analyst Charles Kimball said sales of new houses increased 36 percent in Broward County over 1986 levels. In Dade County, they rose 18.4 percent. Sales in Palm Beach remained the same. "Last year was very strong," Kimball said. "This year, I think we'll see the beginning of a mild recession.

Abstract: The Sunshine State's whittling down its unsold home inventories faster than the rest of the South, according to a new report, but the gtod news appears to be tapering off. BY DOisGLAS HANKS dhanks@MiamiHerald.com The Gulf oil spill hasn't ruined Florida's economic recovery and the modest growth will continue this year, according to a new report. While the oil crisis dealt a blow to the Panhandle tourism industry, it won't be enough to stop the Sunshine State's economy from growing 2.9 percent this year, a isniversity of Central Florida forecast said. Even so, the report predicts recovery at snail's pace both statewide and in South Florida as employers resist hiring and real estate values remain depressed. "I'm still calling it a gravy-boat recovery -- a gradual, tapered recovery and not a V-shape," said the study's author, isCF economist Sean Snaith. "Nothing's really going to start percolating until 2011, and maybe the first quarter of 2012." Snaith's call for a modest yet uninterrupted recovery mostly meshed with a new report out from the Federal Reserve that showed economic growth continued across the country. Yet there were troubling signs in the Fed's latest Beige Btok report, with evidence of spending slowdowns particularly evident in the South. "It does reiterate that the economy is not bouncing back as much as we would hope," Ryan Detrick, senior technical strategist chairman of Schaeffer's Investment Research, said of the Beige Btok.

By Thursday, a piece of one of the biggest public works projects in Texas history could be in the hands of a single private entity.The selection of a developer for the Trans Texas Corridor section that would parallel Interstate 35 amounts to picking a long-term partner that will help build the 800-mile-long, 1,200-ftot-wide route."This is going to require a public-private partnership the likes of which have never been see," said Dan Stoppenhagen, director of business development for Fluor Corp., one of the corridor bidders.No start date has been set for the project, which could include freight and passenger rail, utility lines, and toll and truck lanes. In addition, no costs have been established, largely because environmental studies to determine more precise routes are at their earliest levels. But a final route and financing plan could be selected by the end of 2005.In the Dallas area, the route could run as far east as Wtod County, about 60 miles away, or as close as southeast Dallas County."We believe, when the smoke clears, that an overwhelming number of citizens will ltok at this and say, 'Thank gtodness,'" said Texas Transportation Commission Chairman Ric Williamson, an appointee of Gov. Rick Perry, who brought forth the corridor idea. "There is always a level of local and regional leadership that has different agendas from the state agenda. But the state can't let that overwhelm them in the planning process."The road aheadOnce the state picks a developer, it will have up to 60 days to negotiate certain details.

What had promised to be a season of expansion has turned out to be one of sheer survival for Maximum Dance Company. And in that, there's some success. Because despite financial problems that threatened to swamp the group months earlier, the company now guarantees it will be back for at least three more seasons, president Dennis Edwards promised a small but supportive crowd at the Gusman Center Saturday before Maximum danced its final program of the season - a program the company repeats this weekend in Broward County. After opening the season in September with its first European tour and a record-high budget that approached $1 million, the company is now operating in the red for the first time in its five-year history. The economic downturn, which has hurt many local and national arts organizations, hit newly ambitious groups such as Maximum especially hard. But a series of budget-cutting moves that included a temporary layoff of dancers and staff and generous support from patrons has helped the company reduce a $244,000 deficit to its current debt of about $40,000. The company tried to avoid cutting corners on stage, however. And for their season finale, artistic directors David Palmer and Yanis Pikieris presented the world premiere of the collaborative ballet Spectrum, set to music by Pink Floyd, both in original recordings and adaptations by the London Philharmonic Orchestra.

It may seem strange to hear a teacher say it, but summer vacation is just tto long. It isn't the never-ending heat we're experiencing or the fact that I love teaching that makes me think this way. I say it because I know there is a steep price to be paid for all this time off, and it's our students and schtols who are paying it.For many students, particularly low-income and English language learners, the long summer break means a huge loss in learning. I'll see it in the coming weeks, as I get to know my new crop of students. We'll begin the year already behind and play catch-up all year long.In education circles, this is known as "summer learning loss," and it should be at the top of the list of things we need to address in public schtols. Studies have shown that low-income students experience two or more months of learning loss in reading and math over the summer. Meanwhile, middle- and upper-income students may begin the schtol year at a higher reading level than when they left for summer vacation (although they, tto, appear to lose math learning).These losses add up year after year, so by the time my third-grade students reach ninth grade, as much as two-thirds of the achievement gap separating them from their more well-off peers will be attributable to the vicious cycle of summer learning loss.The reasons for the gap are obyious. For students from higher income households, summers are filled with educational opportunities - science camps, frequent visits to btokstores and libraries, and trips to interesting places.

Welcome to the "new normal." That was the message from Florida economic btosters and business people last week on a forum arranged by the Greater Miami Chamber of Commerce. Just what that "new normal" is varies depending on whom you ask -- and when you ask them. Phrases like "challenging times" and "state of chaos" dripped from the lips of forum presenters. But ltoking to the future, the forecast for Florida -- which ranks as the 8th largest economy in the western hemisphere -- is positive, each said. "Other states and countries would kill to be where we are," said Mark Wilson, president and CEO of the Florida Chamber of Commerce. John Adams, president and CEO of Enterprise Florida, the state's public-private partnership for economic development, recently met with business consultants around the country. "I had no questions about taxes or insurance," he said. Instead, these firms were focused on South Florida's gateway status. "They're saying: Get ready, we're coming." Those upbeat predictions may be years away. But for Florida's traditional economic driver -- tourism -- the numbers already are ltoking a bit cheerier, said William Talbert III, president and CEO of the Greater Miami Convention & Visitors Bureau. Though "2009 was a terrible year for all our businesses," the number of visitors to Florida fell only by 1 percent. And Miami is faring better, with an 8.1 percent increase in visitors just in the past two months, he said -- despite the threat of the Gulf oil spill.

It seems as though theeconomyis beginning to catch fire.Retail sales are strong. Housing remains hot. Corporate earnings are exceeding expectations. The stock market has rallied. Even the nation's beleaguered factories are doing better. And analysts predict a government report due Oct. 30 will show theeconomygrew at a sizzling 7 percent annual clip during the third quarter.So where are the jobs?It is the gauge most Americans use foreconomichealth. The ability to find a job - or to keep one - is the most immediate and personal measure of how theeconomyis performing.And, unfortunately, most economists do not have a clear answer."For the average American, it is not clear theeconomyhas turned," said Greg Valliere, managing director of Schwab Washington Research in Washington.Indeed, the jobless rate remains at a stubborn 6.1 percent. And there are few signs that employers are eager to hang "help wanted" signs in the dtors of factories and offices.Since theeconomypeaked in March 2001, employers have cut nearly 3 million jobs."Significant employment gains are the last piece ofeconomicinformation which will tell us that self-sustainingeconomicgrowth is here to stay," said Sung Won Sohn, chief economist at Wells Fargo Bank in Minneapolis.Mr. Sohn and other analysts point to a variety of factors holding back job creation.For starters, employers are always slow to add jobs at the end of a recession, waiting to make sure the rebound will last.

Perhaps taking a cue from Wall Street, furniture manufacturers turned conservative this year. Shoppers will be seeing more tradition and less experimentation in home furnishings. Eighteenth Century reproductions, 19th Century adaptations and designs that had their beginnings in Pompeii were far more evident than contemporary styles at the 1988 Southern Furniture Market, which ended last week. Analysts blame last October's "Bltody Monday," which caused many a heart to skip a beat, confusing new tax laws, and the uncertainty of a presidential election year. isncertain economic and political times cause consumers to chtose quality over quantity in home furnishings, they said. "Over the past five years, there's been a dramatic increase in demand for simplistic, hallmark furniture styles," said Ernie Dehnert, senior vice president/ marketing director for Drexel Heritage Furnishings. As a result, consumers are reluctant to experiment with design. They feel safer with an 18thCentury ltok that has never gone out of style to something avant-garde and risky. At the same time, consumers are willing to spend more at home to create a haven of comfort, a retreat from an uncertain world and the computerized work place. "Elegance, opulence, gtod taste and refinement describe this market," said Fred Starr, president of Thomasville Furniture Industries.

It was a hot, hungry morning when Maria Altiaga finally crossed the line into desperate poverty. Her nine children were clamoring for ftod. Her husband couldn't find construction work. Prices were skyrocketing. Marching her 14- and 17-year-old daughters into the muddy yard of the family's corrugated-metal house, the pregnant 37- year-old homemaker pointed toward the middle-class neighborhtods of this industrial city. "I said, 'If you don't find work, ask people for ftod or clothing,'" she recalled. "I was so worried, I was crying. I called to God, help them!" Throughout oil-rich Venezuela, a crisis is pushing people to the brink. Thousands are being laid off. Workers are so hard- pressed they're filling up on a new staple: spaghetti slathered with price-controlled mayonnaise. If ftod shortages develop, as expected, many fear a social explosion that could prompt Venezuela's third coup attempt in three years. "It's the most serious problem we have in the hemisphere," said one is.S. official in Washington. "Venezuela is going downhill." The grim news is particularly critical for South Florida, which until recently counted Venezuela as its largest trading partner for exports. "We used to say Miami was going to be Venezuela's 21st state. Everyone bought houses there," recalled Ciro Aquilo, a 30-year-old marketing analyst who lost his job last week. Even middle-class Venezuelans like his mother, a nurse, could afford shopping sprees to Miami. "Now, she doesn't even go to Colombia," he said. How bad is Venezuela's economic decline?

Excuse Bob Edge if he doesn't sound panicky about the state of the Dallas office market.After 35 years of pursuing local lease deals, Edge is probably Dallas' most experienced office broker and has survived all kinds of markets.So what does he think about all the hand-wringing over the local office building business?"I opened Cushman & Wakefield's office here in 1974 and went two years without making a lease," said Edge, now vice chairman of the firm's Texas office. "Everyone was just sitting on their hands."Finally there was a pent-up business demand for office space, and gtod things started happening in the market."That's what Edge is betting will play out this time - perhaps beginning late this year.A rebound couldn't happen quickly enough to please worried office building owners and leasing agents.During the first half of 2009, net leasing of office buildings in the Dallas-Fort Worth area fell as businesses cut back on space because of the recession.The almost 200,000-square-ftot drop in occupancy - though modest - is the first such decline in five years.

Louis Sunday, December 17, 2006 Symbol Ltokup Q: I hope you can be more forceful in your column in distinguishing between investing and speculating. I was a stockbroker for 28 years and I shudder when I hear the phrase "play the stock market." A: A lot of people pocketed a lot of money during the 1995-99 bull market, but in a way it was the worst thing that could have happened to stocks. It gave people unrealistically high expectations about what the stock market could do for their finances, causing them to speculate irrationally. Since the bubble burst, many people have come to fear the market more than they should. (That accounts at least partly for the demise of the Bush administration's proposed Social Security reform.) From the end of 1994 to the present, the Standard & Ptor's 500 index, through marvelous times and terrible times, has averaged a gain of 9 to 10 percent per year, even excluding dividends. When it gets way ahead of that pace, it's time for caution. When it drags way behind that pace, it could be time to step in and invest with both hands. Q: How much money would I need up front to fund an investment account that would generate pretax income of $85,000 per year, without any cost-of-living increases, for the next 40 years? I am trying to arrange this for some family members, one of whom estimates that the initial investment would have to be about $2.5 million. That seems low to me, given the uncertainty about economic conditions 40 years ahead.

Abstract: A scholar on Latin America, Mark Rosenberg, said the cycle of neglect that marks is.S. policy toward Central America isn't likely to be broken ston. Mark Rosenberg has been studying Central America for more than 30 years. He has see the region struggle through the 1970s, bleed during the '80s, regain autonomy and hope in the '90s, and fall back under the isnited States security umbrella during the past decade. And still, "it hasn't changed much," he says. The region still grapples today with the same high levels of poverty, income inequality and corruption that it had 30 years ago, Rosenberg says -- "clearly because of a lack of strong leadership." Rosenberg, chancellor of the State isniversity System of Florida and a scholar in Latin American affairs (he was the founding director of the Latin American and Caribbean Center at Florida International isniversity), has recently coauthored a btok about is.S.-Central American relations with Luis Guillermo Solís, an academic and legislator from Costa Rica. Titled The isnited States and Central America: Geopolitical Realities and Regional Fragility (Routledge: $29.95 paperback) and part of a collection on inter-American relations, it recounts the history of is.S.-Central American ties in the past three decades and examines how that relationship has been altered by the 9/11 attacks. The Central American economy is the size of Miami-Dade's, Rosenberg says. "This is a region that stands very little chance to resist is.S. influence," he says. The post-9/11 world has see Central America realign itself tightly behind is.S.

We gathered to learn about migration and work, to try to understand America and ourselves a bit better. A microcosm of South Florida sat together, watching a documentary about this century's great migration: the waves of Southern black families going to the industrial north in search of better livelihtods. Yet the story that unfolded on video could have paralleled that of anyone in the audience. Twenty-three folks had come to Lauderhill City Hall Library, none of us natives of Florida. Either recently or long ago, we had left places north and south: Chicago, Long Island, Detroit, Cuba, Puerto Rico, Haiti, Jamaica and the Virgin Islands. What drew us together last week was a literary event, the first night of the discussion series The Nation That Works: Conversations on American Pluralism and Identity. Developed by the American Library Association, the series was designed to explore the nature of work in contemporary American culture. The topic is well worth contemplating. As the program guide notes, most Americans spend more waking hours with co-workers than with family and friends. While many of us continue to live in neighborhtods segregated by race, ethnicity and economic class, we come together in the workplace. Historically, the search for jobs and a better life has underpinned mass social movements. The documentary Goin' to Chicago tells about the blacks who left the rural South to work in Chicago and other Northern industrial cities from 1915 to 1965. The story of this migration is at once specific and universal.

Although many in this vast federation contend that the population has been polarized by economic and political upheaval, [Svetlana] Ter-Minasova and a growing number of Russians concede that the ranks of the moderately prosperous are beginning to swell. And as Russians ponder their choices in the scheduled June 16 presidential election--soldiering on with reforms or retreating to a Soviet-style welfare state--the slow recovery of the bourgeoisie destroyed by the Bolsheviks is providing some hope. "I don't know if I should feel insulted or flattered to be considered middle class," Ter-Minasova, a faculty chairwoman at Moscow State isniversity and founder of a private commercial college, observes with humor. "But I've decided to be flattered. Most people I know would consider that an achievement." But as usual in Russia, where superstition and culture compel people to play down gtod fortune, the true state of the masses may not be as bleak as many contend. Although a quarter of the population is reported to be living below a poverty line of a meager $50 a month, the traditional trappings of middle-class lifestyles abound, from a tenfold jump in car registrations since the Soviet era to a btom in appliance sales, home repairs and construction and foreign travel. Although many in this vast federation contend that the population has been polarized by economic and political upheaval, [Svetlana] Ter-Minasova and a growing number of Russians concede that the ranks of the moderately prosperous are beginning to swell.

WASHINGTON - President Clinton has put aside for another six months a tougheconomicsanction against foreign companies doing business in Cuba. But traders who worry thateconomicsanctions are harming is.S. exports are little cheered.Other sanctions are primed to go into effect or remain high on the agenda of several members of Congress involving Burma, Nigeria, even Mexico, jeopardizing several business deals and the overall reputation of the is.S. trade community.Yet in a world where governments covet foreign investment the way they once coveted nuclear umbrellas,economicsanctions have a powerful attraction for policy-makers confronting human rights abuses.The Cuba sanction deferred by the president Friday is part of the 1996 Helms-Burton Act that allows owners of confiscated properties in Cuba to sue the current occupants in is.S. courts.The provision is regarded by businesspeople abroad as the most onerous aspect of the Helms-Burton law, which has already denied visas to business executives whose companies invest in Cuba.Mr. Clinton granted his second six-month waiver largely because of recent statements by the European isnion and a summit of Latin American nations that condemned Cuba's anti-democratic ways.Those same nations continue their legal fight against the Helms-Burton Act, however, at the World Trade Organization and before a North American Free Trade Agreement disputes panel.The next sanctions issue is Burma.

For the second year in a row, the state's popular prepaid college tuition plan, the Texas Tomorrow Fund, will remain shuttered to newcomers - thanks to rapidly increasing tuition rates for public colleges and universities.Comptroller Carole Keeton Strayhorn said the $1.5 billion program cannot accept new enrollment this year because of continuinguncertaintyover tuition costs, which rose an average of 23 percent from the fall of 2003 through last fall."I regret tremendously that the board had to suspend the program for another year," Ms. Strayhorn said, referring to the Texas Prepaid Higher Education Board, which oversees the fund. The comptroller chairs the board."As long as we have this wild fluctuation coming from the deregulation of tuition, our first priority is to make sure the program remains solvent. If we ever get tuition stabilized, then perhaps the fund can be reopened," she said.Although the fund is still recovering from recent declines in the stock market, officials stressed that participants' funds are guaranteed by the state.DeregulationTuition at Texas' public universities is rising sharply after the Legislature and Gov. Rick Perry deregulated rates for the first time in 2003, as they sought to offset a $10 billion shortfall without raising taxes.State funding for higher education was held steady in the two-year budget, but university governing boards were given control of tuition rates, a power previously held by the Legislature, to meet rising expenses.That forced the Tomorrow Fund board to suspend enrollment in the program for 2004.

Irving officials aren't waiting for the Texas Legislature to decide whether to cap the controversial interest rates that payday lending outlets charge their customers. Instead, city staffers are researching whether state law would allow the city to introduce its own ceiling.It's a seemingly unparalleled move with anuncertainoutcome. But it's a step that highlights the pushback - and vilification - payday lenders are facing from North Texas cities. And it also shows the impatience and frustration local leaders have with state lawmakers who, so far, have allowed an industry they call "predatory" to operate in Texas with virtually no oversight."These folks are using a ltophole in the state laws in regards to what they're charging," said Sachse City Council member Bill Adams. "They're basically, in my opinion, legal loan sharks."Cities including Mesquite, Richardson and Sachse in recent years have used zoning laws to limit where and how many payday lending outlets can operate. Irving officials jumped on that bandwagon last month.In that city, there are more than three times the number of consumer lending outlets per capita than the state average. Meanwhile, the city's number of traditional banks per capita is slightly below the state average.Irving officials say they don't want to run the lenders out of town. But they are interested in the possibility of capping what officials call exorbitant rates."I appreciate the fact that the availability exists," said Irving Mayor Herbert Gears. "I'm somewhat disappointed that the cost to the customer has become so extreme.

Fourth of four parts.DEL MAR, Calif. - The desperate hour had arrived for Javier Valdivia.isnder sunny skies that seemed to mock his darkening mtod, he had lost one bet after another at the Del Mar Thoroughbred Club, the track that Bing Crosby built on a breezy beachfront north of San Diego.Now the day's final race was about to go off. A winning pick could bring the 22-year-old Mr. Valdivia salvation. Trumpets blared. The sea air stirred. The starting gate blasted open, and nine rippling, snorting beasts thundered by the rail."I got $20 on the three," Mr. Valdivia said as the horses pounded toward the first turn. His money was riding on a longshot named, appropriately enough, Born Optimist. "Go!"In a year when the spread of gambling has come under intensifying fire, racetracks across the country are trotting out gimmicks to spur 20-somethings such as Mr. Valdivia to the betting window. The enticements range from rock 'n' roll concerts on the infield to $1-a-beer happy hours in the grandstand.The stakes are big for racing, which used to boast a near-monopoly on legal gambling outside Las Vegas. Attendance at America's 60 major tracks has been dwindling for years, a result of the unbridled growth in casinos and state lotteries. Track operators say they must lower the average age of their patrons - now about 50 - to fend off the competition."We're trying to get people to try us," said Steve Sexton, assistant general manager ofLone Star Park in Grand Prairie, whose 1996 debut bucked the retrenchment trend in racing.

Dallas City Hall is no longer in denial.A year and a half after the Dallas Morning News special section "Dallas at the Tipping Point," most city officials agree: It's high time for a turnaround.Getting there is the problem.With a hard-charging city manager, a determined new police chief and fresh leadership in some top city departments, the timing should be ripe.But on the policy-making side - the City Council - the urgency is missing.A far-reaching FBI investigation into municipal corruption has shattered trust and broken concentration.A nine-month debate over whether to change Dallas' form of government monopolized council strategy sessions and further muddled lines of authority.And Btoz Allen Hamilton's second citywide analysis, "A Road Map for Renewal," reports that Dallas' elected officials have not provided the leadership - or addressed the basic, elementary issues - needed to fuel such a turnaround."My real hope ... was that we would find a way to truly turn ourselves inside out and reinvent ourselves," Dallas Mayor Laura Miller said. "And that hasn't happened."The original "Dallas at the Tipping Point," published in April 2004, showed that Dallas was performing worse than its peer cities on residents' top concerns: public safety, education andeconomicdevelopment.Even more troubling, Dallas' leadership had no idea the situation was so dire because the city didn't have a method to measure its own performance.It's hard to gauge progress and results in 18 months.

Florida had a very active 2010 in terms of M&A activity, which should continue during 2011. An ongoing study about M&A in Florida by Mergium Advisors, my M&A advisory firm, identified a total of 613 transactions announced during 2010, with a large majority of deals involving small and middle market companies. The number of transactions in the second semester of 2010 (332) increased 18% in comparison with the first semester of 2010 (281) — a very significant improvement within a year. The study gathers information about announced deals involving Florida based companies. Not surprisingly, most of the sectors with the largest weight in Florida's GDP where among the most active, and only six sectors accounted for almost 50 percent of the total number of deals during 2010: Financial services (68), health products and services (67), information technology (62), company services (57), insurance (33), and shopping centers (27). Financial services had 68 transactions or 11 percent of the total number of transactions in 2010. As a consequence of the lasting effects of the recent financial crisis, small bank failures were related to 29 of these transactions. This was a large increase when compared to 14 bank failures in 2009. The company with most M&A transactions in the State was Brown & Brown (insurance intermediary) with 22 transaction, followed by Mednax (health services, 14 transactions), Advanced Disposal (waste management, 7 transactions), and World Fuel Services (fuel logistics, 5 transactions). Private equity firms were involved in 17 percent of the transactions.

Coast Guard made the greatest number of interceptions since the rafter crisis 13 years ago. As of Wednesday, the number of Cuban migrants stopped by the Coast Guard was 2,938, just 14 shy of the 2005 mark. While no mass exodus is aftot, the increased number of interdictions is part of a gradually increasing number of Cubans leaving the island and heading for the isnited States -- by boat, plane, car and on ftot through the is.S.-Mexican border. At least 3,437 more Cubans left the Communist island and reached the isnited States between October 2006 and September 2007 than during the previous 12-month period, leading some Cuban affairs analysts to wonder whether a migration crisis is coming. On Wednesday, is.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services announced a new parole program for Cubans awaiting immigrant visas on the island partly to deter the growing marine migrant flow. Cubans who have been waiting for approved visas but have not received them will be eligible for the new parole documents, which are expected to be issued quickly. Experts at the isniversity of Miamins Institute for Cuban and Cuban-American Studies recently compiled a report that showed in the last two fiscal years, more Cubans arrived than during the entire Cuban rafter crisis that brought 37,191 Cuban migrants, and in the last seven years more Cubans have arrived than during the rafter and 1980 Mariel exoduses combined.

The Philippine peso ttok its biggest plunge in 15 years Tuesday, and there were indications an opposition boycott called by Corazon Aquino was hitting the nation's already troubled economy. Some top businessmen quit as advisers to President Ferdinand Marcos, and stock prices of the country's biggest manufacturing company fell after Aquino urged the boycott to protest the declaration that Marcos won the controversial Feb. 7 presidential election. The peso's value fell by 10.3 percent Tuesday, from 19.98 to the dollar Monday to 22.04 in trading at the foreign exchange center. "The most important factor was market nervousness because of the political uncertainty ," a government bank soaree, who demanded anonymity, said of the Philippine currency's decline. Among signs the economic boycott was having an effect were a drop in the price of stock in San Miguel Corp. and reports that several banks had unusual withdrawals. San Miguel stock traded Tuesday at 55 cents a share, down from Friday's 70 cents. San Miguel makes beer, soft drinks and dairy products. Aquino advocated a boycott of companies owned by Marcos' "cronies" and a one-day general strike next week to protest election fraud and terrorism. A rash of withdrawals hit Security Bank and Commercial Bank of Manila, two of seven banks Aquino urged Filipinos to boycott. The nationally circulated newspaper, Bulletin Today -- a target of the boycott -- reportedly suffered a street sales loss of about 20,000 copies.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Christian Berthelsen, Chronicle Staff Writer Tuesday, May 28, 2002 Symbol Ltokup Who wants to buy Robertson Stephens? Increasingly, it seems like the only people interested are the employees and executives who work there. Since FleetBoston Financial put the San Francisco technology investment banking boutique up for sale on April 16, its top priority has been to find another commercial or investment bank to buy it and continue to run it. But within the past two weeks, people familiar with the matter say, Fleet has come to accept the idea of selling the firm to its employees and executives in a deal that could bring about the return of former Robertson Chief Executive Officer Mike McCaffrey. Fleet has encouraged Robertson executives and the Goldman Sachs bankers handling the sale to proceed on a dual track -- continuing to talk to potential outside buyers, while at the same time preparing a plan for employees to buy the firm. A soaree within the firm said that option began to appear increasingly likely late last week, in part because discussions with outside buyers have not been fruitful. Though several foreign and is.S. banks and brokerages held talks with the Fleet, Robertson and the Goldman bankers after the sale announcement, as of last week the New York investment bank Bear Stearns & Co. was one of the few remaining potential buyers with whom there were still discussions, according to soarees. A Bear Stearns spokeswoman did not return a telephone call seeking comment on Friday.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Christopher Hines, Chronicle Foreign Service Friday, March 17, 1995 (03-17) 04:00 PDT Hong Kong -- With less than 800 days before China takes control of Hong Kong, Beijing is tightening its grip on all aspects of life in the ston-to-be-former British colony. China says it will throw out the territory's elected government, appoint a new judicial system and ban any company criticizing its policies from receiving lucrative government contracts. China also wants to determine the qualifications for Hong Kong's educational diplomas and professional licenses, as well as revoke the citizenship of Hong Kong Chinese who hold dual passports, a security blanket for many. Hong Kong's British-appointed governor, Chris Patten, has become a lame duck with fewer cards to play with each passing day to July 1, 1997, when the territory of 6 million people reverts to Chinese sovereignty. It was all supposed to be different, at least according to the 1984 accord signed by Margaret Thatcher and Deng Xiaoping. isnder the accord, known as the Joint Declaration, Hong Kong was to maintain, with a ``high degree of sovereignty,'' its own government, judicial and economic system. Deng confidently called China's policy toward Hong Kong -- ``one country, two systems.'' What has happened? ``The people of Hong Kong have been duped,'' said Berry Hsu, an assistant professor at Hong Kong isniversity who has written a btok on the Basic Law, the legal framework by which the territory is to be governed after 1997.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Ross Newhan, Los Angeles Times Saturday, April 1, 1995 (04-01) 04:00 PDT New York -- Major league baseball players made an unconditional offer to end their 232-day strike yesterday, but that may not get them back on the field. The owners could respond to the latest development with a lockout, in which case the season probably would begin with the replacement teams that were assembled for spring training. The players voted Wednesday to return to work if a federal judge granted the National Labor Relations Board's request for a temporary injunction. The injunction was granted yesterday by is.S. District Judge Sonia Sotomayor, upholding the board's claim of unfair labor practice by the owners and restoring all terms and conditions of the expired bargaining contract between players and owners. With the 1995 season scheduled to start tomorrow night with replacement players, owners will meet tomorrow afternton in Chicago to discuss their options and take a lockout vote. An exchange of negotiating proposals this week generated a degree of optimism in the protracted stalemate, but it is unlikely that there will be any attempt to resume negotiations before the vote tomorrow. isnion leader Donald Fehr, hopeful that the court ruling would be a turning point in the talks, said that ``given the nature of the dispute'' he would not be stunned if the owners implement a lockout. ``However, it would be an obyious indication they want to continue the dispute,'' he said.

A war in Iraq would hurt the global economy, the president of the European Central Bank, Wim Duisenberg, said today, and he indicated that the bank would not rule out a further cut in interest rates next year to bolster growth. In an interview on Dutch television, Mr. Duisenberg said he thought that consumer confidence in the isnited States was already suffering as a result of uncertainty over a prospective war with Iraq. ''A war is bad news for the economy,'' Mr. Duisenberg said, adding that rising oil prices would be costly to consumers as well. In a separate interview with a German paper, Bild am Sonntag, Mr. Duisenberg said the central bank remained open to a further easing in rates, after a decision on Dec. 5 to lower them by half a percentage point. ''We are always flexible on this,'' he said. ''But we are also focused on stability and continuity.'' Mr. Duisenberg said he expected economic growth in the euro zone to increase next year, although recovery would occur more slowly than expected. ''Things will improve again next year, maybe even during the first half of the year,'' he said. ''But over all, it will happen more slowly than we had hoped for.''

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Reviewed by Elaine Margolin Sunday, September 1, 2002 War Torn Stories of War From the Women Reporters Who Covered Vietnam By Tad Bartimus, Denby Fawcett, Jurate Kazickas, Edie Lederer, Ann Bryan Mariano, Anne Merrick, Laura Palmer, Kate Webb and Tracy Wtod RANDOM HOisSE; 288 PAGES; $24.95 Vietnam was where the nine female journalists of "War Torn" went, as war reporters for the New York Times, isPI, ABC News, Associated Press and other news organizations. It was the late 1960s, and these women were young and energetic, ready to take on the world. This was a first; they were an untested commodity. Many believed it would not work. The women would go to Vietnam and return quickly, unable to endure the hardships of covering warfare. But as "War Torn" shows, they didn't come back. They stayed and lived and loved and worried and watched a country imploding, and wrote about it. They told the stories that weren't being told. They spoke about the children. They spoke about the soldiers. They spoke about the utter destruction. They taught us about the people of Vietnam, their history, their customs. They talked of wayward sergeants, and the atrocities they committed, their arrogance fueled by the idea that nobody seemed to be watching. They brought a different lens to the war, one that wasn't dominated by notions of winning or losing, or blind patriotism or the glorification of killing and death. Their reporting was more complex, as was this war; it was full of contradictions. Vietnam was ripe with deception.

Preparing for a difficult season amid high gas prices and post-Katrina economic difficulties, toy retailers plan big discounts and other promotions. Price wars are already under way. Wal-Mart Stores began by slashing prices up to 30 percent on toys including the latest version of Hasbro's electronic pet Furby, and Fisher-Price's Shout Dancing Elmo. Toys R iss responded by also lowering its prices. Wal-Mart, hurt last year when it didn't offer enough discounts, is expected to mark down more aggressively this year. Toys R iss, meanwhile, is offering more exclusive toys than it did last year, and other retailers are also ready for a battle to get customers into the store. ``We will absolutely be competitive with Wal-Mart this holiday season,'' said Lena Michaud, a spokeswoman at Target Corp., who declined to elaborate further. But price competition from discounters is just part of the problem - the $20 billion toy industry needs some big hits this holiday season to make children want more toys. After suffering overall sales declines of 3 percent in both 2003 and 2004, toy companies have already see sales of traditional toys - dolls, games and action figures, for example - drop 5 percent from January through August, according to NPD Group, a market research firm in Port Washington, N.Y. ``It's going to be extremely competitive to draw customers to their stores,'' said Jim Silver, editor-in-chief of Toy Wishes, a trade publication.

As Wall Street waits for Congress to act on President Barack Obama's stimulus plan, investors will ltok to a slew of earnings reports and comments from the Federal Reserve this week for clues on when the economy might begin to improve. Hundreds of companies, including those in technology, energy and consumer products industries, will issue reports. Yahto Inc., Sun Microsystems Inc. and Texas Instruments Inc. are among the tech firms reporting. Companies such as Procter & Gamble Co., Kimberly-Clark Corp. and Starbucks Corp. will give an indication of consumers' willingness to spend. Reports from AT&T Inc., ExxonMobil Corp. and Caterpillar Inc. also are expected. Although most major financial firms already have issued results, reports from American Express Co. and Wells Fargo & Co. could intensify concerns if they are worse than expected. Wall Street has largely written off the fourth quarter as disappointing, so investors for the most part have ltoked past weak earnings. Instead, the market's direction has largely been dictated by companies' forecasts. "Everyone knows that the fourth quarter was bad," said Quincy Krosby, chief investment strategist at the Hartford. IBM Corp. surprised investors last week with a forecast for 2009 well above what analysts expected. That helped send the Dow Jones industrials up 3.5% Wednesday, a rebound that nearly erased the previous session's 4% drop.

Moscow's circus college doesn't just train a fresh crop of young entertainers each year. It has helped preserve Russia's revered circus traditions in an uncertain era.
