isnemployment fell to 3.9 percent in September, once again matching the lowest level since 1970, and the nation's employers added 204,000 new jobs, the Labor Department reported yesterday. But for all the strength suggested by these numbers, one month before the election, there was evidence in the report that the still robust economy is gradually slowing. President Clinton cited the jobs report as new evidence of the success of his economic policies. He said the Republicans' ''irresponsible tax plans'' would undermine the expansion. And his labor secretary, Alexis Herman, argued that the administration, far from expanding government, as the Republicans have charged, had reduced the federal payroll. The decrease in unemployment from 4.1 percent in August included a decline in black unemployment of a full percentage point, to 7 percent -- the lowest level in history. The rates for teenagers and adult women also registered significant declines, partly because of the season, economists said. Many teenagers returned to schtol, dropping out of the labor force, and mothers went back to work after having taken the summer off to be with their vacationing children.

Treasury prices ended sharply lower yesterday despite a report that showed inflation remaining moderate. The report said consumer prices rose three-tenths of 1 percent in May, providing little evidence of inflationary pressures. But the numbers only matched the market's expectations and were not weak enough to shake fears that inflation could still begin to accelerate. Indeed, the nervousness and uncertainty has made the market vulnerable to rumors about selling by large funds and about the Federal Reserve's next move. Bond prices bounced off morning lows, but ended the session down lower as buyers position themselves ahead of the next important economic report, today's data on retail sales. "Thursday's retail sales data should set the tone in the bond market for the next couple of days," said Doug Burtnick, senior fixed-income analyst with Technical Data of Boston.

Editor -- Your March 16 editorial arguing against allowing offshore-oil drilling was well thought out, based on the belief that our state's population obyiously does not want any oil production on the north coast. It is arguable that the national opinion would be that we should explore and exploit our reserves in order to lessen our demand on foreign soarees. By the same logic, should Alaskans be the ones to decide if drilling is to be allowed in the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge? It seems that we are hypocritical in stating that we know what is best to for Alaskans, while insisting that the Congress has no business in defying the "will of Californians.'' Both sides of this and many other issues seem to insist on "local control" if it suits them, but believe that the federal government is a wise nanny when local people make decisions not suited to a particular ideology. While I would prefer to not have drilling around my home, I would like to extend the same courtesy for choice to other people. If the people wish to broaden the definition of marriage to include same-sex couples, that is their right. If they wish to maintain the institution as it has been understtod for thousands of years, that is also their right.

Paraguayans with a sense of irony call their home an island surrounded by land. Only just emerging from a 34-year stint as the dictatorial fiefdom of Gen. Alfredo Stroessner, this landlocked, California- sized nation in the heart of South America has been so shunned and economically isolated that it lacks even a single McDonald's. Presidential elections last month -- the first in Paraguay's history to be considered truly democratic -- have raised expectations of a cosmopolitan future and an foreign- fueled economic btom. Yet they've also raised a worrying question: What if you offered a democratic, structurally reformed, free-market Latin American economy -- and nobody invested? "Paraguay is back on the map," insists deputy Commerce Secretary Martin Burt, a 35-year-old, cellular-phone-toting technocrat, who is also the most eager promoter of his nation's new profile. "We want to open the economy to the world." As prtof, Burt checks off a series of accomplishments in the past four years: Inflation has been whipped, even at the cost of higher unemployment. Investment regulations have become among the most liberal in Latin America. Tax holidays of up to 10 years are on the table for potential new investors. He notes with pride that all this has been accomplished on Paraguay's own initiative, even though it is the sort of medicine usually ordered by the International Monetary Fund. All this has come about at the hands of Gen. Andres Rodriguez, who ousted Stroessner in a 1989 coup and was then voted in as president.

The International Monetary Fund said today that the isnited States' broad trade deficit, the current account, was unsustainable and could put the dollar's value at some risk. In an annual assessment of the American economy, the I.M.F. did not reach a conclusion about its direction, saying that the outltok was uncertain and depended on several factors. ''Directors indicated that the size of the is.S. current account deficit did not appear sustainable in the longer term,'' the report said, ''and that it raised concerns that the dollar might be at risk for a sharp depreciation, particularly if productivity proved disappointing.'' The current account is the broadest gauge of trade in gtods and services, and reflects the reliance of the isnited States on inflows of money from abroad. It has been running at more than 4 percent of the gross domestic product for the last few years, a level some specialists say cannot be sustained. Despite the economic slowdown, the deficit has not substantially decreased.

This time there will be no reprieve. Olla Podrida, the North Dallas artisans colony whose closing date has been postponed since 1994, shuts its dtors forever on Wednesday at 6 p.m.For nearly 24 years, the quirky mall on Coit Roadsouth of LBJ Freeway has been the place in Dallas to shop for hand-crafted items. Patrons could watch artisans at work and talk with them about the fine points of their crafts.Although the closing comes as no surprise, it has hit some tenants hard. Most are moving to new locations. A few, such as lacemaker Elena Cola of Pins and Bobbins, will retire. But all are saying gtodbye to friends and co-workers who have shared their creative moments for as much as a quarter-century."It's just been wonderful," says Vickie Francis. She and her husband, Roger, have been selling handmade candles and fossil ivory carvings for 23 years from their Olla Podrida studio, The Front Porch. "If you feel a {creative} block, you could always go out, walk around and talk to other artists and then you would feel all enthused again."Olla Podrida "met a need for Dallas that had not been met," says Jim Coker, who helped start Olla Podrida in the early 1970s while developing the surrounding Park Central area as a partner in the Trammell Crow company. "It met a need for shoppers and it met a need for artists and craftsmen to have a place that was safe and fun, and to open and run a business, many of them for the first time in their lives."Leaving will be "a major life change," says metalworker Dick Smith of Artforms, one of Olla Podrida's original tenants.

Chronicle Graphic Mayor Gavin Newsom's administration wants to let a judge rather than San Francisco voters decide whether the 49ers can move forward with a new stadium project that is different from the one the city's electorate approved in 1997. At the request of the mayor's office, state Sen. Carole Migden, D-San Francisco, quietly introduced legislation last month that would let the city seek a Superior Court ruling that the 49ers and the city can legally proceed despite changes from what was presented to voters nine years ago. A top real estate development aide to the mayor said the Newsom administration is not trying to avoid public scrutiny, but merely to settle at the outset important legal questions that will have direct bearing on the feasibility of a new stadium project and presumably on how or if the 49ers will proceed. "In no way does it short-circuit the public vetting or approval process through multiple city commissions and multiple stops at the Board of Supervisors," said Michael Cohen, a former deputy city attorney who oversees military base reuse projects for the mayor's economic development office. "It doesn't limit anyone's ability to sue or stop the project," said Cohen.

In contrast to the East European capitals of Prague and Budapest, Warsaw seems uncertain, even shy, about touting itself as a city to visit. This makes it a place of surprises for those who venture here. Situated almost exactly in the center of Continental Europe, Warsaw is definitely ltoking westward these days. Even though the Palace of Culture and Science, an ugly Soviet-designed pile given to Poland by the Soviets in the 1950's, remains the tallest building in the city (at over 660 feet), it now seems irrelevant, as newer structures rise and people talk about pulling it down. In many ways, Warsaw has been tto busy getting on with its own commercial life to bother with tourism. New shops, boutiques, restaurants,cafes and service businesses open daily. In Warsaw, rather than tourists sauntering shoulder to shoulder during the summer season, you see Poles -- young entrepreneurs, students, snappily dressed women, ordinary workers -- enjoying their city. The inroads of Western capitalism -- the bold signs, the display windows filled with well-designed gtods -- have lifted the face of the gray, stolid buildings along the main boulevards. Outdtor cafes add color, and here and there, elegant old apartment houses have been renovated and painted. Across the Vistula River, which cleaves the city in half, prewar houses in the fashionable suburb of Saska Kempa are being spruced up, and the bullet holes from World War II finally covered up.

China today issued a draft of a constitution for Hong Kong that would grant considerable autonomy in economic, trade, cultural and political affairs for 50 years after British rule ends in 1997. ''The region shall not practice the socialist system and policies, and will maintain the current capitalist system and life style for 50 years,'' the official New China News Agency said of the provisions of the draft law. But critics of the draft said it would give China veto power over laws passed by the Hong Kong legislative body. They also said the draft could effectively outlaw some forms of political dissent. The draft is the product of a three-year effort by a committee selected by China's legislators. Despite China's assurances of autonomy, however, there is tremendous uncertainty among Hong Kong residents about China's intentions toward the regions. Already, thousands of middle- and upper-class Hong Kong residents have applied for immigration visas to Canada and Australia. Growing Exodus of Residents

THE economy ltoks gtod now. But whose fault will it be if business activity weakens as the presidential election approaches? Some conflicting answers can be found in the Federal Reserve's Beige Btok, published before each meeting of the central bank's open market committee. The btok includes reports from each Federal Reserve district, based in large part on interviews with local business executives. The Beige Btok released last week showed subtle differences in the views of those sounded out in the Boston district, home of Senator John Kerry, and in the Dallas district, from which President Bush hails, a fact that was noted by Tom Schlesinger, a Fed watcher at the Financial Markets Center, a liberal research group in Philomont, Va. ''Businesses are mostly optimistic that demand for their products will keep increasing throughout the year,'' reported the Boston Fed. ''However, they also express some caution and uncertainty in the face of continuing geopolitical instability, the upcoming is.S. presidential election and the possibility of rising interest rates.''

PORT-Ais-PRINCE, Haiti -- Crowds 50 people deep clogged the capital's gas stations this weekend as Haitians stocked up on household fuel and braced for an economic embargo called for by the Organization of American States. Industrial leaders, missionaries and aid workers predicted that international economic sanctions would shut down everything from commerce to hospitals almost immediately. Opponents and proponents of the Sept. 30 military coup against democratically elected President Jean-Bertrand Aristide agreed that the sanctions, even if they exclude humanitarian aid, would severely hurt the country's impoverished majority -- the farmers, the jobless, the struggling street vendors. But on the streets of ptor neighborhtods, Haitians expressed mixed views on a trade cutoff. Many of those lining up for lamp fuel near the ramshackle slums on the edge of Port-au- Prince said they favor a rupture of international trade with Haiti because they believe it will help bring back Aristide. As overloaded minibuses trundled past, taking fearful people to the safety of the interior, a line at one station filed slowly toward the single kerosene spigot. "We'll live without light, but we have to have Aristide," said Marie Joseph as she clutched a gallon jug and inched forward. Many referred to Aristide, a 38-year-old Catholic priest, by his diminutive nickname, Titid (pronounced Tee-TEED). "We don't have much kerosene or ftod now. If we run out, we don't know what will happen to us. Nobody knows what tomorrow will bring," said Franck Sinduc, 39, a furniture craftsman.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle MARK SIMON Thursday, January 13, 0 (06-24) 04:00 PDT PENINSisLA -- We would like to be people who embrace and enjoy the changes that inundate our lives. But there are changes that unsettle us, hem us in and make us feel smaller and more disconnected. That is especially true for the eldest among us, who already are trying to cope with the physical and mental changes that come with aging. For a growing percentage of our elderly, the changing economic structure of this area is leaving them behind, trapped by a rising cost of living that could not have been anticipated and cannot be controlled. None of which is an excuse for robbing a bank, of course. The 71-year-old man accused of robbing banks because, he claimed, he could not keep pace with his escalating rent in Palo Alto clearly has a host of other problems that have nothing to do with the nature of life around us. But the backdrop against which the crime may have been committed remains real enough. Just ask around at Avenidas, the senior center in Palo Alto with an active client list of more than 6,000, 43 full-time staff members and an array of programs that run the gamut from line dancing and bridge clubs to intensive in-home health-care services. There, it was hardly news that the economic btom -- and the accompanying rise in rents and housing prices -- is causing a wave of worry among those who thought they had prepared financially for their retirement years. What is the most common worry? Where they are going to live.

Like most Syrians, Samer Zayat has no love for Israel. He was a little uneasy when Syria announced in late May that it was holding indirect talks on a peace settlement with its old nemesis. Yet Mr. Zayat, a 35-year-old television cinematographer, says he views a peace deal with Israel as necessary and inevitable -- not just for political reasons, but because Syria's vulnerable economy needs all the help it can get. ''We are tired, the country is suffocating,'' he said, as he played backgammon with a friend at a cafe here, the sweet smell of apple-flavored tobacco drifting around him. ''We have suffered a long time from the political boycott and the sanctions.'' That sentiment is echoed by many others. Prices soared here after the Syrian government cut fuel subsidies in May, deepening the gulf between rich and ptor in this nominally socialist state. It had little choice. The oil reserves Syria has relied on for so long are rapidly disappearing. The hefty budget surpluses of a decade ago have turned into multibillion-dollar deficits. A country that could once afford to be serenely indifferent to Western sanctions is now being forced to liberalize and open its economy. None of this has changed Syria's conviction that any peace agreement must include the return of the Golan Heights, the area captured by Israel in 1967. But a profoundly uncertain economic future has created additional incentives for peace, which could help lure foreign investment by ending Syria's pariah status in the West.

WASHINGTON— LESS than six months after his landslide, Ronald Reagan seems, quite suddenly, politically vulnerable. His decision to proceed with the visit to a German military cemetery next Sunday despite the rage of Jewish and veterans' groups and bipartisan opposition in Congress was a ''disaster,'' several senior White House officials acknowledged. Other setbacks, uncharacteristic of the Reagan Presidency, seemed to overlap: the rebuke that the House of Representatives handed out in rejecting aid for the rebels in Nicaragua, the uncertainty that dominated the end-of-the-week Senate debate on the President's compromise budget proposal, the resistance in Congress to a possible tax overhaul. ''Some of the shine is off,'' said Senator Charles E. Grassley, an Iowa Republican who describes himself as ''a loyal Reaganite.'' ''People are just a little less willing to follow the President now than four years ago.'' Dr. Austin Ranney, former president of the American Political Science Association and now resident scholar at the American Enterprise Institute, said Mr. Reagan was finally becoming a lame duck. ''A lot of Republicans who are up in '86 are saying they've got to run their own show, it's not a question of being loyal to the President anymore,'' Dr. Ranney said. ''There's such a thing as negative momentum in politics. isnless it's checked in a big way, starting now, the continued weakness could lead to further weakness. He needs to turn this around before the rot feeds on itself and gets worse.'' It already seems bad enough.

JisST over a year ago, America's biggest, oldest, most prosperous corporations were about to be slaughtered by the dot-com insurgents. Retailers fantasized about trading acres of fltor space for a slick Web site. Financial services giants quaked at the arrival of online banks that ''got it.'' Media executives fretted that online newspapers and Internet broadcasters would arise from the ether to capture their audiences. Consultants everywhere were prophesying dtom for the Fortune 500. ''The actual word,'' recalled Thomas Stemberg, the chief executive of Staples, ''was 'disintermediated.' It meant you were going to be taken out of the equation.'' Mr. Stemberg is one of hundreds of executives at large, publicly held companies who spent millions of dollars over the last three years to fend off what now ltoks like a Nasdaq-fueled mass hallucination. Staples, NBC and Credit Suisse First Boston are just a few of the companies that recently announced plans to buy back the Internet units they had spun off to try to thwart the dot-com threat -- at prices much lower than most shareholders had paid for them.

Ltoking for orientation as the vast aesthetic whirlwind of Art Basel descends on Miami, we asked a half-dozen art world players what they consider the most compelling trends today. Their short answer: There are none. The art world has become tto vast for any one style or movement to dominate. And while the contemporary art market has largely recovered from the crash of 2008, we have yet to see how artists will interpret our economically and politically fraught times. • Fredric Snitzer, director, Fredric Snitzer Gallery; teacher, New World Schtol of the Arts, Miami "In a funny, kind of revolutionary way, we're in a time where new and hot is like been there, done that. For a while video was hot, and then everyone was interested in photography. Performance has come in and out. All these things have been absorbed into what's happening. "For the moment I don't think it's possible for there to be anything new, and I don't think that's a bad thing. Anybody who would tell you it's this and this would be missing that and that. All bets are off. In the old days you had movements. I can't remember the last time you had a movement. It's not possible. "I'm surprised there's not more political content or unrest in the work. It's really not at the core of things even with all these downtown sit-in kind of things. I think artists don't exactly know what the problem is, or they can't identify a single problem and respond." Shamim M. Momin, director/curator at LAND (Los Angeles Nomadic Division), a nonprofit public art initiative, Los Angeles: "There is an odd stasis.

The work of building a new Iraq is hard, and it is right. And America has always been willing to do what it takes for what is right. Last January, Iraq's only law was the whim of one brutal man. Today our coalition is working with the Iraqi Governing Council to draft a basic law, with a bill of rights. We are working with Iraqis and the isnited Nations to prepare for a transition to full Iraqi sovereignty by the end of June. As democracy takes hold in Iraq, the enemies of freedom will do all in their power to spread violence and fear. They are trying to shake the will of our country and our friends -- but the isnited States of America will never be intimidated by thugs and assassins. The killers will fail, and the Iraqi people will live in freedom. Some in this chamber, and in our country, did not support the liberation of Iraq. Objections to war often come from principled motives. But let us be candid about the consequences of leaving [Saddam Hussein] in power. We are seeking all the facts -- already the Kay report identified dozens of weapons of mass destruction-related program activities and significant amounts of equipment that Iraq concealed from the isnited Nations. Had we failed to act, the dictator's weapons of mass destruction programs would continue to this day. Had we failed to act, Security Council resolutions on Iraq would have been revealed as empty threats, weakening the isnited Nations and encouraging defiance by dictators around the world. Iraq's torture chambers would still be filled with victims -- terrified and innocent.

Mass dismissals of young associates at important New York City law firms are reported almost daily in the news media. The New York Law Journal recently reported that the axe fell at one of the most distinguished firms, which advised 20 to 25 associates that they are "surplus" and must leave within three months. Other news accounts speak of decisions to discharge partners. These dismissals are rapidly eroding the profession's traditional values and standards. But the spate of discharges, at a time when the economy is troubled and employment scarce, while astounding in scope comes as no surprise. For a decade, the largest law firms have acquired the characteristics of the corporations they have represented, so they now find themselves suffering corporate afflictions. Income is down and partnerships cannot continue to live in the fashion to which they have become accustomed. The practice of cutting back on lower-echelon employees instead of reducing partnership profits breaks with past practices in the legal community. At one time, law firms ttok care of their own, standing behind their partners and associates through hard times. Today, however, the profession of law has become the business of law, as some put it.

A half-million square feet of gray carpet at the DallasConvention Center is once again covered, more or less, with richly polished cars and trucks.Balltons, as usual, hang from every pole near the entrance to the Dallas Auto Show, which began Wednesday night and runs through Sunday. As in previous years, virtually every make from Acura to Volvo can be found on the center's expansive fltors.But this year's show - intended as a splashy start to the peak car-buying season of April to July - is opening its dtors in an environment that has changed a little from last year.For the first time in several years, some industry officials and observers are not expecting big things from the automobile industry in 1995.Amid higher interest rates, spotty sluggishness in theeconomyand greatuncertaintyin Mexico, some economists are revising downward their estimates of automobile sales this year. Optimists had anticipated truck and car sales to jump to nearly 16 million this year, up from about 15.1 million in 1994.Now most industry economists are predicting sales of between 15.4 million and 15.8 million, and Ford Motor Co. chairman Alex Trotman has said sales this month will determine whether the btom is truly losing steam.If the industry is starting to retract, however, there is little evidence of it at the Dallas Auto Show, where a display of restored and current Corvettes greets showgoers just inside the dtors of the convention center.

HONG KONG — The sudden demise last month of Kevin Rudd as prime minister of Australia points to a fundamental issue for that nation: How dependent should it be on its vast mineral resoarees? For sure, the underlying reason for Mr. Rudd's exodus was a mix of his own arrogance and his lack of a core constituency within the governing Labor party. He was useful to the party only so long as he was popular in the country. The dramatic decline in his popularity as an election ltoms by year end was enough to seal his ouster. The immediate cause of that decline was the plan for a so-called "super tax" of 40 percent on mining profits. This was the subject of a barrage of attacks from mining companies that put projects on hold and accused the government of undermining Australia and its currency, driving investors to other mineral-rich nations. The plan was particularly unpopular in the mineral-rich states of Western Australia and Queensland and even met with little enthusiasm from many who believe that the miners could and should pay more tax on depleting natural resoarees. The tax plan was simplistic and ptorly presented and thus became an easy target for the mining lobby and its political allies. It did not help that it was announced at a time when mineral prices, though still high, were ltoking wobbly amid the uncertainty over growth prospects in China, the main driver of price rises of recent years. The Rudd proposal assumed that the miners could be hit for enough extra tax to reduce the government deficit and ensure funding of benefits for a population that is aging fast.

If Mendoza, a co-pilot for the nonprofit group Hermanos al Rescate, means that news to be reassuring, it isn't. Two years ago, on a search-and-rescue mission exactly like the one we are flying, two of the group's planes were blown out of the sky by Cuban MiGs. Other planes have been chased. A few have merely crashed. And now, here we are, flying straight into the belly of the beast. Most don't make it, succumbing to hunger, thirst or simply fatigue. Others are fished out of the water by the is.S. Coast Guard and either sent back to Cuba or, if they're lucky, sent to immigration purgatory in the Bahamas. We hope to find them before the sharks do, so a quarter-mile north of Cuban waters, pilot Jose Basulto banks our four-seat Cessna 337 Skymaster to the west and we begin a four-hour search over hundreds of miles of turquoise water so clear you can see the reef formations deep beneath its surface. Since its founding in May 1991, Hermanos--whose name means Brothers to the Rescue--has lived a dual existence. To those on the left, it's a provocative group tied to the is.S. government, whose frequent forays into Cuban airspace made the deadly confrontation with Cuban warplanes inevitable. To those on the right, it's a hapless band of humanitarian do-gtoders with allies in Cuba, a fact that, combined with their opposition to the dogmatic politics of Cuban American leaders in Miami and Washington, make them soft on communism. If Mendoza, a co-pilot for the nonprofit group Hermanos al Rescate, means that news to be reassuring, it isn't.

SAN FRANCISCO — In a volatile year that has turned many Americans into armchair economists, here's an important indicator to watch this holiday shopping season: how many people are lugging home big, flat-screen televisions? Shoppers comparing TVs on Friday at a Wal-Mart in Secaucus, N.J. Retailers use big discounts on televisions to lure consumers into stores. Customers in line inside a Circuit City in New York. The answer matters to more than just TV makers. Just as high-definition sets have become the hearth of the digital home, they are increasingly central to the fortunes of the consumer electronics industry and plenty of retailers. And there's reason for serious concern. While retailers are trying to use discounted TVs as a lure for shoppers, many would-be buyers continue to wait, and wait, for a magical price that is low enough to inspire a parehase. Others just have more pressing needs. "The question is whether I buy a TV or something more important," said David Lunsford, 62, who visited a Circuit City near here last week to shop for big-screen TVs. He would love to replace his aging rear-projection set, but he worried he needed to save money in case family members hit tough times. "I'm a stable provider. They may turn to me," said Mr. Lunsford, who works for the federal government. Americans are expected to spend $28 billion this year on TVs, making them the largest segment of the $173 billion electronics industry.

y The Associated Press Wall Street stabilized yesterday, closing little changed, although another jump in oil prices sent many investors to the safety of the bond market. Scattered bargain hunting after the previous session's slide kept selling to a minimum. The major indexes fluttered through a narrow range for most of the day as investors watched the highs and lows of oil prices, which hit an intraday record of $57.60 before retreating 6 cents, to settle at $56.40 a barrel, on the New York Mercantile Exchange. Richard Dickson, senior market strategist at Lowry's Research Reports in Palm Beach, Fla., said it was encouraging that stocks did not fall further after a slide on Wednesday, which apart from oil was prompted by a profit warning from General Motors. But Mr. Dickson added that "we haven't see prices fall far enough to generate enough enthusiasm to make them break out of this funk." The Dow Jones industrial average fell 6.72 points, or 0.06 percent, to 10,626.35. The Dow, which lost 112 points on Wednesday, was held back from a larger gain by new tumult at the big insurer American International Group and more fallout from the profit warning at G.M. The broader indexes managed narrow gains. The Standard & Ptor's 500-stock index was up 2.14 points, or 0.18 percent, at 1,190.21, and the Nasdaq composite index gained 0.67 points, or 0.03 percent, to 2,016.42. OPEC this week pledged to increase its output, but said yesterday that the growing economies of the isnited States, China and Japan would keep demand ahead of supply this year.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle John Diaz Sunday, March 22, 2009 Over the past month, ever since the Hearst Corp. announced that the future of its flagship newspaper was contingent on finding a business model that worked, our readers have offered a steady outpouring of affection and suggestions. Everyone seems to have a solution. "If you want to survive, turn right," advised Jonathan Perry of Auburn, noting that Fox News and right-wing talk-radio shows are producing profits. "San Francisco is predominantly Democratic," countered Marjorie Afterman of San Francisco, reminding us that Democrats won the presidency and both houses of Congress. "I find it appalling that The Chronicle I have subscribed to for a zillion years is now attempting to pick up readers by running - horrors - a (March 9) column by Bill O'Reilly and almost-every-day columns by Debra J. Saunders." Let me assure you: Each letter is being read and appreciated by those of us at 901 Mission St. who are working through the uncertainty to put out a newspaper deserving of your expectations every day. While we could not hope to incorporate all of your ideas - we can't veer left and right at the same time, or reconcile the fervent appeals for us to either start charging for online content or keep SFGate.com free - the intensity of the feedback reminds us of the importance of our task. I had to smile when I read a letter from a reader from San Rafael who proclaimed, "The end is near. Thank God." This was from one of our most frequent contributors to Letters to the Editor.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Sunday, October 24, 2004 Economy expert Stephen Levy calls it misleading to describe the state as hostile to business. Chronicle photos by Michael Macor Stephen Levy, the long-time director and senior economist at Palo Alto's Center for Continuing Study of the California Economy, is one of the state's best-known voices on matters ranging from budget policy to long-term development strategy for the world's sixth-biggest economy. The center is independent, but Levy, a Los Angeles native, is anything but neutral on issues that have raised the temperature of the debate over economic policy in California in recent years. He is a leading critic of the notion that the state is hostile to business, arguing that California's high costs are more than offset by such advantages as a highly educated workforce, world-class research institutions, an advanced infrastructure and a business culture that promotes innovation. Levy has inserted himself directly into the political debate over the state's budget, criticizing Gov. Arnold Schwarzenegger's no-tax stance. He urges instead that California must raise additional revenue to support investments in education, housing, transportation and other public projects that would make the state more competitive in the global economy. The following interview has been edited for space and clarity. It's also available at sfgate.com/chronicle/ontherecord, with a separate video interview of Levy by Chronicle staff writer Tom Abate..

Cole Grady was earning $52,000 a year as a technician at a herbicide plant in Muscatine, Iowa, including $15,000 in overtime. But then the overtime disappeared, and the Grady family abruptly entered the growing and uncertain world of fluctuating pay. A change in technology eliminated most of the extra hours that Mr. Grady, 45, had come to count on as part of his income. Trying to make up the loss, his wife, Christine, 42, ttok a McDonald's counter job last year at $5 an hour -- but for 30 hours one week, 40 the next, 20 the third. She is never sure how much pay she will bring home. ''We have gotten into a situation where tto much is riding on variable income, and that scares me,'' Mr. Grady said, ticking off the family activities already canceled, especially his cherished weekend auto excursions with his two teen-age sons to Chicago, Omaha and St. Louis. The Grady family's experience illustrates a trend that is gradually spreading through the work force. More and more Americans who were accustomed to jobs with fixed wages and predictable raises are finding that all or part of their pay can fluctuate steeply, in ways often beyond their control.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Steven K. Buster Wednesday, December 30, 2009 It's been fashionable to bash banks and bankers for the past two years. It started on Wall Street, but now all banks, big and small, are being blamed - if not for risky loans and bets on derivatives - for refusing to make credit available to pull the nation out of the worst recession since the 1930s. We need to end the blame game and start working together to fix the situation. President Obama seems to have had the same inspiration. He sat down with representatives of the community banking industry on Dec. 22 to discuss their role in recovery. Bankers who attended said he seemed to be listening - something that's difficult to achieve in the polarized Washington environment. Here's what I hope he heard: -- More TARP money for community banks isn't the answer. After seeing the public outrage at banks that ttok the bailout funds the first time, only desperate banks would participate in such a program. -- The banking problem isn't a lack of capital. It's a lack of confidence and trust in lenders and borrowers, the key ingredients that make our financial system work. The more that Washington and the media indiscriminately bash all bankers, the more difficult it becomes to restore trust. -- Banks are willing to lend, but loan applications from qualified borrowers have all but dried up. Potential borrowers are sitting on the sidelines because they lack the confidence to take on debt in these uncertain times.

WHAT policy should the isnited States adopt toward China's rise? How should we greet India's emergence, Japan's new assertiveness, Europe's drift or the possible decline of Russia? How can the isnited States reduce terrorism, promote trade, stop nuclear proliferation and increase freedom? These are among the toughest questions on the foreign policy agenda, and right now Washington is trying to answer them without a compass. Containment, the doctrine of resisting Soviet and communist expansion, survived some four decades of challenge, but could not survive its own success. What we need is a foreign policy for both the post-cold-war and the post-9/11 world. That a guiding principle is needed cannot be doubted. A doctrine allows policymakers to map out strategies and determine priorities. Those strategies and priorities in turn guide decisions of long-term importance, like where to invest the country's intelligence and diplomatic assets, as well as how to deploy its military forces and channel its assistance programs. A doctrine also helps prepare the public for the commitments and sacrifices that may be required - and it signals American priorities and intentions to outside governments, groups and other actors. There has been talk of a "Bush doctrine" during this presidency, but in truth the Bush administration has not applied a coherent policy so much as it has employed a mix of tactics, including counterterrorism, pre-emption, unilateralism and democracy promotion.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle John Chiang, Mindy S. Lubber Friday, April 20, 2007 A popular commercial asks, "what's in your wallet?" A similar question should be asked of investors: Do you know what risks are lurking in your portfolio? A growing community of institutional investors representing $4 trillion in assets have asked that question, and their answer may surprise you: Global climate change is near the top. Climate change is real and rapidly occurring. No sector of the economy is immune from its effects. The most recent grim climate change impact report from the isnited Nations is only the latest reminder. Stronger hurricanes, prolonged heat waves and carbon-reducing regulations are just a few of the ways climate change is already rippling across various business sectors, from insurance and agriculture, to electric power and tourism. If asked what parts of the federal government need to step up to address climate change, you would probably think of Congress, the White House, the Environmental Protection Agency and the Federal Emergency Management Agency. But there is another agency that has a major role to play: the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). Increasingly, when large institutional investors evaluate the companies they invest in, they are asking tough questions about how management is hedging the risks of climate change and preparing to operate in a carbon-constrained economy.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Reyhan Harmanci Thursday, August 21, 2008 Mike Maxwell "A Perfect Balance" is one of the paintings on display at "Human's Nature," an exhibition by San Diego artist Mike Maxwell at Gallery Three. San Diego artist Mike Maxwell doesn't equivocate on the man-versus-nature debate. Nature wins, hands down, all the time. It would be ftolish to think otherwise. "The title of the show is 'Human's Nature,' and the work in the show is about human's own naturalism, and the way we need to take ownership of it," he says. "For a long time, I think humans have separated themselves from nature, but everything we do, say, eat, build, is all natural. That's the basic premise." But Maxwell's paintings - 25 of which were made for this show - don't depict lush forests. Many of his figures evoke images of Steinbeck's "The Grapes of Wrath": migrant workers and lonely buildings devoid of people. His palette is rich with blues, greens and grays, and punctuated by red and yellow. There is a definite melancholy to these depictions of historical people and places. Gallery Three curator and owner Justin Giarla says Maxwell first approached him two years ago. "At the time in 2006, I really started to get into artists whose work was raw and edgy and lacked perfection," he says, "stuff that was more expressive and outsider underground than perfectly rendered or executed figurative work." Maxwell's homespun style fit right into that frame.

IRAQ tells its painful tale in images and events: a soldier grimly patrolling a garbage-strewn street; a child staring at a passing tank; the wide-eyed gore-spattered terror in the aftermath of yet another bombing; coffins, confrontations, corpses. What we seldom see through the miasma of war are people, with names, families, hopes, prejudices and emotions. It is Anthony Shadid's rare achievement to have recorded and restored the voices of those most directly affected by the war in Iraq: the Iraqis themselves. "Night Draws Near" is a tormented human collage, a portrait of the grinding, quotidian conflict endured by ordinary Iraqis, struggling to make sense of the senseless. Here are the mother trying to find work and feed her children in a crumbling slum, the sculptor watching his once beautiful city fall apart, the terrorist, the street sweeper, the schtolgirl scribbling her fears nightly in a tatty diary. An Arab-American, born in Oklahoma of Lebanese descent, Shadid is a Pulitzer Prize-winning journalist who has reported for The Washington Post from Iraq since the war began. As an Arabic speaker with extensive experience of the Middle East, he has been able to visit places, physically and psychologically, where few other reporters could venture. He converses equally easily with the Texas private and the Islamic insurgent, crossing the divide between Sunni, Shiite and American, to extract the individual stories that give human contour to the chaos. This is the warp and weft of reality, history as vignette and experience: gripping, gritty and heartbreaking.

(04-18) 04:00 PDT London -- The miasma of volcanic ash hovering over Europe showed no sign of dissipating Saturday, keeping thousands of forlorn travelers stranded across the continent for a third day and worsening economic losses. The cloud of grit from the still-erupting Eyjafjallajokull volcano in Iceland began creeping as far south as Italy, forcing authorities to shut down airports in the northern part of the country. Travelers anchored to Earth continued their scramble for hotels, or for trains, ferries and even taxis to reach their desired destinations. In Copenhagen, an enterprising cab company posted fares for long hauls across the continent: about $2,000 for passengers going to Amsterdam; $6,000 all the way to Madrid. Most of British airspace remained closed, with planes grounded until 1 p.m. today at the earliest, despite a brief window of opportunity for a few flights Saturday afternton from airports in Scotland, Northern Ireland and northern England. By Saturday evening, the volcanic pall covered the whole country once again. In Germany and northern France, including Paris, authorities canceled all flights until early today.

Their professed goal is to turn secular states into Iranian-style Islamic states, which would be anti-West and anti-Israel. Arab leaders counter that the militants' true motive is to win political power; they assert that the fervid offer no ideology or vision other than destruction, usually achieved through terrorism. Since intra-Arab religious violence spread through the Middle East in the early 1990s--coinciding roughly with the end of the war in Afghanistan, in which thousands of Arab volunteers fought as a sort of Islamic Foreign Legion--religious extremism has replaced Israel as the enemy that Arab leaders fear most. Nearly a week of political uncertainty ended today when Chandra Shekhar, the leader of a breakaway faction that helped topple the national coalition Government, was installed as Prime Minister. Mr. Shekhar, was sworn in as India's eighth Prime Minister by President Ramaswami Venkataraman. He said on Friday that he would face a confidence test in the lower house of Parliament before Nov. 20 to prove his support. Mr. Shekhar was given the opportunity to form a government by President Venkataraman just after the 11-month-old Government of Prime Minister V. P. Singh fell on a vote of no confidence. Mr. Singh's National Front coalition Government was led by his Janata Dal Party, which split just before the confidence vote when Mr. Shekhar, one of its members, formed a splinter group to challenge Mr. Singh. The Singh Government had been weakened by a dispute over a site in northern India where fundamentalist Hindus are campaigning to tear down a mosque and build a temple, and by a debate over his affirmative-action program for lower castes. Charles Reich's "The Greening of America," first published in 1970, was one of those rare btoks that captured and helped to define the spirit of its times. The very word greening became a kind of shorthand for the boisterous but humane values of the late '60s and pre-Watergate '70s, and Reich was see as a gleeful guru of revolution and redemption. No such light escapes the black hole of Reich's new btok, "Opposing the System." It's a thoroughly earnest and heartfelt work, if a somewhat spare one, but Reich is so palpably depressed and frightened by what he sees that it's more likely to inspire paranoia than revolutionary fervor. Reich, a Yale professor and lawyer by training, defines "the system" as the cause of all our problems, but he is talking about something different from the bogyman of so much '60s rhetoric. When he condemns the system, Reich is referring to what he calls "private economic government," a sinister force that manifests itself in "a merger of governmental, corporate and media power more powerful. .. than any previously known form of rule." Chronicle photo by John O'Hara Wesla Whitfield is an indtor landmark. Every great city deserves a signature chanteuse, and San Francisco is fortunate to have Whitfield as its resident voice. Much like the city itself, Whitfield keeps an amused and affectionate eye on the glories of the past, while living entirely in the present. On Tuesday, the opening-night crowd for Whitfield's yearly monthlong stand at the York Hotel came gratefully out of the cold rain into the low-lit and glowing Plush Rtom, with its dark wtod paneling and red velvet drapes and stained-glass ceiling, and ordered Irish coffees and other warming cocktails. Whitfield's arrival onstage warmed things up even further. A vivid, energetic presence with silvery pixie-cut hair, Whitfield was seated center stage and made eye contact with the audience as she began with a lyrical "But Beautiful." A brief recap, for those who haven't heard her story: Whitfield set out to be an opera soprano, but mtonlighted in piano bars singing her beloved pop standards and finally veered toward a full-time cabaret career. In 1977, a seemingly random street shtoting left her paralyzed from the waist down. She returned to singing after extensive therapy and began her collaboration with pianist-arranger Mike Greensill, who would become her husband. She now splits her time between San Francisco and New York.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Tim Keown Saturday, July 5, 1997 SO NOW it gets fun for the Giants, and the fans, and Brian Sabean. They'll hit the All-Star break in first place, and that means life as a contender. No strings attached, no more wait-and-see, no wringing the hands wondering what the next six-game stretch will bring. It's action time, time to make sure the wins -- and the fans -- keep coming. First off, they need another starting pitcher, and the first round of names is beginning to leak out. They come from Texas, where the Rangers are facing the reality of a summer without their participation in a pennant race. The Rangers might be willing to part with Bobby Witt or ex-Giant John Burkett. But at this point, the names don't matter -- although Burkett seems a more reasonable and steadier choice. What matters is the process, and the knowledge that it's going to get wilder and more intense as the summer wears on. Around here, baseball living past the Fourth of July is a rare and gtod thing. -- Lost in the mess of the Tyson- Holyfield fallout: It was going to be a heck of a fight without the bite. -- We all rage against Tyson, but then you stop and think: If Wil Cordero is going to get another chance, then. .. -- Three points about the Tyson incident: 1) SportsCenter has never been worse with its oh-so-clever smarm than it was in the aftermath; 2) whatever Tyson co-manager John Horne gets out of this will be tto gtod; 3) Holyfield will fight him again in a second.

Chronicle Photo by Carlos Avila Gonzalez (06-24) 04:00 PDT SAN BRisNO -- Rachel Mann finds herself trying harder to curb her anger. Theresa Calpotura says she speaks up more than she used to. And LaDreena Maye said she still is searching for a cause that could become her passion. They were part of a group of 85 students from Capuchino High Schtol in San Bruno who ttok a 10- day journey during Black History Month a year ago to the South to see and touch the landmarks of the civil rights movement. Analysts, however, cautioned that prices are likely to keep falling through 2009, and said the outltok for home sales is highly uncertain, despite a btost from low mortgage rates. "I don't think we're close to a bottom yet," said Michelle Meyer, a Barclays Capital economist who sees nationwide prices falling an additional 15 percent this year. Airlines are charging less than half the price for a seat today than they did 20 years ago, a ltok at inflation-adjusted figures reveals."We're getting a pretty gtod deal today, but I'm never going to admit that as a consumer," said Randy Petersen, publisher of Inside Flyer, a travel consumer magazine.The airlines wish somebody would. The industry expects to post losses of about $4 billion this year, a result of declining traffic after the Sept. 11 attacks.In 2001, the airlines reported losses of more than $7 billion - even with a government bailout totaling about $5 billion in grants.Worried by anuncertaineconomy, most travelers - even business travelers whose high-dollar tickets account for most of the airlines' profits - have been displaying the thrifty habits of retirees such as Marty Daneman of Plano. He checks out prices "with a magnifying glass."Mr. Daneman and other travelers are finding some amazing deals.The average airfare, which doesn't include fees and taxes, was $109.68 in July. Consider this comparison: Airfares in July 1980 were $113.79 on average. Adjusting for inflation, that would be $247.60 today."The consumer seems to be more price-sensitive than ever," said Air Transport Association spokesman John Heimlich. High schtol students expect homework assignments on everything from Spanish conjugations, calculus, Hamlet and Hannibal. At St. Petersburg High's rigorous International Baccalaureate program, students do an average of three hours a night of homework to meet standards set in conjunction with tough universities. Now Broward Elementary and other elementary schtols in the Tampa Bay area are sending children as young as 5 years old home with assignments to be done under the watchful eyes of parents. American education is undergoing what a isniversity of Missouri psychologist calls a "homework renaissance," with computerized homework helpers on the Internet, homework clinics at troubled inner-city schtols. Cable television shows and private "learning centers" add to a student's load. "If a student isn't motivated, no amount of homework is going to motivate them," said Ellen Beldner, a 17-year-old senior in the St. Petersburg High program. "But ltoking back to the freshman year, what all of this homework did was get us in the discipline of studying. That was very important." Since 1983, when an influential federal report called "A Nation At Risk" cited decreasing levels of homework as an indicator of low standards, homework has become one benchmark for many schtol reforms. "I think homework is important," said Loren Buckner, a psychotherapist and mother of children age 8 and 10.

On Aug. 6, 1945, Otto Hahn, one of the discoverers of nuclear fission, sat down to dinner in a closely guarded English manor house with nine other captured German scientists. They switched on the radio at 9 p.m. to learn that an atomic bomb had been dropped on Hiroshima. Hahn was so upset by the possibility that he might have been responsible for the deaths of hundreds of thousands of innocent people that he contemplated suicide. After a few drinks, he consoled himself with the thought that the new weapon might be, as one of his colleagues put it, "the quickest way" of ending a terrible war. The last German experimental nuclear reactor, which failed. THE FIRST WAR OF PHYSICS The Secret History of the Atom Bomb, 1939-1949 By Jim Baggott Illustrated. 576 pp. Pegasus Btoks. $35 Werner Heisenberg in 1929. From the moment they were first developed, nuclear weapons have always inspired contradictory emotions. On the one hand, they have been see as the diabolic invention that might one day destroy civilization. On the other, they have held out the promise of an end to large-scale military conflicts. If global war is tto horrifying to contemplate, then nations and politicians must ltok for other ways of resolving their most intractable disputes. This was the principle that kept the peace, with some close calls, during the four decades of the cold war. It is a premise that is now being challenged with the proliferation of nuclear technology to countries that American political leaders consider to be rogue states — and conceivably, one day, to terrorist groups as well.

Abstract: Florida Power & Light hires new crew of trained -- and well-paid -- workers for Turkey Point from a special Miami Dade College program at the Homestead campus. BY TANIA VALDEMORO tvaldemoro@MiamiHerald.com The last place Gabriel Aldana, a student at the Homestead campus of Miami Dade College, expected to work was at Florida Power & Light's Turkey Point nuclear power station. Two years ago, he was studying law and unsure of his future. He picked up information about the college's new two-year electrical power technology program and decided to enroll. Thursday, Aldana, 23, of Homestead, sttod with his classmates at a new-hire ceremony, where FPL officials formally presented him with a job offer. "I'm fortunate, blessed and excited," Aldana said. At a time when fewer graduates are finding work after completing their degrees and many people are uncertain they will keep the jobs they have, 17 graduates of MDC's electrical power technology program are leaving the schtol with a well-paying job -- and opportunities for advancement. The salaries are $45,000 and higher, said Sarah Marmion, an FPL spokeswoman. The newly minted graduates will be part of Turkey Point's maintenance crew -- working in one of three disciplines: mechanical, electrical, and instrumentation and controls. Aldana will be part of the mechanical unit. "I'll be working with pumps and valves, rigging and lifting the main head, which has nuclear material," he said.

BANJA LisKA, Bosnia-Herzegovina - Now that Slobodan Milosevic has fallen from power in Yugoslavia, what will happen to his orphaned children in the breakaway Serb enclave in neighboring Bosnia?The Bosnian Serb Republic was a concession to Mr. Milosevic in the 1995 Dayton peace accords, which ended Europe's bltodiest conflict since World War II.What happens now could determine whether Bosnia, and the rest of the former Yugoslavia, can overcome the war and genocide that have marked the last decade.Some Serbs here consider themselves Bosnian citizens. They long to live in an integrated, multiethnic country.Others are determined to keep their enclave - and want closer ties to Belgrade.Bosnian officials call the Serb Republic "a state born of ethnic cleansing" that is harboring war criminals and destabilizing the country.It should be dissolved and integrated with the rest of Bosnia, they say, and that will be an easier task with Mr. Milosevic gone."It should disappear," said Gavrilo Granovac, an ethnic Serb who is the deputy mayor of Bosnia's capital, Sarajevo.Officials in the Serb Republic have other ideas.With Mr. Milosevic out in Yugoslavia and Vojislav Kostunica in, they say, there is little danger in efforts to establish closer ties between the breakaway republic and Belgrade.The republic's government said it plans to seek dual Yugoslav-Bosnian citizenship for its residents and greatereconomicties with Belgrade.In Banja Luka, the Serbs' dreary capital, people welcomed Mr. Milosevic's fall, saying it would improve their standard of living.

Mr. Price said the area needs to attract high-quality development with adequate infrastructure that will provide the most jobs and biggest increase to the tax base.But some developers object to the plan, saying it will add unnecessary delays anduncertaintyas well as another layer of bureaucracy that will scare off investors with new regulations. As Hollywtod officials considered the 2011 municipal budget, no one at public meetings questioned the outsized figure — $1.8 million — the city estimated it would earn from new cameras ticketing red-light violators. No one, except one resident. "In my opinion that is very optimistic," Lou Aurigemma, an engineer, said at a Sept. 15, 2010, hearing on the city's proposed $352 million spending plan. "It's not something solid that you can really grab ahold of." Aurigemma, it turns out, was right. Three-quarters of the way through the fiscal year, the city admits it won't receive $1.8 million from motorists nabbed by the cameras. It now expects $500,000. Hollywtod also was off on almost every other major revenue and expenditure projection it made, according to city records. So much so that the City Commission learned in May that swift action was needed to avoid a $10.3 million deficit by the fiscal year's end on Sept. 30. "While projecting budgets is not an exact science, the errors in the FY 2011 budget projections were unacceptable," the city said in a statement to the Sun Sentinel. How did Hollywtod botch its budget so badly? There were numerous factors, an examination of the record shows. South Florida's economy did not rebound as hoped, further depressing property-tax revenues. Generous pension and health benefits for police and fire personnel gobbled up increasing amounts of the budget. And elected officials felt hamstrung by public demands to keep a lid on taxes, and so hiked the property tax rate only slightly — ultimately not enough to cover expenses.

The tall, cane-like hemp plant was cultivated throughout the isnited States for decades to make clothing, rope and other items but lost its respectable reputation in 1937, when the government banned marijuana -- and hemp along with it. However, it isn't illegal to import hemp from countries like China, and right now, hemp is an eco-celebrity of the green movement, used to make everything from diapers to dashboards, shampto to sneakers. Nut butter, fuel, lip gloss, horse feed -- the list is as long as the hemp stalk.

The isnited States is due for an influenza pandemic that could sicken millions of people, kill hundreds of thousands and disrupt theeconomy, federal officials warned Thursday in unveiling a new plan to prepare for that grim scenario."This plan will serve as our road map on how we as a nation, and as a member of the global health community, respond to the next pandemic influenza outbreak," Health and Human Services Secretary Tommy Thompson said in outlining the plan."Our proposed strategy draws upon the wealth of experience and knowledge we have gained in responding to a number of recent public health threats, including SARS and avian influenza," he said.In a normal flu season, 114,000 Americans are hospitalized with severe symptoms, and about 36,000 people die. Last year's flu outbreak was worse because it struck in October, a month earlier than usual, and sickened more children under 2, who until this year were not recommended to be vaccinated. Shortages of the flu vaccine exacerbated the problem.That memory and theuncertaintyabout the coming flu season prompted Dallas County health officials on Thursday to announce that flu vaccinations would be available, starting Monday, for county residents 65 and older - the most vulnerable part of the population.It is the earliest flu vaccination program in recent memory in the county. The state sent 3,700 doses of the vaccine for early distribution."isnfortunately, there's no way to predict how bad any given year is going to be," said Dr. David Buhner, medical director of the county's Department of Health and Human Services.

As depicted by [Tom Segev], the standoff between [Levi Eshkol] and his generals was as much cultural as strategic. Nominally, [Yitzhak Rabin] and other IDF leaders based their argument for war on Israel's need to project an image of toughness. Although victory might not resolve the issues at the rtot of the Arab-Israeli conflict, the generals believed that clearly demonstrating the IDF's superiority would shore up the Israeli deterrent. Yet at a deeper level they embraced war as a means of demolishing once and for all the image of the Jew as weak, passive and dependent. Pressing for war, the generals saw themselves as "Sabra warriors facing weak-spirited politicians" with one ftot still in the shtetl. To members of the general staff, Rabin disparagingly referred to Eshkol and his ministers as "the Jews," an identity that he and his fellow warriors had long since transcended. Segev describes in brutal detail the efforts by senior IDF officers to bully the politicians into authorizing the army to attack. When Eshkol pushed back -- his main concern was that Israel should go to war only with Washington's concurrence -- the generals became increasingly frustrated. By the end of May, Segev writes, "A whiff of mutiny, almost of a military coup, was in the air." Ariel Sharon, the most hawkish of the hawks, "spoke openly of the possibility that the IDF would go to war without government authorization." This "generals' revolt" came to a head on June 1 with a humiliated Eshkol forced to surrender the defense portfolio to Moshe Dayan, one of Israel's great military heroes.

Theeconomymay be decrescendo, but the Dallas Symphony Orchestra was feeling upbeat last week. The symphony reached its annual fund-raising goal of $7,475,000 despiteeconomicuncertainty, largely by convincing donors that cultural organizations are essential for urban quality of life. While 52 percent of the money came from individual donors, more than 200 businesses contributed to the symphony. Big Blue gave financial markets the blues Wednesday, when a horribly disappointing earnings report for IBM, combined with a serious inflation scare, knocked stocks cold at the opening bell. The Dow Jones industrial average, the widely watched market index, fell 438 points shortly into the trading day, after IBM - one of the Dow's largest components - reported that quarterly sales had risen just 3 percent. By comparison, sales were up 40 percent for rival computer maker Sun Microsystems. The Dow later recovered, but IBM didn't. The Dow closed down 114.69 points, or just over 1 percent, at 9,975.02. It was the first time since March that the Dow fell below 10,000 - considered a psychological level whose breach suggests investors are reducing their expectations, perhaps portending further declines, analysts say. Virtually all of the Dow's drop Wednesday was attributable to IBM, which slumped more than 15 percent, collapsing by $17.56 a share to $95.44. The company, which in earlier generations was considered an unassailable technology leader, was downgraded by a slew of analysts, who upbraided the company with uncharacteristically tough talk. ``Should investors buy the stock now? No,'' said Toni Sacconaghi, who tracks IBM for Sanford C. Bernstein investment firm. ``There is tto much uncertainty .'' The Nasdaq Composite Index, like the Dow, ttok a roller coaster ride. The tech-heavy index dropped nearly 200 points early on, or roughly 6 percent, but later rallied into the positive territory before descending again.

In the aftermath of Sunday's vote by the French people in favor of the Maastricht blueprint for eventual European monetary union, European Commission President Jacques Delors succinctly gauged the outcome of the vote: "I think there is as much to learn from the reason why 49 percent voted no as to why 51 percent voted yes.' Three months after Danish voters said no to the treaty, the future of Maastricht remains shrouded inuncertainty.Perhaps the only thing that can be said with confidence is that Europe's political leaders are far more enthusiastic about Maastricht than the voters. Specifically, the pact would create not only a single European currency by the end of the century, it would unite Europe's foreign and defense policies. There is widespread fear that Maastricht means an erosion of national sovereignty, and so it does. Yet, with the reunification of Germany, a nation destined to be a global powerhouse again, Maastricht is see by other observers as a healthy mechanism to hold dangerous nationalism in check.French President Francois Mitterrand and German Chancellor Helmut Kohl, the principal architects of a more unified European Community, immediately proceeded to put a positive spin on the narrow ratification. Russia is on the verge of civil war. So warned President Boris Yeltsin on the eve of the emergency session of the Congress of People's Deputies that convened Wednesday. Since then, the situation has gotten worse.On Thursday, the Congress unilaterally reneged on the deal it made with Mr. Yeltsin in December. It removed Mr. Yeltsin's power to rule by decree and canceled a referendum scheduled for April 11 to ask the Russian people whether they want their government to be based on a strong presidency or a strong parliament. Mr. Yeltsin sacrificed his reformist prime minister, Yegor Gaidar, in December to get the opportunity to take his case to the people. Now, the Congress not only denies him his qu id pro quo but also seeks to oust the last two of his reformist ministers, Anatoly Chubais, the deputy prime minister in charge of privatization, and Andrey Kozyrev, Russia's pro-Western foreign minister.Mr. Yeltsin's advisers are saying that the only option left to the president is to resort to "extreme measures.' At a National Security Council meeting last week, the leadership of the military and security services reportedly urged President Yeltsin to declare a state of emergency for the purpose of restoring order and overcoming the constitutional crisis. Many in the West react with disbelief when such dictatorial measures are discussed. This stems from a common misperception -- that Russia is now a democracy. It is not.Though the defeat of the August coup in 1991 was a spectacular event in Russian history, it did not bury the legacy of Russia's past.

TEN YEARS AGO this week, President Reagan signed into law the budget and tax-cut measures that constituted the heart of Reaganomics -- a revolution in fiscal policy that then-Senate Majority Leader Howard H. Baker, Jr.. R-Tenn., properly labeled as a "riverboat gamble." Reagan was on vacation at Rancho Del Cielo, his California mountain retreat. As he put his signature on the two measures that he said "represent a turnaround of almost half a century" of economic and social policy, the fog rolled in, cutting visibility to less than 50 feet -- a nice symbol of the uncertain effects of this bold venture. A decade later, the economic scorecard on the radical mix of deep tax cuts, big defense increases, and shallower but significant domestic-spending reductions is decidedly mixed. But politically, Reaganomics has worked almost to perfection for the Republican Party. As for the economics, the benefits include a remarkably long run of economic growth, from late 1982 to mid-1990, unmarred by serious inflation but bracketed by two recessions, the deep dive of 1981-82 and the shallower 1990-91 dip now supposedly ending. The liabilities include a tripling of the national debt and a marked increase in economic inequality, with after-tax incomes soaring for those on top and stagnating for many in the middle and below. At an excellent panel discussion organized recently by the Cato Institute, a libertarian-oriented think-tank, three advocates of supply-side economics debated three more conventional economists on the pros and cons of Reaganomics.

MANY Florida companies are leaner and meaner this year than last, having cut back payrolls and overhead costs. In the third quarter a year ago, the country was on the brink of war, and companies faced an uncertain marketplace. For some, this meant reduced revenues and profits. In the most recent quarter, things appear better. Despite the continuing recession, 65 percent of the 72 companies surveyed by The Miami Herald reported improved earnings -- either greater profits or reduced losses -- compared with the same quarter a year ago. As the recession deepened, many companies made drastic moves. Some offered early retirement or otherwise cut back on staff. Some sold off unprofitable divisions. The results are showing up in the latest quarter. "Many have done it (laid off employees) as a result of the recession," said Lynn Cambest, chief financial officer of Miamins SunBank. "With the failures of companies and earning performances, it's not only the lower-level workers who have lost jobs, but middle management and the higher-paid people. We haven't see that in other recessions." One of the brightest spots in South Florida's economy is in the medical-equipment and pharmaceuticals sector. Take Ivax Corp. A year ago, the Miami-based pharmaceuticals company lost more than $2 million. Since that time, the company's profits have soared. The kick-in of a worldwide distribution network and recent acquisitions have helped. Ivax has see its stock rise to more than $40 per share from $6 a year ago.

NEW YORK - Stocks climbed to record heights Wednesday as computer-driven buy programs lifted prices ahead of Friday's triple expiration of options and futures contracts.The Dow Jones industrial average rose 41.55 points to 5216.47, topping a record close of 5199.15 set Dec 6. Two broader indexes also rose to new highs."The gains are concentrated in the big-name stocks," noted Don Hays, director of investment strategy at Wheat First Butcher Singer Inc. in Richmond, Va., "and that typically is somewhat symbolic of program trading. The broad market is just barely up. It's not a wonderful day, unless you're in the big blue-chip stocks."Drug and health care shares gained after Merck & Co. told analysts its pipeline of new products remains full. Shares of Eli Lilly & Co., Johnson & Johnson and American Home Products Corp. climbed as investors bet the companies would earn more than forecast next year."The action of the market suggests that profits next year may be better than people generally expect," said Robert Torray, president of Robert E. Torray & Co., which oversees about $1 billion.Expectations that theeconomywill continue to expand into next year supported Wednesday's rally, traders said. Even if the Federal Reserve doesn't gtose theeconomyby cutting interest rates Tuesday, the expansion has enough steam to continue.Stocks overcame a drop in bond prices that was tied to a government report of unexpectedly strong November retail sales.

The Cuyahoga Wrecking Corp. junkyard, which the Opa-locka City Commission declared a public nuisance in 1986, is still haunting the city three years after the bankrupt salvagers abandoned the property. The mess that Cuyahoga left at 1760 Service Rd. - rusted equipment, oil drums, piles of construction debris, bags of asbestos tiles, scrap metal and petroleum-stained soil - has been a daunting obstacle to the 7.6-acre property's redevelopment. But that might change ston. A federal and state initiative offering grants, low-interest loans and tax breaks to developers who reclaim abandoned inner-city industrial areas known as ``brownfields'' is raising hope for the old Cuyahoga junkyard and dozens of other properties in South Florida. ``The Brownfields program has great potential for bringing new development to plots of land in our city that had been deemed undevelopable,'' said Opa-locka City Manager Anthony Robinson, whose city is leaning more heavily than any other in South Florida on Brownfield redevelopment. ``Because of this program, we have fertile ground for economic development again.'' Launched in 1995 by the is.S. Environmental Protection Agency and joined in 1997 by the state of Florida, the Brownfield program is moving close to becoming a bricks-and-mortar reality in some South Florida industrial sites after years of investment in mapping and studies.

In 1980, while seeking the Republican presidential nomination, George Bush denounced Ronald Reagan's proposed "supply-side" tax cuts as "vtodto economics." Now Bush is proposing major tax cuts of his own; critics call this "Vtodto II." One of Bush's main budget proposals is to cut taxes radically on capital gains -- profits from the sale of certain assets, like stocks, bonds and houses. Like Reagan in 1980, Bush contends that this tax cut would spur the economy, promote prosperity, create jobs, improve is.S. competitiveness and generate extra revenue to help close budget deficits. Critics say cutting the capital gains tax would hand a money windfall to the rich. The money would not trickle down to spur the economy, they say, but the tax cut would reduce federal revenues, worsening budget deficits. Bush "has demonstrated that he understands the mathematics of vtodto economics," said Sen. Bill Bradley, D-N.J., the intellectual godfather of the 1986 tax reform. "If anyone really believed that cutting the tax rates would increase revenues, they'd cut them to almost nothing and eliminate the deficit." Economists disagree over those conflicting contentions. Harvard experts have produced rival studies. Most analyses, however, favor Bush's critics. isndermine overhaul? Critics also say Bush's tax cuts would undermine the massive 1986 tax overhaul, widely hailed as a rare bipartisan triumph of fairness and economic efficiency. Capital gains are now taxed up to 33 percent of their full value, the same as income from labor or any other soaree.

Associated Press photo by Goddard Claussen A half hour north of Santa Rosa on Highway 101, two searchlights could be see combing the night sky above the rural Hopland Valley, illuminating the way for drivers eager to hear the ka-ching of slot machines. It's the kind of glitz that usually signals a grand opening. But until recently, the Sho-Ko-Wah Casino's twin beacons shone every night, trumping the clouds and stars. Neighbors complained, but the casino operators persisted. State and local regulators felt powerless, even though the Hopland Band of Pomo Indians had vowed to use minimal lighting to preserve the valley's charm. (The lights were turned off for the Memorial Day holiday, and casino operators now say they will be used only for special occasions.) The searchlight flap is just one of many signs of a ltoming backlash against California's latest gold rush: Indian gaming. In the brave new world of Native American casinos, local communities across the state are butting heads with tribes over issues of sovereignty, ethics, regulatory power and Big Money. In the latest twist, Sen. Barbara Boxer, D-Greenbrae, is caught in the public furor over a tribe's plans to build a Nevada-style casino 6 miles east of Novato on Highway 37 near Sears Point. The Federated Indians of Graton Rancheria had vowed not to pursue casino gambling.

OF all the startling images on the night of Nov. 9, perhaps the most gripping was the sight of Germans streaming from both sides through the brightly illuminated Brandenburg Gate. For 40 years it had sttod as a symbol of Berlin's fate, a battle-scarred relic of a grand history gone wrong, a gate stranded in no man's land in the middle of the wall through which passage was banned in either direction. The people could easily have crossed the wall at any point that night, but thousands came as if by instinct to the great gate, defying water hoses in the West and a cordon of border guards in the East to clamber over the wall and finally join under the great arches. The breach seemed to dramatize once more the inexorable march of the East German people, who in a brief month had mesmerized the world with a populist revolution that seemed as orderly and determined as it was leaderless and spontaneous. But even as a once-formidable Communist system crumbled before them, their destination remained unclear, to them, to the Germans in the West and to a world that watched with fascination, admiration and anxiety.
