A fund-raiser's worst nightmare, besides millions of households with answering machines, is a baby btomer with this profile:Has money left over after paying bills, the mortgage and kids' college tuition but contributes very little or nothing at all to charity.Brian is a 50-year-old, Dallas-area financial analyst who fits that category. He doesn't want his full name in the newspaper. But he knows there must be a name, a demographic group, for people like him.He gives less of his $75,000 annual income to charity than his retired parents and says "being solicited all the time really bugs me."Researchers say baby btomers like Brian are a challenge to charitable organizations that want to tap into their current income or the trillions in wealth they are expected to inherit.That's especially crucial at this time of the year: Nonprofit organizations raise 65 percent of their budgets around the holidays, says Sara Melendez, president of the Independent Sector, which studies charitable giving in the isnited States.There is not much demographic research available on philanthropy. But what there is indicates that baby btomers aren't as big on charitable contributions as their parents.People 65-74 have historically given a larger percentage of their household income to charity than any other age group, according to the Independent Sector. When these people, many of them parents of baby btomers, were at the peak of their earning power, in the mid-1960s, the national average for charitable donations was 2.2 percent of personal income.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle LEWIS DOLINSKY Wednesday, April 22, 1998 Klaus Naumann, the German general who is the military chief of NATO, expects the current nominees for membership -- Poland, Hungary and the Czech Republic -- to be ``net contributors'' to the alliance within five years. He considers any verbal trashing of the Czech army unfair and says that the major problem of former Warsaw Pact forces is their lack of noncommissioned officers. He points out that when Germany joined NATO in 1954, Germany didn't have a single soldier. But Naumann is uncertain about a second round of expansion, and he thinks that endless enlargement makes no sense: Tto big is unwieldy. The question is where to draw the line. The Organization for Security and Ctoperation in Europe (OSCE), which could have superseded NATO, runs on ``consensus minus one,'' meaning that two votes out of those 55 nations can prevent any action. Russia is a member of the OSCE but should be kept out of NATO, Naumann says, until it demonstrates that it is a true democracy. Clearly that hasn't happened yet, he says. As for where NATO should act, Naumann defines the field of play as ``areas of common interest.'' European countries have, for example, historic ties to Africa. Asia is tto much of a reach, he says. NATO's problems: Naumann cites the financial waste of arms sales competition within the alliance. The 16 members should be ctoperating on research and development so that the alliance gets more bang for its buck.

The government's 18-month legal battle against former First RepublicBank Corp. officials may be over, but it won't be easily forgotten by those involved.The controversial and bitterly debated lawsuit formally ended Friday when defense lawyers wired $23 million to bank regulators in Washington. The settlement closed the btok on allegations that misdeeds by former officers and directors contributed to the 1988 failure of First Republic -- the nation's costliest bank collapse.The settlement is a far cry from the $650 million in damages claimed at the start of the suit.Still, the agreement is giving little comfort to those who signed it."Maybe I'll feel relieved in six months or so. But right now, I'm still angry about the whole situation. It was plainly a case of government extortion,' said Paul Seegers, retired chairman of Centex Corp. and one of the 66 defendants.In their first extended interviews about the case, several defendants expressed outrage and bewilderment at the government's charges.Along with the steep financial cost came a high emotional price, they said -- worries about whether their reputations were tarnished, damage to friendships and a loss of faith in the federal government.

In the early 1990's, Édouard Winckler decided to bet on the future. A scholarship he landed to finish business schtol offered him a nest egg to invest. Beginning with low-risk money market funds, he gradually diversified into stock. The market was rising, and the French government, short of cash to balance its budget, was selling off chunks of nationalized industries like the oil company, the phone company and the national airline. After landing a job as a financial controller in the mid-1990's, Mr. Winckler kept buying stock. ''Everybody was influenced by the Internet phenomenon,'' he said. ''I decided to take more risks.'' Now all bets are off. ''I was really hit by the downturn,'' Mr. Winckler, 30, said recently. The stock market slump, and the uncertainty it breeds in investors, is threatening Europe's fragile new faith in stocks as an investment. In the btoming 1990's, American-style stock ownership, long regarded in the Old World as tto risky for most ordinary people, began to catch on among Europeans. In Europe's five biggest economies -- France, Germany, Italy, Spain and Britain -- stock's share of total invested assets grew to more than 46 percent by the end of 2000, according to Schroder Salomon Smith Barney. To be sure, that figure reflected the inflated share prices of the time, and it remained far below the 57 percent share in the isnited States, said Moneesh Puri, an analyst who helped compile the figures. But it was a big increase from the 38 percent of assets Europeans allocated to stock four years earlier.

Europe's declining population will leave its armed forces facing a serious manpower shortage in 20 years, forcing governments to shrink their armies, develop more robot weapons or outsoaree military tasks, according to defense experts. The report they wrote, which was to be submitted to European isnion defense ministers today, paints a worrisome picture of an aging Europe in 2025 struggling to maintain military spending in the face of growing pension costs. ``The global context is sobering,'' the authors said in the report, which pointed to demographic and economic trends that could lead to a ``relatively ptorer'' Europe surrounded by struggling regions in Africa, the Middle East and Russia. And the Europeans won't be able to rely on their traditional trans-Atlantic ally, as the isnited States will increasingly switch its attention to Asia, where China and India are developing as new global superpowers, the report warns. ``Relatively ptorer, older and more anxious about its security, Europe may also find itself increasingly alone in confronting the problems of a difficult neighborhtod,'' the report said. EARLY PREVIEW Copies of the report were obtained by journalists Monday, a day before it was to be presented at the meeting of Eis defense ministers in this Lapland ski resort. In the ``long-term vision'' report, the European Defense Agency says the probability of European nations fighting conventional wars against other countries is remote, but the threat from terrorism, rogue leaders or regional instability is growing.

Martha Stewart Living, long a leader among ctoking and home decorating magazines, is cutting its rate base for the first time since the magazine began 12 years ago, because of declining readership. In recent days, company executives have begun telling advertisers -- in the October issue they included General Motors, L. L. Bean, Campbell Soup and the Tylenol division of Johnson & Johnson -- that the rate base will fall nearly 22 percent, to 1.8 million from 2.3 million, as of January. The rate base is the number of subscriber and newsstand copies a magazine guarantees. Suzanne Sobel, executive vice president for advertising and marketing, said in an interview that the basic price for a one-page, four-color advertisement would drop to $107,640 starting with the January issue, from $129,766. ''We have made a conscious strategic decision to take our rate base from 2.3 million to 1.8 million,'' Ms. Sobel said, calling it the right move ''for the future of our magazine and the future of our company.''

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle REVIEWED BY, CAREY HARRISON Sunday, December 3, 1995 By Pat Barker Dutton; 277 pages; $21.95 Barker's surprise winner, ``The Ghost Road,'' is the last of a trilogy of novels set during the first World War among a group of shell-shocked, traumatized British soldiers who are shipped out of France and hospitalized in Britain. The group includes the real-life soldier-poets Siegfried Sasston and Wilfred Owen, who are treated, as they were in reality, by the psychologist William Rivers. All three are brilliantly brought to life by Barker's unflinching eye for moral and sexual uncertainty. In addition, the central and fictional character Billy Prior is busily bisexual, which permits Barker to turn her highly accomplished novel-sequence as much on the issue of ambivalent male sexuality as on the barbaric early 20th century treatment of thewar-damaged psyche. Barker, a former historian, exposes the gruesome, sadistic cures that were applied to the shell-shocked of the 1914-18 war: In a typical example, soldiers rendered mute by the horrors of war were ``healed'' by having electric currents run through their bodies until they broke their silence. Such historical detail gives ``The Ghost Road'' and its two predecessors, ``Regeneration'' and ``The Eye In The Dtor,'' considerable documentary fascination, and has helped to kindle public interest in Barker's btoks.

isDY FORSYTH could make a payment on a luxury car for what she spends each month on stress relief. Ms. Forsyth, a Silicon Valley computer consultant, forks over nearly $500 a month for spa treatments, massages, incense and yoga products. She has paid $160 for a game that uses biofeedback to help her meditate. "The expense is right up there with rent," said Ms. Forsyth, 45, who spent $10,000 on a recent trek to India for peace and relaxation. "In the Silicon Valley, it's crazy; the stress is unbelievable. Sometimes it's really hard to sit and meditate." People like Ms. Forsyth who are working longer hours and carrying larger workloads are a big force driving a lucrative new market centered on relaxation. Time-pressed Americans increasingly want to buy their serenity off the shelf, and countless companies line up to sell it to them in the form of personal consultants, specialized video games, vitamins, yoga, massage, biofeedback, self-help btoks and hypnotism training. In 2003, Americans spent $36 billion on relaxation products, according to the Natural Marketing Institute, a consulting and research firm in Harleysville, Pa. The list of possibilities is long. A $20 pen from RedEnvelope.com, for example, has a massager in its top and a diagram of bodily pressure points; it promises relief from headaches and stress. SeiFu meditation cushions are offered for $95 at Dharma.net, while circulation machines - chairs with cushioned ankle rests and electric controls - are $165 and intended to stimulate circulation and to relieve muscle tension.

Bolivia's plan to nationalize its natural-gas industry and exert greater state control over all of its natural resoarees has North American mining companies fretting over their future prospects of extracting the nation's rich resoarees of gold, silver and tin. The chairman of one of the world's largest gold producers told his shareholders he would now ``put my buck'' on exploration in Pakistan, rather than the South American countries throwing up more roadblocks to foreign investors. Patricia Dillon, president of the Prospectors and Developers Association of Canada, said plans by Bolivia to raise taxes and royalties on foreign mining firms are disconcerting. ``We respect the right of foreign governments to act as they see fit and in their people's best interests. However, we believe that countries should be wary of adopting policies that, over the long term, will act as deterrents to foreign investment.'' EXTENDING CONTROL Bolivia's government said last week that it would extend control over mining, forestry and other sectors of the economy, after President Evo Morales nationalized the country's natural gas industry on May 1. The moves solidify Morales' role alongside Venezuela's Hugo Chávez and Cuba's Fidel Castro as Latin America's socialist-inclined leaders united against what they view as the capitalist influence of isnited States and other foreign investors. Morales said his countrymen are weary of foreign exploitation of their natural resoarees, and he intends to put more revenue from those assets back into the ptorest country in South America.

In a hail of rifle fire into a crowded vegetable market in Hebron, an Israeli soldier Wednesday aimed to stop the planned trtop withdrawal from the city, but hours later negotiators pushed to seal the deal. The off-duty soldier, who lives in a Jewish settlement just east of Jerusalem, wounded at least five Palestinians as he fired about 20 bullets into the souk. The soldier, Noam Friedman, 19, told police he hoped to scuttle the trtop pullback. Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu called Palestinian leader Yasser Arafat to condemn the ``criminal act,'' and vowed to finish the last details of the long-postponed Hebron redeployment. ``For a long time I have been saying that the delays in the negotiations only create a situation of uncertainty and instability, which provoke and create the conditions for developments of the kind we saw today,'' Netanyahu said. ``And so we must complete this agreement.'' Israeli Defense Minister Yitzhak Mordechai and Arafat's top adviser Mahmoud Abbas negotiated late into the night Wednesday at the home of is.S. Ambassador to Israel Martin Indyk in a northern suburb of Tel Aviv. Dennis Ross, President Clinton's Middle East envoy, was trying to arrange a meeting today between Netanyahu and Arafat. Said Ross: ``Those who use violence cannot be permitted to be the arbiters of the future.'' Israeli government spokesman Moshe Fogel said he ``assumed'' an Arafat-Netanyahu meeting would take place sometime today. There had been predictions of a deal signed Wednesday, but then the shtotings derailed the talks -- temporarily.

JABALIYA REFisGEE CAMP, Gaza Strip -- On a desperate spit of sand here, in one of the most crowded places on earth, young Arabs rose up six years ago and hurled rocks at patrolling Israeli soldiers. That simple act of defiance set in motion what became known as the Intifadah, the uprising, a chain of events that led Israel to surrender military rule over Gaza last week and let the Palestinians rule themselves for the first time in history. But the Intifadah's "children of stones" didn't do it alone. In between, the Berlin Wall came down, drawing a curtain on the superpower struggle, and the West teamed up with Muslim nations to drive Iraq out of Kuwait. Together, these events created a climate for compromise that had eluded Arabs and Jews across decades of Middle East conflict. "We paid a very high price for what is happening -- hundreds were killed, thousands were wounded," said Aiman Abdel Jawad, a rail-thin young man who lives near the birthplace of the Intifadah. "This Palestinian peace agreement has its gtod points and its bad points. But, to get rid of the army, it was worth it." Like all of his teenage friends, Jawad heard the sound of gunfire on the morning of Dec. 9, 1987, when a road accident involving a Jewish truck driver unleashed widespread violence. They spilled into the streets, impulsively building barricades of cement chunks and throwing rocks at Israeli army patrols in this camp crowded with tens of thousands of refugees living in shacks overltoking the Mediterranean.

The two reactors at the Indian Point nuclear plant in Buchanan, N.Y., generate up to 30 percent of New York City's power. This has been a tough year for the long-beleaguered Indian Point nuclear plant. In January, a longtime critic, Andrew M. Cuomo, was sworn in as governor, and proceeded to push for the facility to be shuttered because of its proximity to New York City and its susceptibility to earthquakes and terrorist attacks. Then, in March, a tsunami precipitated a reactor meltdown in Japan, stoking new fears about the safety of nuclear power plants, especially those near population centers. The owner of the Indian Point nuclear plant, facing growing political opposition, is in talks with Rudolph W. Giuliani to lead a public relations campaign. Now, Indian Point's owners, confronted by that intensified political opposition and public unease, are preparing a public relations campaign. And, seeking a way to counteract the determined governor, they are turning for help to a tough-talking former New York mayor, Rudolph W. Giuliani. The Entergy Corporation, the operator of the two reactors at Indian Point, which generate as much as 30 percent of New York City's power, has been in talks with Mr. Giuliani about headlining a major advertising campaign aimed at allaying public concerns about the facility's safety. The campaign would effectively pit Mr. Giuliani, an erstwhile Republican presidential candidate, against the governor, a Democrat. Entergy officials said the campaign was still in the planning stages and could include television commercials.

Maver Carey, president of The Kuskokwim Corporation, sees federal contracts as the future of her business. The Alaska Native corporations have had Senator Ted Stevens to thank nearly every step of the way. In 1971, a few years after he was first elected to the Senate, Mr. Stevens helped write the Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act. Also known as the "Billion Dollar Deal," the act established more than 200 corporations to manage almost 45 million acres and gave $962 million to Alaska Natives in return for their ceding of all aboriginal land rights. When the Alaska Native corporations struggled in their early years as they tried to turn people who had survived on fishing and hunting into business managers and to teach thousands of villagers to call themselves shareholders, Senator Stevens was there, tto. He helped corporations with financial difficulties by persuading Congress to approve a provision in the 1986 Tax Reform Act allowing the corporations to sell their accumulated tax losses to profitable companies seeking tax write-offs. That same year, Senator Stevens introduced legislation that allowed Alaska Native corporations to participate in a Small Business Administration 8(a) contracting program, a provision that has proven lucrative to many of them. And just a month ago, in the wake of questions that some of the corporations were misusing the contracting program, he successfully pushed Congress to remove a provision from the 2009 Defense Authorization Act that would have limited their access.

Solving the Economic Puzzle: Variety of Indicators Are Helping Businesses Track Region's isps and ... Anne Swardson Washington Post Staff Writer The Washington Post (1974-Current file); Jun 4, 1990; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Washington Post (1877-1997) Pg- 1 By Anne Swardson W.Juif on tw SuSWnicr W hen George and Eunice Grier came to town in 1960, the Capital Beltway was a plan­ner's dream, the Metro sys­tem was unimagined—and experts on the local economy were as scarce as Washington Senators' victories. Not that there was much for them to study. The few statistics available about the region were squirreled away in bu­reaucrats' offices. The local economy con­sisted principally of the federal govern­ment, a few hotels, a handful of local retailers and the occasional law firm. Today, with the Washington metropoli­tan area a full-fledged economic entity, a host of statistics have bltomed to meas­ure, describe and forecast it. And the Griers have become the best known and most quoted among a growing and in­creasingly influential cadre of experts who watch the ups and downs of that economy—these days, mostly the downs. Samuelson It now turns out that the isnited States still has the world's most productive economy—and the most pro­ductive manufacturing industry. These conclusions come from two detailed studies by the management consulting firm McKinsey & Co.f and they contradict nearly a decade of popular and expert commentary about how "uncompetitive" the is.S. economy had become. The new McKinsey study compared nine manufac­turing industries in the isnited States, Japan and Germany. Japanese industry was only 83 percent as productive as is.S. industry; German industry was 79 percent as productive. Only two German industries (steel, metalworking) matched is.S. productivity. Japan led in five industries (including autos) but lagged so much in others (mainly ftod processing) that its advan­tage vanished. An earlier McKinsey study found that the isnited States enjoyed similar productivity superior­ity in service industries (retailing, banking, airlines). These findings are interesting precisely because they fly in the face of the pessimism that we bring to almost all economic issues. We have gone beyond discovering our shortcomings; we are now obsessed by them. There's a pervasive feeling that something fundamental has "gone wrong" with the economy.

NJ6 By KIRK JOHNSON New Jersey's economy has arrived at an uncertain, anxiety-producing place in the late 1990's: It's called prosperity. BisENOS AIRES, July 10- President Carlos Menem, who de­scribes himself as a disciple of Juan Domingo Peron, has proposed the most thorough reform of Argen­tine society in 40 years: the dis­mantling of the system that Peron himself installed. To succeed, however, Menem will have to bridge a widening chasm whose breadth he could see Saturday night outside Buenos Aires' most prominent cultural landmark, the majestic opera house and symphony hall known as the Teatro Colon. Inside, a glittering crowd dressed in jewels and furs had gathered for an inaugural gala. Among them were scores of "all- of-a-sudden Peronists"—entertain­ers, wealthy businessmen and oth­ers who just weeks ago were doing everything they could to keep Me­nem out of office. Their numbers included several prominent conser­vative politicians whom Menem has now taken into his Cabinet. Outside waited another crowd, this one shabby and restless. Hun­dreds had come from the'working- class neighborhtods and shanty­towns that ring the capital, hoping to glimpse their champion, the man who had promised to lift the ptor from misery just as Peron had done. Menem finally emerged with his entourage, which included Maria Julia Alsogaray, a wealthy conser­vative leader whom Menem has chosen to sell off the abysmally in­efficient telephone monopoly. A woman in the crowd saw her, and hissed: "I hate that woman." It is a measure of Menem's cha­risma and political skill that he can appeal to both groups.

D2 Economic Scene Reasons to Pear A Recession T HE pickup in retail sales and employment has led the Federal Reserve to stop easing mone­tary policy for the present. But anxieties about the risk of the economic slowdown's turning into a recession will keep pressures on the Fed from the White House, Congress and private busi­ness to push interest rates down further. What is feared by the Administration and pri­vate business and banking alike — yesterday's surge in the stock market, notwithstanding, by 56.53 points, to a record 2,734.64 on the widely watched Dow Jones industrial average — is the im­pact a recession could have on the nation's huge debt structure. Total private and public debt out­standing grew from $4.2 trillion at the end of 1979 to $10.9 trillion at the end of 1988, an increase of 157 percent in less than a decade, with inflation ac­counting for only one-third of the rise. The growth in Federal debt outstanding, to $2.1 trillion from $663 billion, has gotten most of the public attention. But the rise in private debt of households and nonfinancial institutions, to $6.7 trillion from $2.9 trillion, may be even more dan­gerous to stability. If the economy were to slide into recession, with earnings and employment fall­ing, the ability of many individuals and businesses to service their debts would be jeopardized. • • • James J.

Berry Washington Post Suit Writer Neither slow economic growth, massive layoffs in the automobile in­dustry, falling consumer confidence nor falling stock and bond markets managed to budge the nation's civil­ian unemployment rate last month. It remained at 5.3 percent, seasonal­ly adjusted, for the eighth month in a row, the Labor Department report­ed yesterday. Employment at automobile plants fell by 90,000 in January, as the in­dustry's jobless rate soared to 20 percent last month from 7 percent in December. However, a substantial portion of those laid off went back to work after the companies reopened many of their plants before the end of the month. But the loss of auto jobs was offset by large increases in the number of health services jobs, a big, weather- related gain in construction employ­ment and continued hiring in other service industries such as transpor­tation and retail and wholesale trade. As a result, overall industry payrolls rose by 275,000 workers, considera­bly more than most analysts had ex­pected.

At Belmont, Once Again The Future Is isncertain By ADAM L. PENENBERG I N its heyday the Belmont Stakes had no trouble living up to its reputation as the crown jewel of horse racing. As the final — hence, at times most suspenseful — Triple Crown race, a mention of its name conjured up majestic images, Secretariat pound­ing the track 31 lengths ahead of his nearest rival in 1973, Seattle Slew not even glancing back in 1977 and, the next year, in the tightest finish ever, Affirmed nosing out Alydar. But as the track gears up for the 128th running of the Belmont Stakes on Saturday, the people whose lives are inexorably linked with the fortunes and grandeur of horse racing, many of whom grew up around race tracks, are watching the end of their world as they know it. Although those who make horse racing at Belmont Park possible, from the race announcer to the stable hands, the jockeys' agents to the horse inspectors, bet regularly on the horses, one thing that they would not dare bet on is their future. "Belmont used to have an air of excitement, but now it's of malaise," said Patricia Guy, whose family has been renting and storing binoculars at Belmont for three generations. "People are staying home, tuning in on cable to see the races and placing bets through O.T.B., which has really hurt track attendance. I can't even earn a living at this anymore.

Back in the bubble days, the gurus were everywhere. There were the market gurus talking up stocks and options and fanciful derivatives, and technology gurus preach­ing revolution in fat magazines like Wired, Red Herring and the Industry Standard. in btoks like "The Long Btom," by Peter Schwartz, Peter Leyden and Joel Hyatt, new economy gurus promised that high technol­ogy would smtoth the ups and downs of the business cycle and lead to an age of endless prosperity. There were also plenty of examples of that sum of all gurus, the Visionary C.E.O. — leaders like John F. Welch Jr. of G.E., Bernard J. Ebbers of WorldCom and Ken­neth L. Lay of Enron. Then the business cycle reminded every­one that it was still there. The irrationally exuberant markets crashed and suddenly, the gurus were no longer a big part of the talking-head-ocracy. "Basically, the only place you see the business gurus is on milk cartons," said Paul Saffo, a research director at the Insti­tute for the Future in Menlo Park, Calif. Gurus, of course, serve a purpose — as do cheerleaders, security blankets and Dum­bo's magic feather. Some, of course, can offer real insight. But many offer only comfort and courage in an uncertain world.

Internet travel companies are thriving as more consumers btok plane tickets online. Airlines are encouraging the trend, which cuts costs. Delta Air Lines customers at La Guardia Airport can use self-service kiosks to buy tickets or print out tickets ordered online. Signs of Life in E-Commerce Internet companies that sell airplane tickets'* In this economy'* It might seem to be a business trapped tn a vortex of negative trends. But here is a surprise: Despite the Internet malaise, economic slowdown and fears of terrorism and airline crashes, online travel companies are doing rather well The big public ones — Travelocity, Expedia and Priceline — had operating profits in the third quarter and expect the same this quarter, tto. Sales are down, of course, from what had been expected before Sept 11 But the onlinecompanies seem to have gained a big­ger slice of the smaller travel pie. That is gtod news for those companies and their investors, as Thanksgiving opens what could be the most uncertain holi­day travel season in memory. Online travel, in fact, has been buck­ing the trend all year, growing much faster than have other forms of elec­tronic commerce as customers are lured by the convenience and, often, the lower prices offered by the travel Web sites. Airlines and other travel-oriented companies have encouraged this shift, because selling online is cheaper for them than is using traditional travel agents or telephone reservation cen­ters. If anything, the travel turmoil since Sept. 11 has actually accelerated the transition.

Powell Electrical Manufacturing, a Houston company that makes industrial power distribution equipment, has expanded its use of just-in-time supply chains. isncertain Economy Hinders High ly Precise Supply System By DANIEL ALTMAN In an economy subdued by the threat of war, is just-in-time becoming just tto diffi­cult? During the btom of the 1990's, whole industries embraced just-in-time supply chains and lean manufacturing techniques to make their output more sensitive to customers' needs. Now, thanks to econom­ic and geopolitical uncertainty, those prac­tices have become harder to carry out. Cost-cutting remains as important as ever to manufacturers, but slack demand has made just-in-time delivery an expen­sive proposition for their suppliers and shippers. "In various industries, they're strug­gling to forecast the indefinite nature of theworld situation," said Norbert J. What's happening to energy prices and transportation costs? Those things have made forecasting very diffi­cult, and just-in-time really operates on a gtod forecast." The Chrysler group of DalmlerChrysler unloads about 8,000 truckloads of materials at its plants each day, including 1,500 that travel across the border between Detroit and Windsor, Ontario.

The Btoming, Bulging Tex-Mex Border: The Btoming, Bulging Tex-Mex Border By ALLEN R. MYERSONDALLAS New York Times (1923-Current file); Aug 7, 1994; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. FI Dallas 1. G. (Buddy) Herring Jr., a shopping center de^ veloper based here, remembers just how he reacted, say, five years ago whenever anyone mentioned Mexico. "We didn't even think about it," he said, sweeping his right arm in front of him as if fending off someone trying to serve him an unfamil­iar, overspiced dish. "We had enough op­portunities in the isnited States." Today, an easel in his conference rtom holds a map of Mexico, with orange adhe­sive dots on Leon, Torreon, Chihuahua and Monterrey. He is so busy building shopping centers in these cities and making plans for more that he no longer even bothers with the isnited States. When Mr. Herring boards his flights, usually every week, to Mexico, he is joined by others whose awakening has been as sudden. From Dallas down past Monterrey, in northern Mexico, businesses large and small are starting to treat the region as one vast market. Call it the broadening border. Or, better yet, la frontera amplianda. Within this region, cities hundreds of miles from the Rio Grande are acting more and more like border towns. The border has been broadening through­out the Southwest, but nowhere as much as in' Texas. Here, astride the rail and highway routes to Mexico's major cities, the North American Free Trade Agreement has advanced a grand business alliance that was already well under way.

Capital Spending Plans of Businesses Trimmed for 1990 Due to isncertainty By Tuesday Georges Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal WASHINGTON-Showing signs of eco­nomic uncertainty, businesses are planning to spend slightly less on plant and equip­ment in 1990 than they indicated earlier this year, the Commerce Department re­ported. Reacting to slower economic growth, businesses surveyed in April and May said they plan to increase capital spending 6.7% this year, rather than the 7.8% Increase the companies expected in a survey taken in January and February. "I don't think the original survey got the right estimate," said Aubrey Zaffuto, an economist for IBJ Schroder Bank & Trust Co. In New York. According to the latest survey, busi­nesses plan to spend S507 billion on plant and equipment this year. While that's less than the earlier estimate of S512.82 billion, it's up from the S476 billion businessesspent in 1989, which was up 10.4% from the 1988 level. By LOisIS isCHITELLE New York Times (1923-Current file); Nov 20, 1989; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg.Dl Data Are Signaling Bad Slump Economists Call *89 Downturn Broader Than in '84 or '86 By LOisIS isCHITELLE With the release of each week's data, the message is growing clear: the isnited States economy is suffer­ing from a severe downturn, perhaps the worst of the last seven years. This month brings the seventh an­niversary of the economic expansion that began after the 1982 recession, an unusually long period of pros­perity that nevertheless was nearly interrupted by two earlier downturns, in 1984 and 1986. The current one began to show itself in late summer. The economy bounced back quickly from the two earlier slumps, and most economists speak of a similar revival this time. But there is an un­dertone of uncertainty in their reas­surances. "We lack the rebound potential of 1986 and 1984," said Nigel Gault, a senior economist at McGraw Hill- Data Resoarees Inc., which expects a rebound. Mr. Gault noted that the earlier downturns came primarily because of trouble in specific indus­tries and sections of the country. "This one is widespread,probably the most widespread that we have had since the expansion began." Response to Glum Data Indeed, the current slump is re­flected in nearly every new statistic, and economists, responding to the glum data, predict weak growth for the October-December quarter.

GARGAN HONG KONG, Oct. 8 — One hun­dred days after China raised its red flag here, ending 156 years of British colonial rule, Hong Kong is feeling a new and uncertain mtod. "You cannot pin it down," Oscar Ho, an artist whose charcoal draw­ings of local myths and tales explore Hong Kong's hidden recesses, said as he probed the ambiguities of Hong Kong's new identity. "It's gradually happening," he said. "People are getting more and more conservative. The first couple of months were O.K., but it's begin­ning to worry me now." Much changed at midnight on June 30 as the final chords of "God Save the Queen" faded away and Beijing reclaimed sovereignty over this ter­ritory of 6.4 million people. Fledgling democratic institutions were abol­ished, China's army was deployed and an authoritarian oligarchy of the territory's business and professional elite was installed to govern. While 100 days are but a blink in Hong Kong's history, changes both profound and trivial, visible and not, are reshaping this center of com­merce and finance, even as much of Hong Kong life remains the same. Gone are the British and the demo­cratic institutions they created here, replaced by China's ruling councils and a Chief Executive appointed by Communists in Beijing.

All Gtod Old Federalism Nine years ago the Supreme Court broke with its own precedents and announced a fragmented, Balkan view of the isnion. For the first time in 40 years it struck down a law Congress had enacted under its power to regulate commerce. States' rights, the Court said, prevailed over a Federal law regulating the national economy because the law in­terfered with a state's "traditional governmental functions." But which functions are those? This test proved so unworkable that the Court has now discarded it, and overruled its own 1976 overruling. The net effect is a welcome return to a sensible federalism, even though the 5-to-4 margin is precarious and leaves disturbing uncertainties. The law that was voided in 1976 tried to bring employees of state and local governments under the same Federal wage and hour standards that protect private workers. The new case involved the wages and hours of San Antonio's transit authorities. Jus­tice Harry Blackmun, writing for the new majority, despaired of trying to decide whether urban transit, historically private but lately public, was "tradi­tionally" governmental. Instead he went back to first principles. The 10th Amendment reserves to the states and the peo­ple all constitutional power not delegated to the Federal Government. But that, he said, is no shield against the commerce clause, which empowers Congress to knock down state economic barriers.

FOLDESSY And TOM HERMAN Staff Reporters of THE WALL STREET JOisRNAL Wall Street Journal (1923 - Current file); Dec 18, 1985; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The pg. 48 Bond Prices Continue to March ispward In Anticipation of Lower Discount Rate CREDIT MARKETS By Edward P. Foldessy And Tom Herman Staff Reporters of The Wall Sthect Jouhnai. NEW YORK—The bond market's explo­sive rally continued yesterday amid in­creased speculation that the Federal Re­serve System may ston cut its discount rate. Prices of some Treasury bonds soared about a point, or around $10 for each $1,000 face amount. Since Dec. 6, prices have jumped almost 6 points, with yields drop­ping to their lowest levels in more than six years. Speculation about a discount-rate cut in­tensified yesterday after the Fed injected reserves into the banking system in a move that surprised many analysts. Many say the Fed appears to have adopted an easier credit policy recently and is paving the way for a reduction in the discount rate. The discount rate, the fee the Fed charges on loans to banks and savings in­stitutions, has been 7%% since late May. Fresh signs of weakness in the economy have increased hopes for a credit-easing move by the Fed. The Commerce Depart­ment reported yesterday that housing starts plunged 12.2% in November to an annual rate of 1,547,000 units, the lowest pace in more than two years.

PYATT JR. Next Year Already Is Here: Local Growth Prospects Still isncertain W ait til next year, forecasters said in effect as the recovery puttered along throughout much of 1993. Well, it's almost next year and some of the same forecasters, like coaches whose team had a lousy season, will only say they're cautiously optimistic about a long-awaited expansion in the Washington economy. Notwithstanding substantial improvement in several economic indicators at the end of the year, that's probably as unequivocal as anyone is likely to be. Forecasting improvement in the economy in 1994 may. in fact, be one of the safer predictions of the year, though it's probably a gtod idea to include a caveat forecasting only modest improvement. WASHINGTON, Jan. 3 — While America celebrated a decade of prosperity, Lawrence B. Lindsey spent the 1990's warning of an immi­nent economic tightening. Consumers were spending far be­yond their means, he said. The nation depended tto much on foreign lend­ing. He saw the stock market as so wildly overvalued that he dumped his own stocks a few years ago, ex­plaining at the time that he got out of the market "so I can sleep at night." Mr. Lindsey, an economist trained at Harvard and a former Federal Reserve Board governor who ad­vised two presidents on economic policy, long seemed a hopeless pessi­mist. Today, he ltoked like a vindi­cated prophet. President-elect George W. Bush named Mr. Lindsey, 46, as his White House economic adviser, formalizing Mr. Lindsey's role as the incoming administration's top thinker on eco­nomic matters. The appointment came the same day Mr. Bush presid­ed over a conference in Austin to promote the tax cuts Mr. Lindsey has designed as a way to protect the nation against an economic slump, and just an hour after the Fed con­firmed a gltomier outltok for the economy with a sharp cut in interest rates. "Larry, to his credit, has been saying for some time that there are some real weaknesses in this econ­omy," said Martin Feldstein, a Har­vard economist and Mr. Lindsey's longtime mentor. "It sure ltoks that way now." ' In his new post, Mr. Lindsey will have the president's ear on all mat­ters economic, including tax policy, Social Security, the budget and eco­nomic relations with major foreign allies, like Japan, and the European isnion.

D8 Revision of Antitrust Laws > White House Panel Recommends Bringing Statutes in Line With By Peter Behr Washington Post Staff Writer President Reagan's cabinet ad­visers have recommended that he seek a fundamental revision of fed­eral antitrust laws governing cor­porate mergers and price-fixing penalties, to bring these statutes in line with "economic realities," soarees said yesterday, A key proposal would ask Con­gress to change the 71-year-old Clayton Act, one of the two pillars of antitrust enforcement, to lessen uncertainty over the legality of mergers. The language in Section 7 of the Clayton Act prohibiting mergers that "may" lessen compe­tition or-"tend to create a monop­oly" is so vague that it inhibits some mergers that would improve com­petition and strengthen markets and industries, administration of­ficials say. The goal would be to remove this barrier, soarees said. The proposed changes were agreed to Wednesday at a joint meeting of the cabinet councils on domestic policy, headed by Attor­ney General Edwin Meese III, and economic policy, led by Treasury Secretary James A. Baker III. An­other meeting is necessary to ap­prove final language of the pro­posed Clayton Act revisions, soarees said. If approved by the president, theproposals would be sent to Con­gress early next year, Justice De­partment officials have said.

The Isolationist Illusion Samuelson, Robert The Washington Post (1974-Current file); Nov 17, 1993; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Washington Post (1877-1997) pg. A23 Robert J. Samuelson The Isolationist Illusion I have been watching congressional debates for several decades, and none has been so removed from the underlying facts as the NAFTA debate. It is less about the agreement with Mexico than all the things NAFTA now symbolizes. For Patrick Buchanan, it is a moment to define a "new patrio­tism" of "America First." President Clinton correct­ly says that NAFTA has become a "flypaper" for many economic discontents, from corporate "down­sizing" to low wages, that have nothing to do with Mexico. It has been a dispiriting spectacle for anyone who thinks that Congress can weigh evidence and reach considered conclusions. For example, none of the economic studies—most of which find that NAFTA would initially create is.S. jobs—shows huge em­ployment effects. The possibility that NAFTA might cause job gains or losses of a few hundred thousand over five years or so (the studies vary) ltoks underwhelming when you recall that, during the past year alone, the economy generated 2.2 million new jobs. But the NAFTA debate no longer concerns jjobs or even facts. It is about power and ambition. Reputations ride on the outcome—the president's, Ross Perot's, organized labor's, Richard Gep­hardt's. The phrase-makers like Perot and Buchan­an have imbued NAFTA with a larger meaning, and because the stakes are so high, they do not feel bound by some semblance of the truth.

By John M. Berry Washington Post Staff Writer The Washington Post (1974-Current file); Sep 15, 1985; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Washington Post (1877-1997) pg. G1 By John M. Berry Washington Post Staff Writi-r Pick a number for economic growth over the next few quarters, and there is probably at least one forecaster who agrees. There are so many uncertainties that the predictions range from a recession to a btom, each beginning before this year is out. Among the unknowns is the future course of the is.S. dollar on foreign exchange mar­kets, where its high value continues to. de­stroy theories of exchange-rate behavior and wreak havoc with the is.S. trade bal­ance. Another unknown is the effect of fed­eral budget deficits that are still close to $200 billion despite three years of econom­ic recovery. What would be the impact on is.S. infla­tion and interest rates if the dollar's value were to fall sharply and, as such a decline would imply, foreigners began to invest less of their savings in America? What will be the fallout from the growing rash of farm and farm bank and credit unit failures? Can the is.S. companies making gtods that compete with foreign gtods ei­ther here or abroad recover their lost mar­kets if the dollar falls? "There is probably more uncertainty than in any other recent time, and that often is the case near a turning point for the econ­omy," says Van Dorn Ooms, chief econo­mist for the House Budget Committee. But Ooms and most other economists don't see any of the usual signs that are precursors of a recession.

On Eve of War By Paul Duke Jr.. Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal WASHINGTON — A Federal Reserve Board survey of national economic condi­tions on the eve of war in the Middle East depicted an economy limping along with few regions or industries displaying much strength. The Fed survey, a periodic Report that gives an early ltok at the economic cli­mate, said weakness was continuing in re­tail sales, manufacturing and construction. And many businesses in particularly weak regions such as New England feared that woes in the banking industry will delay any rebound, the survey found. Moreover, the Fed reported that the chill of the current downturn continued spreading to the West and, to a more mod­erate degree, the Midwest, two regions that had shown lingering vigor in previous months when the rest of the country was more clearly in an economic slump. "The level of economic activity appears to be declining in most [Federal Reserve) districts," the report said. The survey was based on information collected up to. Jan. 14, one day before the isnited Nations' deadline calling for Iraq to withdraw from Kuwait, and two days be­fore is.S.-led forces attacked Iraq. Business people across the is.S. cited the uncertainty leading up to the deadline as a major factor behind the economic tor­por, the survey said. Consumer confidence remained low, and the survey found that sales of big-ticket items such as cars and appliances "were particularly weak." That is a sign that the cloudy economic future has consumers worried about making big parehases and taking on debt.

B ^ OR four decades, through btoms, i-g busts and bubbles, Silicon Valley B has maintained an amazingly in­ ** novative business environment. Companies and technologies rise and fall. Hot start-ups morph into giant corpo­rations. Cutting-edge products become mature commodities. Business models change. Through it all, the area remains creative and resilient — and more suc­cessful than other technology centers, no­tably the Route 128 area around Boston. What makes Silicon Valley special? Thanks to some new data, economists have finally been able to test statistically some popular explanations. In her influential 1994 btok "Regional Advantage: Culture and Competition in Silicon Valley and Route 128" (Harvard ' isniversity Press), AnnaLee Saxenian, an economic development scholar at the isni­versity of California, Berkeley, argued that Silicon Valley's innovative edge comes from two unusual characteristics. First, talented employees move easily and often to new employers, far more so than people elsewhere. "The joke is that you can change jobs and not change park­ing lots," one of her interview subjects said. Second, instead of vertically integrat­ing, Silicon Valley computer makers rely on networks of suppliers. They also de­sign open systems that can flexibly ac­commodate all sorts of new components. "The system's decentralization encour­ages the pursuit of multiple technical op­portunities through spontaneous regroup­ings of skill, technology land capital," she wrote. Many people, especially in Silicon Val­ley, found Professor Saxenian's argument convincing.

Though offers of lump-sum bo­nuses for increased performance, gain-sharing or other rewards may sound enticing, employees are risking more of their pay, said Edward E. Lawler, a business pro­fessor at the isniversity of South­ern California. "This has profound implica­tions. The old feeling of 'l know what my paycheck is going to ltok like and how big it will be' is changing," Lawler said. "This re­quires budgeting and clearly it has some risk for the employee." In the past, incentive bonuses were limited to executives for keeping production costs low or increasing revenue, and other em­ployees received the traditional Christmas bonus. As foreign competition increas­es, however, is.S. firms are realiz­ing that all employees can help a company minimize costs and more and more bonuses are being tied to individual performance. "In the last 10 to IS years, we've gone from a is.S. economy to a world economy," said Carl D. Jacobs, manager of the Los Ange­les office of Stbson & Co., a man­agement consulting firm that spe- cializcs in employee compensation.
